Mathematically, $A = -\dfrac{dN}{dt} = \lambda N$, where λ is the decay constant and N is the number of radioactive atoms present at that instant.

**SI Unit:** **Becquerel (Bq)**, where 1 Bq = 1 disintegration per second.

**Relationship with Curie:**

$$1 \text{ Curie (Ci)} = 3.7 \times 10^{10} \text{ Bq}$$

The Curie is the conventional (non-SI) unit originally defined as the activity of 1 gram of pure Radium-226.

*(Word count: ~80; KTU ideal: 60–100 words for a 3-mark definition question.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Distinguish between Half-Life (T½) and Mean Life (τ) of a radioactive isotope.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Half-Life (T½) | Mean Life (τ) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | Time for activity (or number of atoms) to reduce to **half** its initial value. | **Average lifetime** of a single radioactive atom before it decays. |
| Formula | $T_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693}{\lambda}$ | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ |
| Numerical relation | $T_{1/2} = 0.693 \, \tau$ | $\tau = 1.443 \, T_{1/2}$ |
| Typical use | Convenient for engineering and safety calculations. | Used in theoretical nuclear physics. |

Both are *material properties* and are independent of the amount of radioactive material present.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the radioactive decay law $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ from first principles. Define each term and state the SI unit of activity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A radioactive source has an initial activity of **8000 Bq**. After **10 years**, its activity drops to **500 Bq**. Calculate:
   (i) the **decay constant** λ,
   (ii) the **half-life** T½ of the source,
   (iii) the **activity after 25 years** from the start. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

**(a) Derivation of the Decay Law:**

1. Consider a sample containing N(t) radioactive atoms at time t.
2. The probability of any single atom decaying per unit time is the **decay constant λ** (unit: s⁻¹).
3. In an infinitesimal time dt, the expected number of decays is:

   $$dN = -\lambda \cdot N(t) \cdot dt$$

4. Rearranging and integrating:

   $$\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

5. Solving:

   $$\ln \left( \frac{N}{N_0} \right) = -\lambda t \implies N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$$

6. Since **Activity A = λN**, and N₀ corresponds to A₀ = λN₀:

   $$\boxed{A(t) = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}}$$

7. **SI Unit of Activity:** Becquerel (Bq) = 1 disintegration per second.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** A₀ = 8000 Bq, A(10 yr) = 500 Bq.

**(i) Decay Constant λ:**

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{t} \ln \left( \frac{A_0}{A(t)} \right) = \frac{1}{10} \ln \left( \frac{8000}{500} \right) = \frac{1}{10} \ln(16)$$

$$\lambda = \frac{2.7726}{10} = 0.2773 \text{ yr}^{-1}$$

**(ii) Half-Life T½:**

$$T_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda} = \frac{0.693}{0.2773} = 2.5 \text{ years}$$

*(Sanity check: 10 years = 4 half-lives → activity falls to (1/2)⁴ = 1/16 of 8000 = 500 Bq ✓)*

**(iii) Activity after 25 years:**

$$A(25) = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda \cdot 25} = 8000 \times e^{-0.2773 \times 25} = 8000 \times e^{-6.9325}$$

$$A(25) = 8000 \times 9.77 \times 10^{-4} = 7.82 \text{ Bq}$$

**Verification using half-life formula:**

$$A(25) = A_0 \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^{25/2.5} = 8000 \times \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^{10} = 8000 \times \frac{1}{1024} = 7.81 \text{ Bq} \checkmark$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the units of radioactivity (Becquerel and Curie) in detail. Why was the Curie originally defined the way it was? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A laboratory uses a **5 μCi source of Phosphorus-32 (³²P)** for tracer studies. The half-life of ³²P is **14.3 days**. Calculate:
   (i) the **activity in Becquerel**,
   (ii) the **number of radioactive atoms** in the source,
   (iii) the **activity after 30 days**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

**(a) Units of Radioactivity:**

**Becquerel (Bq) — SI Unit:** Defined as the activity of a quantity of radioactive material in which **one nucleus decays per second** (1 Bq = 1 s⁻¹). Named after **Henri Becquerel**, the discoverer of radioactivity (1896).

**Curie (Ci) — Conventional Unit:** Originally defined as the activity of **1 gram of pure Radium-226 (²²⁶Ra)**, which was experimentally found to undergo $3.7 \times 10^{10}$ disintegrations per second. The value was later adopted as the standard definition.

**Conversion:**

$$1 \text{ Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10} \text{ Bq}, \quad 1 \text{ Bq} = 2.703 \times 10^{-11} \text{ Ci}$$

**Historical Reason for the Original Definition:** In 1910, when Marie Curie established the unit, Radium-226 was the most precisely measurable radioactive standard. Defining 1 Ci in terms of 1 gram of Ra-226 allowed experimenters worldwide to reproduce a standard source by simply weighing out 1 gram of radium, ensuring *uniformity of measurement* across laboratories.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution for ³²P Source:**

**Given:** A₀ = 5 μCi, T½ = 14.3 days.

**(i) Activity in Becquerel:**

$$A_0 = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ Ci} \times 3.7 \times 10^{10} \text{ Bq/Ci} = 1.85 \times 10^{5} \text{ Bq}$$

$$A_0 = 185{,}000 \text{ Bq} = 185 \text{ kBq}$$

**(ii) Number of Radioactive Atoms:**

First convert half-life to seconds:

$$T_{1/2} = 14.3 \times 24 \times 3600 = 1{,}235{,}520 \text{ s}$$

Decay constant:

$$\lambda = \frac{0.693}{T_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{1.23552 \times 10^{6}} = 5.61 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

Number of atoms:

$$N = \frac{A_0}{\lambda} = \frac{1.85 \times 10^{5}}{5.61 \times 10^{-7}} = 3.30 \times 10^{11} \text{ atoms}$$

**(iii) Activity after 30 days:**

$$A(30) = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$$

Here $\lambda t = (5.61 \times 10^{-7}) \times (30 \times 24 \times 3600) = 1.454$

$$A(30) = 1.85 \times 10^{5} \times e^{-1.454} = 1.85 \times 10^{5} \times 0.2336 = 4.32 \times 10^{4} \text{ Bq}$$

**Result:** $A(30) \approx 43.2 \text{ kBq} \approx 1.17 \ \mu\text{Ci}$.

**Verification using the half-life shortcut:** 30 days = (30 / 14.3) half-lives = 2.098 T½.

$$A(30) = 5 \ \mu\text{Ci} \times (1/2)^{2.098} = 5 \times 0.2333 = 1.167 \ \mu\text{Ci} \checkmark$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Failing to convert μCi → Bq (or years → seconds) before applying λ. Half mark is deducted for *every wrong unit*.
> 2. **Confusing λ and T½:** Writing λ = 0.693 × T½ instead of λ = 0.693 / T½. This is the **most common error** and costs **2–3 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting Negative Sign in dN/dt:** Writing dN/dt = +λN instead of −λN. The negative sign reflects physical reality (atoms are *lost*).
> 4. **Mixing Up Specific Activity with Total Activity:** Specific activity has units of Bq/kg or Bq/g — do not confuse with total Bq.
> 5. **Skipping the Final Unit:** Always end every numerical answer with the correct unit. A correct number *without units* loses **1 mark** in KTU valuation.
> 6. **Half-Life Shortcut Pitfall:** The formula $A = A_0 / 2^n$ works only when t is an *integer multiple* of T½. For non-integer multiples, use $A = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$ or break t into integer part + fraction of T½.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Activity Rate**

- **Definition:** Activity = number of radioactive decays per unit time. **SI Unit = Becquerel (Bq = 1 s⁻¹).** Old unit = Curie (1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq).
- **Core Equation:** $A = \lambda N = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$. This is the **most tested formula** in the entire module.
- **Decay Constant λ** is the *probability* of decay per atom per second. It is a **constant for a given isotope**, independent of sample size.
- **Half-Life T½ = 0.693 / λ** and **Mean Life τ = 1 / λ = 1.443 T½**.
- **Activity is proportional to the number of atoms present** — more mass = more atoms = higher activity.
- **Activity is NOT a conserved quantity** — it always decreases with time, never increases (for a single sample).
- **Decay Law (Number form):** $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ and **Decay Law (Activity form):** $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ are mathematically identical in shape.
- **Shortcut for integer half-lives:** $A(n) = A_0 / 2^n$ after n half-lives.
- **Number of atoms in a source:** $N = A \cdot T_{1/2} / 0.693$ (when T½ is in the same time unit as A).
- **Industrial relevance:** Activity rate governs the design of shielding, calculation of safe handling time, and selection of detection instruments in level, thickness, density, and radiography applications.
- **Always quote final answers with units** — KTU examiners award full marks *only* with proper units in the answer.
- **Remember the historical value:** 1 Ci was originally defined using 1 gram of Radium-226 — frequently asked in viva questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inventory Analysis
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT743 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inventory Analysis

# 📦 Inventory Analysis — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Inventory** is the stockpile of raw materials, work-in-process (WIP), finished goods, and MRO (Maintenance, Repair, and Operating) supplies held by an organization to satisfy present and future demand. **Inventory Analysis** is the systematic, quantitative, and qualitative study of inventory stock levels, costs, demand patterns, ordering policies, and classification methodologies used to **minimize total logistics costs while guaranteeing a target service level**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Industrial Safety & Instrumentation, Inventory Analysis is positioned as a *decision-support tool* that links:
- **Process Safety** (avoiding stock-out hazards in critical spares for safety systems like SIS, ESD valves)
- **Production Continuity** (preventing line stoppages)
- **Capital Efficiency** (reducing tied-up working capital)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Terminology (Locked):** *Inventory* must be understood as a **buffer against uncertainty** — uncertainty in demand, uncertainty in lead time, and uncertainty in supply. Every formula you write in the exam must reduce one of these three uncertainties.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Kitchen Pantry" Model

Imagine your home kitchen as a factory:
- **Groceries in the fridge** → Raw materials inventory
- **Dough kept aside for tomorrow's bread** → Work-In-Process (WIP)
- **Pre-cooked meals in the freezer** → Finished Goods inventory
- **Gas-cylinder refills stored** → Safety stock (the "what if the delivery guy is late?" reserve)

If you buy too little, you go hungry on busy days (a **stock-out**). If you buy too much, the vegetables rot and your money is locked inside the fridge (a **holding cost**). **Inventory Analysis is the mathematical recipe to buy *just enough* to never go hungry, yet never waste.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industry Metrics (Memorize):**
> - **D** = Annual Demand (units/year)
> - **Q** = Order Quantity (units/order)
> - **S** = Ordering Cost per order (₹/order) → typically **₹200 – ₹2000**
> - **H** = Holding Cost per unit per year (₹/unit/year) → typically **20–30% of unit cost**
> - **L** = Lead Time (days)
> - **d** = Daily demand (units/day) = D/working days
> - **z** = Service level z-score (e.g., 1.65 for 95%, 2.33 for 99%)

### 1.3 Why Inventory Exists — The 7 Functional Reasons

| # | Function | Industrial Safety Link |
|---|----------|-----------------------|
| 1 | **Decoupling** operations | Buffer between reactors to prevent pressure surges |
| 2 | **Meeting demand uncertainty** | Spare relief valves for shutdown systems |
| 3 | **Buffering supply uncertainty** | Lead-time slack for imported DCS spares |
| 4 | **Economies of scale** | Bulk purchase of PPE kits |
| 5 | **Pipeline / Transit stock** | Spares on offshore platforms |
| 6 | **Hedge against price changes** | Locking Lube oil price before monsoon |
| 7 | **Smoothing production** | Avoiding restart costs in distillation columns |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🧠 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cost Architecture of Inventory

There are **exactly four cost families** the examiner can ask you to compute. If you forget one, your answer is incomplete.

### A. Ordering Cost (Setup Cost) — $C_O$

Costs incurred *every time* you place a purchase order or change over a machine.

$$C_O = \text{Requisition cost} + \text{Purchase order cost} + \text{Receiving cost} + \text{Inspection cost} + \text{Setup cost}$$

**Annual Ordering Cost** $= \dfrac{D}{Q} \times S$

- Behavior: **Decreases** as Q increases (fewer orders per year).

### B. Holding Cost (Carrying Cost) — $C_H$

Costs incurred to *physically keep* one unit in the store for one year.

$$C_H = \text{Capital cost} + \text{Storage cost} + \text{Obsolescence} + \text{Insurance} + \text{Security} + \text{Shrinkage}$$

**Annual Holding Cost** $= \dfrac{Q}{2} \times H$

- Behavior: **Increases** as Q increases (more average stock sitting in the warehouse).

> [!TIP]
> The **$Q/2$** term assumes **instantaneous replenishment** — i.e., stock drops from $Q$ to $0$ linearly as demand consumes it, then jumps back to $Q$. The average inventory is therefore $(Q+0)/2 = Q/2$.

### C. Shortage / Stock-out Cost — $C_S$

Lost profit, lost customer goodwill, **line stoppage penalties**, and in industrial-safety contexts, **catastrophic failure cost** if a critical safety spare is missing.

### D. Purchase Cost

$= D \times C$ where $C$ is unit price. Constant — does **not** depend on Q, so often ignored in EOQ optimization.

---

## 2.2 The Total Cost Function — The Heart of EOQ

$$TC(Q) = \underbrace{\frac{D}{Q} S}_{\text{Ordering}} + \underbrace{\frac{Q}{2} H}_{\text{Holding}}$$

**The optimum is where Ordering Cost = Holding Cost.** This is the **Golden Rule of EOQ** — examiners love to ask you to prove this.

$$\frac{dTC}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$

This is **Wilson's Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** — the single most-tested formula in KTU inventory papers.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Print this table mentally — 70% of every numerical question in this module is solved using these eight lines.

| # | Concept | Formula | Unit | When to Use |
|---|---------|---------|------|-------------|
| 1 | **EOQ (Wilson)** | $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ | units | Constant demand, no shortages |
| 2 | Total Inventory Cost | $TC = DS/Q + QH/2$ | ₹/year | Annual cost comparison |
| 3 | Number of orders/year | $N = D/Q$ | orders/yr | Reorder frequency |
| 4 | Cycle Time (TBO) | $T = Q/D$ years | time | Time Between Orders |
| 5 | **Reorder Point** | $ROP = d \times L + SS$ | units | "When to order" trigger |
| 6 | **Safety Stock** | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$ | units | Demand or LT variability |
| 7 | Lead-Time Std Dev | $\sigma_{LT} = \sqrt{L} \cdot \sigma_d$ | units | When daily demand varies |
| 8 | **EPQ (Production)** | $Q_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H(1-d/p)}}$ | units | Order built up gradually |
| 9 | EOQ w/ Backorders | $Q_b = \sqrt{2DS/H} \cdot \sqrt{(p+1)/p}$ | units | Stockouts permitted |
| 10 | Max Backorder | $B^* = Q_b / (p+1)$ | units | Peak backorder level |
| 11 | Inv. Turnover Ratio | $ITR = COGS / \overline{Inv}$ | times/yr | Working capital efficiency |
| 12 | Average Inventory | $\overline{Inv} = Q/2$ | units | Standard EOQ model |
| 13 | Carrying % of Unit Cost | $H = i \cdot C$ | ₹/unit/yr | When only $i$ & $C$ given |
| 14 | $H$ from $r$ & $C$ | $H = r \cdot C$ | ₹/unit/yr | $r$ = carrying rate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Lock:** In the KTU paper, $S$ is ordering cost (NOT setup cost alone — it's the *combined* ordering + setup cost per cycle), and $H$ is the *per-unit-per-year* holding cost. Do not interchange them.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Industrial Safety

- **SIS / ESD Spare Management:** Safety-Instrumented-System valves and shutdown relays have low demand frequency but **catastrophic stock-out cost**. The EOQ model is invalid here — we use **Min-Max with high safety stock** based on **SIL (Safety Integrity Level)** calculations from IEC 61508/61511.
- **PPE Kitting:** Personal Protective Equipment (gloves, helmets, respirators) has predictable, high-volume demand → **standard EOQ applies**.
- **Calibration Gas Cylinders:** Critical for gas detectors → **EOQ with explicit safety stock formula using z = 2.33 (99% service)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of Wilson's EOQ

**Assumptions (you must list these in the exam):**
1. Demand rate $D$ is known and **constant**
2. Lead time $L$ is known and **constant**
3. Order is received **instantaneously** (infinite replenishment rate)
4. No quantity discounts
5. Only ordering cost and holding cost vary with $Q$
6. No stockouts permitted

**Step 1:** Write annual ordering cost as a function of $Q$.

$$C_O(Q) = \left(\frac{\text{Number of orders}}{\text{year}}\right) \times \left(\frac{\text{Cost per order}}{}\right) = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S$$

*Logic:* If we order $Q$ units each time and need $D$ units annually, we must place $D/Q$ orders per year, each costing $S$.

**Step 2:** Write annual holding cost as a function of $Q$.

$$C_H(Q) = \left(\frac{\text{Average inventory level}}{}\right) \times \left(\frac{\text{Holding cost per unit per year}}{}\right) = \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H$$

*Logic:* Inventory drops linearly from $Q$ to $0$ over one cycle, so the average is $(Q + 0)/2 = Q/2$. Each unit costs $H$ per year to store.

**Step 3:** Total annual variable cost.

$$TC(Q) = \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{HQ}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate with respect to $Q$ and set to zero.

$$\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $Q$.

$$-\frac{DS}{Q^{2}} = -\frac{H}{2} \implies \frac{DS}{Q^{2}} = \frac{H}{2} \implies Q^{2} = \frac{2DS}{H}$$

$$\boxed{Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}}$$

**Step 6:** Verify the second derivative is positive (we have a minimum, not a maximum).

$$\frac{d^{2}(TC)}{dQ^{2}} = \frac{2DS}{Q^{3}} > 0 \quad \text{always, since } D, S, Q > 0$$

✅ Confirmed minimum.

**Step 7:** Proof that **Ordering Cost = Holding Cost at the optimum**.

At $Q = Q^*$:

$$C_O = \frac{DS}{Q^{*}} = \frac{DS}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} = \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}}$$

$$C_H = \frac{Q^{*} H}{2} = \frac{\sqrt{2DS/H} \cdot H}{2} = \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}}$$

$$\therefore C_O = C_H = \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}} \quad \text{at optimum}$$

This is the **fundamental trade-off theorem** — always state it in your exam answer.

**Step 8:** Minimum Total Cost.

$$TC_{\min} = \sqrt{2DSH}$$

---

## 3.2 Reorder Point (ROP) Derivation

**ROP** is the inventory level that triggers a new purchase order.

**Without variability:** $ROP = d \times L$

- Logic: During the lead time $L$ days, demand $d$ units/day will be consumed. We must reorder *before* we run out.

**With demand variability (Safety Stock included):**

$$ROP = \overline{d} \cdot L + z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$$

where $\sigma_{LT} = \sigma_d \sqrt{L}$ if daily demand is normally distributed with std dev $\sigma_d$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trap:** Students often write $\sigma_{LT} = \sigma_d \cdot L$ — that is **wrong**. Variance adds, not standard deviation: $\sigma_{LT}^{2} = L \cdot \sigma_d^{2}$, therefore $\sigma_{LT} = \sigma_d \sqrt{L}$.

---

## 3.3 Production Order Quantity (EPQ / EMQ) Derivation

When inventory is **replenished gradually** at rate $p$ (production rate) while demand continues at rate $d$, the effective holding cost is reduced.

**Inventory buildup rate** $= p - d$ (units/day) during production phase.

**Maximum inventory** $= (p-d) \cdot T_p$ where $T_p$ is the production run time.

Since $T_p = Q/p$, max inventory $= (p-d) \cdot Q / p = Q \cdot (1 - d/p)$.

**Average inventory** $= \dfrac{Q}{2} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right)$

**Total cost:**

$$TC = \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{Q H}{2} \left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)$$

Differentiating and setting to zero:

$$\boxed{Q_p^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H\left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right)}}}$$

**Special case:** If $p \to \infty$ (instantaneous replenishment), $d/p \to 0$ and we recover Wilson's EOQ. ✅

---

## 3.4 EOQ with Planned Backorders

If a firm **deliberately allows stockouts** (e.g., customers are willing to wait) and the backorder cost per unit per year is $B$:

$$TC(Q, B) = \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{H(Q-B)^{2}}{2Q} + \frac{B^{2} P}{2Q}$$

where $P$ is the annual penalty per backordered unit. Setting partial derivatives to zero:

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{P+H}{P}}$$

$$B^{*} = Q^{*} \cdot \frac{H}{P+H}$$

---

## 3.5 ABC Analysis (Always Better Control) — Pareto Framework

The **80/20 rule** applied to inventory:

| Class | % of Items | % of Annual Value | Control Policy |
|-------|-----------|-------------------|----------------|
| **A** | 10–20% | 70–80% | Tight control, frequent review, EOQ |
| **B** | 20–30% | 15–25% | Moderate control, periodic review |
| **C** | 50–70% | 5–10% | Loose control, bulk ordering |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📊 Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Inventory Cycle — Sawtooth Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Inventory Level Q"] -->|"Steady demand consumption"| nodeB["Inventory reaches ROP"]
    nodeB -->|"Place new order"| nodeC["Lead Time L days elapse"]
    nodeC -->|"Instant replenishment"| nodeA
    nodeD["Safety Stock SS"] -.->|"Lower bound never crossed"| nodeB
    nodeE["Time axis t"] --> nodeF["Cycle Time T = Q/D"]
    nodeF --> nodeA
```

## 4.2 Inventory Decision Tree (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS["START: Inventory Decision"] --> nodeQ1{"Is demand known and constant?"}
    nodeQ1 -->|"Yes"| nodeQ2{"Is replenishment instantaneous?"}
    nodeQ1 -->|"No - variable"| nodeS3["Use Probabilistic Model: ROP with Safety Stock"]
    nodeQ2 -->|"Yes"| nodeS1["Classical Wilson EOQ: Q* = sqrt 2DS / H"]
    nodeQ2 -->|"No - production lot"| nodeS2["EPQ Model: Qp = sqrt 2DS / H times 1 over 1 minus d by p"]
    nodeS1 --> nodeS4{"Are backorders permitted?"}
    nodeS2 --> nodeS4
    nodeS4 -->|"No"| nodeS5["Standard answer: no shortages"]
    nodeS4 -->|"Yes"| nodeS6["Backorder EOQ with Penalty P"]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS7["Classify via ABC and VED analysis"]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS8["Compute ROP = d*L plus z*sigma"]
    nodeS7 --> nodeS9["Set Min-Max levels for critical safety spares"]
    nodeS8 --> nodeS10["Apply Just-In-Time for low-value items"]
    nodeS6 --> nodeS11["Report TCmin = sqrt 2DSH"]
    nodeS9 --> nodeS12["End"]
    nodeS10 --> nodeS12
    nodeS11 --> nodeS12
```

## 4.3 Inventory Classification Systems Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA1["Inventory Item"] --> nodeB1{"Based on Consumption Value?"}
    nodeB1 -->|"Yes"| nodeC1["ABC Analysis"]
    nodeB1 -->|"No"| nodeB2{"Based on Criticality?"}
    nodeB2 -->|"Yes"| nodeC2["VED: Vital Essential Desirable"]
    nodeB2 -->|"No"| nodeB3{"Based on Usage Rate?"}
    nodeB3 -->|"Yes"| nodeC3["FSN: Fast Slow Non-moving"]
    nodeB3 -->|"No"| nodeB4{"Based on Unit Price?"}
    nodeB4 -->|"Yes"| nodeC4["HML: High Medium Low"]
    nodeB4 -->|"No"| nodeC5["SDE: Scarce Difficult Easy"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeD["Applied to Store Control"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeD
    nodeC3 --> nodeD
    nodeC4 --> nodeD
    nodeC5 --> nodeD
```

## 4.4 Cost Behaviour vs Order Quantity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeX["Order Quantity Q"] --> nodeY1["Ordering Cost DS/Q"]
    nodeX --> nodeY2["Holding Cost QH/2"]
    nodeY1 --> nodeZ1["Hyperbolic decrease"]
    nodeY2 --> nodeZ2["Linear increase"]
    nodeZ1 --> nodeW["TC = Ordering plus Holding"]
    nodeZ2 --> nodeW
    nodeW --> nodeOPT["Optimum at intersection: Q* where OC = HC"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Define inventory. List any four types of inventory with one industrial example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Inventory is the stock of physical items held by an organization to meet customer demand, support production, and buffer against uncertainty in supply and demand.

**Four types (2 marks — ½ mark each):**

1. **Raw Materials** → e.g., Iron ore stored at a steel plant intake bay.
2. **Work-In-Process (WIP)** → e.g., Partially machined engine blocks on the assembly line.
3. **Finished Goods** → e.g., Packed LPG cylinders ready for dispatch from the bottling plant.
4. **MRO (Maintenance, Repair, Operating Supplies)** → e.g., Spare bearings, lubricants, gaskets used to maintain plant equipment.

*(Examiner's note: A 5th type — *Pipeline/Transit Inventory* — also fetches credit.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Explain the concept of Safety Stock. Why is it needed even when EOQ is computed?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Stock (1.5 marks):** Safety stock is the extra inventory held *beyond* the expected lead-time demand, to protect against variability in either demand rate or supplier lead time.

**Need (1.5 marks):**

- EOQ assumes **constant** demand and lead time. Real life has variability.
- It acts as a **buffer against stockouts** when actual demand exceeds forecast or when supplier delivery is delayed.
- It ensures a **target service level** (e.g., 95% or 99%) is maintained.
- In **safety-critical industries** (oil & gas, nuclear, pharma), safety stock is mandatory — a missing safety valve can cause a hazardous shutdown.

**Formula:** $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$ where $z$ is the service-level constant (1.65 for 95%, 2.33 for 99%).

---

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Note (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Answer ANY ONE full question from each module. Each 14-mark question carries two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### 🅰️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - 2024 Pattern - Module 2 Choice A]`

**(a)** A manufacturing plant uses **12,000 units/year** of a component. The ordering cost is **₹250 per order** and the holding cost is **₹4 per unit per year**. Calculate:
   (i) Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)
   (ii) Number of orders per year
   (iii) Time between orders
   (iv) Minimum total annual cost.

**(b)** Explain the **ABC analysis** of inventory with a suitable industrial example. Why is 'A' class inventory given tight control?

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION (a) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
$D = 12{,}000$ units/year, $S = ₹250$/order, $H = ₹4$/unit/year

**(i) EOQ (3 marks):**

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 250}{4}}$$

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{6{,}000{,}000}{4}} = \sqrt{1{,}500{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{Q^{*} \approx 1224.7 \approx 1225 \text{ units}}$$

**(ii) Number of orders per year (1.5 marks):**

$$N = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{12{,}000}{1224.7} \approx 9.80 \approx 10 \text{ orders/year}$$

**(iii) Time between orders (1 mark):**

$$T = \frac{Q^{*}}{D} = \frac{1224.7}{12{,}000} = 0.102 \text{ years} \approx 37.3 \text{ days}$$

(assuming 365 working days)

**(iv) Minimum total cost (1.5 marks):**

$$TC_{\min} = \sqrt{2DSH} = \sqrt{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 250 \times 4} = \sqrt{24{,}000{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{TC_{\min} = ₹4{,}898.98 \approx ₹4{,}899 \text{ per year}}$$

**Verification:** $C_O = (1224.7/1224.7) \times 250 = $ wait — using $D/Q \cdot S = 9.80 \times 250 = ₹2{,}450$. $C_H = 1224.7/2 \times 4 = ₹2{,}449.4$. Sum ≈ ₹4,899 ✓ (with rounding they balance — proves OC = HC at optimum).

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION (b) — 7 Marks

**ABC Analysis (3 marks):**

ABC analysis is an inventory classification technique based on the **Pareto principle (80/20 rule)**. Items are classified by their **annual consumption value** (Consumption × Unit Cost):

- **A items (~10–20% of SKUs):** Contribute **~70–80%** of annual consumption value. **Tight control** — frequent review, accurate records, EOQ ordering, close follow-up with vendors.
- **B items (~20–30% of SKUs):** Contribute **~15–25%** of value. Moderate control — periodic review (monthly/quarterly).
- **C items (~50–70% of SKUs):** Contribute **~5–10%** of value. Loose control — bulk ordering, simple two-bin system.

**Industrial Example (2 marks):**

In an **automobile assembly plant:**
- **A items:** Engine blocks, transmissions, ECUs (few items, very high value, used daily).
- **B items:** Brake pads, filters, gaskets (moderate value, regular consumption).
- **C items:** Nuts, bolts, washers, cable ties (huge variety, low unit cost).

**Why tight control on 'A' items? (2 marks):**

1. **High capital lock-up** — small inventory errors cause large financial losses.
2. **Stockout impact** — missing one engine block halts an entire production line.
3. **Tighter vendor management** is needed to ensure on-time delivery.
4. **Forecasting accuracy** matters most — small demand errors translate to large value errors.

---

### 🅱️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - 2024 Pattern - Module 2 Choice B]`

**(a)** A factory requires **5,000 units/year** of a critical spare. The daily demand is normally distributed with **mean = 20 units/day** and **standard deviation = 4 units/day**. The lead time is **10 days** and the desired service level is **97.5%**. Calculate:
   (i) Reorder Point (ROP)
   (ii) Safety Stock
   (iii) If the company uses an EOQ of 500 units, how many orders are placed per year, and what is the cycle time?

**(b)** Differentiate between **Fixed-Order-Quantity (Q-System)** and **Fixed-Order-Period (P-System)** inventory models. In an industrial safety context, which system is preferred for **SIL-3 emergency shutdown valves**, and why?

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION (a) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
$D = 5{,}000$ units/year, $\bar{d} = 20$ units/day, $\sigma_d = 4$ units/day, $L = 10$ days, Service Level = 97.5%

**Step 1 — Find z (1 mark):**
For 97.5% service level, $z = 1.96$ (from standard normal table).

**Step 2 — Lead-time demand standard deviation (1 mark):**

$$\sigma_{LT} = \sigma_d \sqrt{L} = 4 \times \sqrt{10} = 4 \times 3.162 = 12.65 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3 — Safety Stock (2 marks):**

$$SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT} = 1.96 \times 12.65 \approx 24.8 \approx 25 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4 — Reorder Point (2 marks):**

$$ROP = \bar{d} \times L + SS = 20 \times 10 + 25 = 200 + 25$$

$$\boxed{ROP = 225 \text{ units}}$$

**Step 5 — Orders/year and Cycle Time (1 mark):**

$$N = \frac{D}{Q} = \frac{5{,}000}{500} = 10 \text{ orders/year}$$

$$T = \frac{Q}{D} = \frac{500}{5{,}000} = 0.1 \text{ year} = 36.5 \text{ days}$$

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION (b) — 7 Marks

| Feature | **Q-System (Continuous Review)** | **P-System (Periodic Review)** |
|---------|--------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Trigger | Reorder Point (continuous monitoring) | Fixed time interval (e.g., weekly) |
| Order Quantity | Fixed (= EOQ) | Variable (up to a Maximum level) |
| Record keeping | Every transaction | Only at review time |
| Inventory position tracking | Real-time | Only at review |
| Safety stock requirement | Lower (since response is instant) | Higher (must cover review period + lead time) |
| Use case | High-value items (A-class), critical spares | Grouped ordering, low-value items |

**Industrial Safety Context (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **SIL-3 Emergency Shutdown (ESD) Valves** as per **IEC 61508/61511**, the **Q-System (Fixed-Order-Quantity)** is preferred because:
> 1. **Continuous monitoring** ensures immediate detection of depletion — critical when a missing valve can defeat the Safety Instrumented Function (SIF).
> 2. **Lower safety stock** means less capital locked up while still meeting the required Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD).
> 3. **Real-time traceability** required for audit under OSHA / PSM (Process Safety Management) regulations.
> 4. The P-System's periodic review window introduces an unacceptable **hazardous delay** if demand spikes between reviews.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in Inventory Analysis:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the safety-stock term in ROP.** Many students write only $d \times L$ — that's **wrong when demand is variable**. You lose 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Confusing $\sigma_{LT} = \sigma_d \cdot L$ with $\sigma_d \sqrt{L}$.** Variance adds, not std dev. This is the #1 algebraic trap in KTU papers.
> 3. **Not stating EOQ assumptions.** Wilson's formula is valid only under the 6 classical assumptions. If the question says "with quantity discounts" or "with backorders", you must derive the *modified* formula. Using Wilson blindly loses full marks.
> 4. **Mixing up $S$ and $H$.** $S$ = cost per ORDER; $H$ = cost per UNIT per YEAR. Don't transpose them in the EOQ square root.
> 5. **In ABC analysis**, students often forget to mention **annual consumption value** (price × quantity) and just write "value" — examiners mark this as incomplete.
> 6. **Units mismatch:** If $D$ is given monthly and $H$ yearly, convert before applying EOQ. Show the unit conversion step explicitly.

---

## 🧾 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **⭐ HIGH-YIELD — Memorize these for last-night revision:**

- [ ] **Wilson's EOQ:** $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ — derived assuming constant demand, no shortages, instantaneous replenishment.
- [ ] **Golden rule at optimum:** Ordering Cost = Holding Cost.
- [ ] **Minimum TC:** $TC_{\min} = \sqrt{2DSH}$.
- [ ] **ROP formula (probabilistic):** $ROP = dL + z\sigma_d\sqrt{L}$.
- [ ] **EPQ formula:** $Q_p = \sqrt{2DS/[H(1 - d/p)]}$.
- [ ] **Number of orders/year:** $N = D/Q$.
- [ ] **Time Between Orders:** $TBO = Q/D$ (convert to days using 250–365 working days).
- [ ] **Average inventory (EOQ):** $\bar{I} = Q/2$.
- [ ] **Inventory Turnover Ratio:** $ITR = COGS / \bar{I}$ — higher is better.
- [ ] **ABC:** 10/20 contributes 70/80 of value — Pareto.
- [ ] **VED:** Vital (must stock), Essential (must stock), Desirable (can do without).
- [ ] **H = r × C** where $r$ is carrying rate and $C$ is unit cost.
- [ ] **Safety Stock grows with z** — doubling service level from 95% → 97.5% raises z from 1.65 → 1.96, increasing SS by ~19%.
- [ ] **In industrial safety (SIL-rated spares):** Min-Max with high SS, NOT pure EOQ.
- [ ] **Q-system vs P-system:** Q for critical/A-class; P for grouped/C-class.
- [ ] **Backorder EOQ:** $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H} \cdot \sqrt{(P+H)/P}$.
- [ ] **Reorder is triggered when inventory hits ROP, NOT when it hits zero.**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire and Explosion Hazard Rating of Process Plants - The Dow Fire and Explosion Hazard Index
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT743 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fire and Explosion Hazard Rating of Process Plants - The Dow Fire and Explosion Hazard Index

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Dow Fire and Explosion Hazard Index (F&EI)** is a *quantitative, semi-empirical risk assessment methodology* developed by the **Dow Chemical Company** (first published in 1964, refined through multiple editions by the **American Institute of Chemical Engineers – Center for Chemical Process Safety / CCPS**). It rates the **relative magnitude of fire, explosion, and reactive chemical hazards** of a defined process unit (e.g., a reactor, distillation column, or storage tank farm) in a chemical process plant.

The Dow Index converts qualitative process knowledge into a **single dimensionless numerical score (F&EI)** that:
- Ranks the process unit on a hazard severity scale (Light → Extreme).
- Estimates the **radius of exposure** (area that could be affected by a fireball/blast).
- Drives decisions on **insurance premiums, plant layout, loss-prevention engineering, and regulatory compliance.**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine a hospital's *patient-acuity scoring system* (like the APACHE score in ICUs). Doctors don't just say "the patient is sick"; they assign numerical points to vital signs, lab values, age, etc., to predict mortality risk. The Dow F&EI does exactly the same for a process unit: instead of blood pressure and heart rate, it scores **flammability, reactivity, operating pressure, inventory, and layout penalties** to predict how catastrophically the unit might "die" in a fire/explosion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition**: A higher Dow F&EI number means the process unit has *more fire/explosion energy stored* and *more triggers that can release it*.

### 1.3 The Three Foundational Pillars of the Dow Index

The Dow methodology is mathematically anchored to three pillars:

| Pillar | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Material Factor** | $MF$ | Intrinsic energy-release potential of the chemical | **1 – 40** |
| **General Process Hazards** | $GPH$ | Penalties for *plant-level* unsafe features | Penalty terms $\geq 1.0$ |
| **Special Process Hazards** | $SPH$ | Penalties for *unit-specific* high-risk conditions | Penalty terms $\geq 1.0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These three pillars combine multiplicatively. A *doubling* of any one pillar roughly *doubles* the F&EI. This is why process safety engineers obsessively try to reduce each term — even a 10% reduction in $MF$, $GPH$, or $SPH$ can drop the F&EI by ~10%.

### 1.4 Why the Dow Index Matters in Industry

- **Insurance underwriting** — Insurers (e.g., Factory Mutual, Allianz) use Dow scores to set premiums for property loss insurance.
- **Plant layout design** — Determines inter-unit spacing, fire-wall requirements, and emergency-vehicle access roads.
- **Quantitative Risk Analysis (QRA)** — A precursor input for more advanced techniques like **HAZOP**, **LOPA**, and **Bow-Tie analysis**.
- **Regulatory benchmarking** — Used by OSHA's **Process Safety Management (PSM, 29 CFR 1910.119)** and India's **Major Accident Hazard (MAH) rules** under the Factories Act.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Material Factor ($MF$) — The Heart of the Dow Index

The **Material Factor** is the *starting score* that quantifies the **intrinsic fire/explosion energy** locked inside a chemical. It is derived from the **NFPA 704 hazard diamond ratings**:

- $N_f$ = NFPA **Flammability** rating (0–4)
- $N_r$ = NFPA **Reactivity** rating (0–4)

The MF is then read off Dow's tabulated chart using $(N_f, N_r)$ coordinates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard MF Values (Memorize for KTU Exams)**:
> - **Acetone**: $N_f=3$, $N_r=0$ → $MF = 16$
> - **Methane**: $N_f=4$, $N_r=0$ → $MF = 21$
> - **Hydrogen**: $N_f=4$, $N_r=0$ → $MF = 21$
> - **Gasoline**: $N_f=3$, $N_r=0$ → $MF = 16$
> - **Methanol**: $N_f=3$, $N_r=0$ → $MF = 16$

A higher $MF$ indicates that the material has *more available energy* per unit mass that could be released during a fire or BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion).

### 2.2 General Process Hazards ($GPH$) — Plant-Level Penalty Stack

The GPH captures penalties for *general plant features* that affect all units equally.

| # | GPH Category | Penalty Range | Typical Trigger |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Exothermic chemical reactions** | $0 – 1.75$ | Hydrogenation, polymerization, sulfonation |
| 2 | **Endothermic processes** | $0.20 – 0.40$ | Calcination, pyrolysis, cracking |
| 3 | **Material handling & transfer** | $0.25 – 1.05$ | Open transfer vs. closed piping |
| 4 | **Enclosed / indoor process units** | $0.25 – 0.90$ | Confined-space operations |
| 5 | **Access** | $0.20 – 0.35$ | Restricted egress for fire crews |
| 6 | **Drainage & spill control** | $0.25 – 0.50$ | Bunding, sloped flooring |

The total GPH penalty is **summed**:
$$GPH_{\text{sum}} = \sum_{i=1}^{6} GPH_i$$
And the GPH multiplier used in the F&EI equation is:
$$GPH_{\text{factor}} = 1 + GPH_{\text{sum}}$$

### 2.3 Special Process Hazards ($SPH$) — Unit-Specific Penalty Stack

| # | SPH Category | Penalty Range | Typical Trigger |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Toxic materials** | $0.20 – 0.80$ | HCN, phosgene, chlorine |
| 2 | **Sub-atmospheric pressure** | $0.50$ | Vacuum distillation |
| 3 | **Operation in or near flammable range** | $0 – 1.20$ | Above LEL or below UEL operation |
| 4 | **Dust explosion potential** | $0.25 – 2.00$ | Powder handling (grain, coal, polymers) |
| 5 | **Relief / overpressure devices** | $0 – 1.50$ | Set pressure above MAWP |
| 6 | **Low-temperature operation** | $0.20 – 0.30$ | Cryogenic LNG, ammonia |
| 7 | **Quantity of flammable material** | $0 – 3.00$ (chart-based) | Inventory mass vs. enthalpy |
| 8 | **Corrosion & erosion** | $0.10 – 1.50$ | Sour service, slurry handling |
| 9 | **Leakage (joints & packing)** | $0.10 – 1.50$ | Flanged joints, pump seals |
| 10 | **Use of fired heaters** | $0 – 1.00$ | Direct-fired reboilers |
| 11 | **Hot oil heat exchange system** | $0.15 – 1.15$ | Thermic-fluid heaters |
| 12 | **Rotating equipment** | $0.50$ | Pumps, compressors |

The total SPH penalty is **summed**:
$$SPH_{\text{sum}} = \sum_{i=1}^{12} SPH_i$$
And the SPH multiplier used in the F&EI equation is:
$$SPH_{\text{factor}} = 1 + SPH_{\text{sum}}$$

### 2.4 The Master F&EI Equation

$$\boxed{F\&EI = MF \times GPH_{\text{factor}} \times SPH_{\text{factor}} = MF \times \bigl(1 + \sum GPH_i\bigr) \times \bigl(1 + \sum SPH_i\bigr)}$$

### 2.5 Hazard Severity Ranking (Dow's Master Table)

| F&EI Range | Hazard Degree | Operational Implication |
|---|---|---|
| $1 - 60$ | **Light** | Standard industrial housekeeping |
| $61 - 96$ | **Moderate** | Routine safety measures |
| $97 - 127$ | **Intermediate** | Enhanced monitoring required |
| $128 - 158$ | **Heavy** | Major loss-prevention investment |
| **$\geq 159$** | **Very Heavy / Extreme** | Plant relocation or redesign often justified |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners *love* asking students to state this ranking. Memorize the cutoff values: **60, 96, 127, 158**.

### 2.6 Loss Control Credit Factors (LCCF) — Mitigation Reduces the Index

The base F&EI assumes *no* active fire protection. Dow introduced **three credit categories** that *reduce* the actual operating hazard:

| Credit Category | Symbol | Range | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Process Control** ($C_1$) | $C_1$ | $0.61 - 0.98$ | DCS, alarms, interlocks, ESD systems |
| **Material Isolation** ($C_2$) | $C_2$ | $0.85 - 0.98$ | Emergency block valves, remote isolation, vapor return |
| **Fire Protection** ($C_3$) | $C_3$ | $0.70 - 0.97$ | Sprinklers, foam systems, gas detectors, fire brigade |

The **Actual (Operating) F&EI** is:
$$\boxed{F\&EI_{\text{actual}} = F\&EI_{\text{base}} \times C_1 \times C_2 \times C_3}$$

### 2.7 Radius of Exposure ($R$) and Damage Factor ($DF$)

Once the F&EI is computed, Dow provides two engineering deliverables:

**Radius of Exposure (in feet):**
$$\boxed{R \; (\text{ft}) = 0.84 \times \sqrt{F\&EI \times 1000}}$$

**Or in metric (meters):**
$$R \; (\text{m}) = 0.256 \times \sqrt{F\&EI \times 1000}$$

**Area of Exposure:**
$$A = \pi R^2$$

**Damage Factor** ($DF$) is read from a Dow chart (or tabulated approximation) based on $F\&EI$:

| F&EI | Damage Factor $DF$ | Damage State |
|---|---|---|
| $1 – 60$ | $0.00$ | No damage |
| $61 – 96$ | $0.10$ | Minor |
| $97 – 127$ | $0.30$ | Moderate |
| $128 – 158$ | $0.60$ | Heavy |
| $159 – 200+$ | $0.85 – 1.00$ | Total destruction |

**Base Maximum Probable Property Damage ($MPPD_0$):**
$$MPPD_0 = \text{Replacement Cost} \times DF$$

With Loss Control Credits:
$$MPPD_{\text{actual}} = MPPD_0 \times \bigl(1 - C_1 C_2 C_3 \times \text{Adjustments}\bigr)$$

### 2.8 The 10-Step Dow F&EI Procedure (KTU Standard)

The Dow Index is implemented through a **systematic 10-step workflow** — KTU questions often ask students to enumerate these steps:

1. Identify the **process unit** (smallest independent unit, e.g., a single reactor).
2. Determine the **Material Factor ($MF$)** from NFPA ratings.
3. List applicable **General Process Hazards** and apply penalties.
4. List applicable **Special Process Hazards** and apply penalties.
5. Compute $GPH_{\text{factor}} = 1 + \sum GPH_i$.
6. Compute $SPH_{\text{factor}} = 1 + \sum SPH_i$.
7. Compute base **$F\&EI = MF \times GPH_{\text{factor}} \times SPH_{\text{factor}}$**.
8. Determine **Loss Control Credit Factors** ($C_1$, $C_2$, $C_3$).
9. Compute **$F\&EI_{\text{actual}} = F\&EI \times C_1 \times C_2 \times C_3$**.
10. Determine **Radius of Exposure** and **Damage Factor** for layout decisions.

### 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All equations must be reproduced from memory in the KTU exam.** The following consolidated table is your exam-day reference:

| # | Concept | Equation | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Material Factor (chart-read) | $MF = f(N_f, N_r)$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | GPH multiplier | $GPH_{\text{factor}} = 1 + \sum_{i=1}^{6} GPH_i$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | SPH multiplier | $SPH_{\text{factor}} = 1 + \sum_{i=1}^{12} SPH_i$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Base F&EI | $F\&EI_{\text{base}} = MF \cdot GPH_{\text{factor}} \cdot SPH_{\text{factor}}$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Loss Control Credit (composite) | $LCCF = C_1 \cdot C_2 \cdot C_3$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Actual F&EI | $F\&EI_{\text{actual}} = F\&EI_{\text{base}} \cdot LCCF$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Radius of exposure (feet) | $R = 0.84 \cdot \sqrt{F\&EI_{\text{base}} \times 1000}$ | feet |
| 8 | Radius of exposure (meters) | $R = 0.256 \cdot \sqrt{F\&EI_{\text{base}} \times 1000}$ | meters |
| 9 | Area of exposure | $A = \pi R^2$ | $\text{ft}^2$ or $\text{m}^2$ |
| 10 | Base MPPD | $MPPD_0 = \text{Replacement Cost} \times DF$ | currency |

### 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dow's own plants in Texas & Louisiana** use the F&EI to decide on blast-resistant control rooms (typically required when $F\&EI > 128$).
- **Singapore's SCDF** (Singapore Civil Defence Force) requires Dow F&EI compliance for petrochemical storage terminals under the **Fire Safety Act**.
- **Reliance Jamnagar (India)** used F&EI during the design phase to lay out the world's largest refinery complex — a textbook case study.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Full Dow F&EI Calculation

**Problem (KTU-style)**: A pharmaceutical intermediate is manufactured in a **jacketed batch reactor** containing **acetone** as the solvent. The unit operates at **atmospheric pressure**, has **5,000 kg of acetone inventory**, uses **exothermic mixing** with mild polymerization, has **open material transfer** through flanged joints, is located **indoors in a non-ventilated shed**, and has **poor drainage**. The plant has **no active fire protection systems**, only a DCS with alarms, and an emergency block valve.

**Given Data:**
- NFPA ratings for acetone: $N_f = 3$, $N_r = 0$ → $MF = 16$
- GPH penalties to apply: Exothermic reaction $= 0.50$, Material handling $= 0.85$, Indoor unit $= 0.90$, Access $= 0.35$, Drainage $= 0.50$
- SPH penalties to apply: Toxic (acetone mild) $= 0.20$, Operation in flammable range $= 0.80$, Quantity penalty (chart: $0.50$), Corrosion $= 0.20$, Joints/leakage $= 0.50$
- Process Control $C_1 = 0.94$ (DCS + alarms), Material Isolation $C_2 = 0.98$ (remote valve), Fire Protection $C_3 = 1.00$ (none)
- Replacement cost of unit = ₹ 50,00,000 (INR 50 lakhs)

**Compute:**

#### Step 1 — Sum of GPH penalties

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum GPH_i &= 0.50 + 0.85 + 0.90 + 0.35 + 0.50 \\
&= 3.10
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 2 — GPH Multiplier

$$GPH_{\text{factor}} = 1 + 3.10 = 4.10$$

#### Step 3 — Sum of SPH penalties

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum SPH_i &= 0.20 + 0.80 + 0.50 + 0.20 + 0.50 \\
&= 2.20
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 4 — SPH Multiplier

$$SPH_{\text{factor}} = 1 + 2.20 = 3.20$$

#### Step 5 — Base F&EI

$$\begin{aligned}
F\&EI_{\text{base}} &= MF \times GPH_{\text{factor}} \times SPH_{\text{factor}} \\
&= 16 \times 4.10 \times 3.20 \\
&= 16 \times 13.12 \\
&= 209.92
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since $F\&EI_{\text{base}} = 209.92 > 158$, this unit is classified as **"Very Heavy / Extreme"** hazard.

#### Step 6 — Loss Control Credit Factor

$$\begin{aligned}
LCCF &= C_1 \times C_2 \times C_3 \\
&= 0.94 \times 0.98 \times 1.00 \\
&= 0.9212
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 7 — Actual F&EI

$$\begin{aligned}
F\&EI_{\text{actual}} &= F\&EI_{\text{base}} \times LCCF \\
&= 209.92 \times 0.9212 \\
&\approx 193.37
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Even with active DCS and isolation valves, the actual F&EI (193.37) remains in the **Very Heavy** category. This signals that **fire protection systems** ($C_3 < 1.0$) must be added to bring the unit down to "Heavy" or below.

#### Step 8 — Radius of Exposure

$$\begin{aligned}
R \; (\text{ft}) &= 0.84 \times \sqrt{F\&EI_{\text{base}} \times 1000} \\
&= 0.84 \times \sqrt{209920} \\
&= 0.84 \times 458.17 \\
&\approx 384.86 \; \text{ft} \quad (\approx 117.3 \; \text{m})
\end{aligned}$$

This means **any ignition source within ~385 ft of the reactor** could potentially involve the unit in a fire/explosion.

#### Step 9 — Area of Exposure

$$A = \pi R^2 = \pi \times (384.86)^2 \approx \pi \times 148{,}117 \approx 465{,}232 \; \text{ft}^2$$

#### Step 10 — Maximum Probable Property Damage ($MPPD$)

From the Dow Damage Factor table, for $F\&EI = 209.92$, $DF \approx 0.85$ (Very Heavy band).

$$\begin{aligned}
MPPD_0 &= \text{Replacement Cost} \times DF \\
&= 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.85 \\
&= 42{,}50{,}000 \; \text{INR} \; (\text{₹ }42.5 \; \text{lakhs})
\end{aligned}$$

With loss controls:
$$\begin{aligned}
MPPD_{\text{actual}} &= MPPD_0 \times \bigl[1 - (C_1 \cdot C_2 \cdot C_3)\bigr] \\
&= 42{,}50{,}000 \times (1 - 0.9212) \\
&= 42{,}50{,}000 \times 0.0788 \\
&\approx 3{,}34{,}900 \; \text{INR} \; (\text{₹ }3.35 \; \text{lakhs})
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering interpretation:** Loss-control systems reduce the **probable financial loss** by ~92% (from ₹42.5 lakhs to ₹3.35 lakhs) — but the **physical radius of exposure** does *not* shrink because that is governed by $F\&EI_{\text{base}}$, not the LCCF. Engineers must therefore **both reduce base hazards AND add protection systems**.

### 3.2 Analytical Sensitivity Matrix — What Happens If We Mitigate?

The following matrix shows how each individual penalty reduction impacts the final $F\&EI_{\text{base}}$ for the example above:

| Mitigation Action | Old Penalty | New Penalty | New $\sum GPH$ or $\sum SPH$ | New Multiplier | New F&EI |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Close material transfer (replace open flanges) | $0.85$ | $0.25$ | $2.50$ | $3.50$ | $16 \times 3.50 \times 3.20 = 179.20$ |
| Move reactor outdoors (deconfinement) | $0.90$ | $0.25$ | $2.45$ | $3.45$ | $16 \times 3.45 \times 3.20 = 176.64$ |
| Install sprinkler system ($C_3$) | $1.00$ | $0.85$ | — | — | Actual $F\&EI = 209.92 \times 0.85 = 178.43$ |
| Add gas detection + ESD ($C_1$) | $0.94$ | $0.75$ | — | — | Actual $F\&EI = 209.92 \times 0.75 \times 0.98 \times 1.00 = 154.31$ |
| Reduce inventory from 5000 kg to 1000 kg | Quantity $0.50$ | Quantity $0.10$ | $\sum SPH = 1.80$ | $2.80$ | $16 \times 4.10 \times 2.80 = 183.68$ |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that *inventory reduction* and *outdoor siting* are the most effective single mitigations — both individually push the base F&EI out of the "Extreme" band ($>158$).

### 3.3 Sensitivity to Material Factor Change

If we swap acetone ($MF = 16$) for **methanol** ($MF = 16$) — same MF, so no change.
But if we swap for **hydrogen** ($MF = 21$):

$$\begin{aligned}
F\&EI_{\text{base}} &= 21 \times 4.10 \times 3.20 \\
&= 21 \times 13.12 \\
&= 275.52 \quad (\text{Extreme})
\end{aligned}$$

A **31% increase** in F&EI just by changing the solvent — illustrating why material selection is the *first* design decision in process safety.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Top-Level Dow F&EI Calculation Flow

The diagram below maps the **complete Dow F&EI calculation workflow**, including all branches for penalty addition, hazard ranking, mitigation, and damage estimation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([Start: Identify Process Unit]) --> nodeMF{Determine Material Factor from NFPA N_f and N_r}
    nodeMF --> nodeMFVal[MF value 1 to 40]
    nodeMFVal --> nodeGPH[Enumerate General Process Hazards]
    nodeGPH --> nodeGPHSum[Sum all GPH penalties]
    nodeGPHSum --> nodeGPHMult[GPH factor equals 1 plus sum]
    nodeGPHMult --> nodeSPH[Enumerate Special Process Hazards]
    nodeSPH --> nodeSPHSum[Sum all SPH penalties]
    nodeSPHSum --> nodeSPHMult[SPH factor equals 1 plus sum]
    nodeSPHMult --> nodeFEI[Compute Base F and EI equals MF times GPH factor times SPH factor]
    nodeFEI --> nodeRank{F and EI greater than 158}
    nodeRank -- yes --> nodeExtreme[Hazard Rank: Extreme or Very Heavy]
    nodeRank -- no --> nodeModerate{F and EI greater than 96}
    nodeModerate -- yes --> nodeInter[Hazard Rank: Intermediate]
    nodeModerate -- no --> nodeLow[Hazard Rank: Light or Moderate]
    nodeFEI --> nodeLCCF[Determine Loss Control Credits C1 C2 C3]
    nodeLCCF --> nodeActual[Actual F and EI equals Base F and EI times C1 times C2 times C3]
    nodeActual --> nodeR[Compute Radius of Exposure R equals 0.84 times sqrt F and EI times 1000]
    nodeR --> nodeDF[Read Damage Factor DF from chart]
    nodeDF --> nodeMPPD[Compute MPPD equals Replacement Cost times DF]
    nodeExtreme --> nodeReport[Generate Safety Recommendation Report]
    nodeInter --> nodeReport
    nodeLow --> nodeReport
    nodeMPPD --> nodeReport
    nodeReport([End of Dow Analysis])
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Dow F&EI as a Safety System

This block diagram shows the Dow F&EI as a *three-layer safety-engineering system*: **Input Layer (Material + Process Data)**, **Computation Layer (Penalty Stack + Risk Multiplication)**, **Output Layer (Ranking + Radius + MPPD)**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer["Input Layer"]
        nodeNFPA[NFPA Ratings: N_f and N_r]
        nodeInv[Inventory and Enthalpy]
        nodeOps[Operating Conditions]
        nodeLayout[Plant Layout Data]
    end
    subgraph ComputeLayer["Computation Layer"]
        nodeMFcalc[Material Factor Lookup]
        nodeGPHcalc[GPH Penalty Stack]
        nodeSPHcalc[SPH Penalty Stack]
        nodeFEIcalc[F and EI Multiplier]
        nodeLCCFcalc[LCCF Composite]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer["Output Layer"]
        nodeRankOut[Hazard Ranking 1 to 200 plus]
        nodeRadOut[Radius of Exposure R]
        nodeDFOut[Damage Factor DF]
        nodeMPPDOut[MPPD Value in Currency]
    end
    subgraph MitigationLayer["Loss Control Credit Mitigation"]
        nodeC1[C1 Process Control]
        nodeC2[C2 Material Isolation]
        nodeC3[C3 Fire Protection]
    end
    nodeNFPA --> nodeMFcalc
    nodeInv --> nodeSPHcalc
    nodeOps --> nodeGPHcalc
    nodeLayout --> nodeGPHcalc
    nodeMFcalc --> nodeFEIcalc
    nodeGPHcalc --> nodeFEIcalc
    nodeSPHcalc --> nodeFEIcalc
    nodeFEIcalc --> nodeRankOut
    nodeFEIcalc --> nodeRadOut
    nodeRankOut --> nodeDFOut
    nodeDFOut --> nodeMPPDOut
    nodeC1 --> nodeLCCFcalc
    nodeC2 --> nodeLCCFcalc
    nodeC3 --> nodeLCCFcalc
    nodeLCCFcalc --> nodeRankOut
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree — Hazard Ranking Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([Compute F and EI]) --> nodeQ1{F and EI less than or equal to 60}
    nodeQ1 -- yes --> nodeLight[Hazard: LIGHT]
    nodeQ1 -- no --> nodeQ2{F and EI between 61 and 96}
    nodeQ2 -- yes --> nodeMod[Hazard: MODERATE]
    nodeQ2 -- no --> nodeQ3{F and EI between 97 and 127}
    nodeQ3 -- yes --> nodeInt[Hazard: INTERMEDIATE]
    nodeQ3 -- no --> nodeQ4{F and EI between 128 and 158}
    nodeQ4 -- yes --> nodeHeavy[Hazard: HEAVY]
    nodeQ4 -- no --> nodeVery[Hazard: VERY HEAVY OR EXTREME]
    nodeLight --> nodeAct1[Action: Standard housekeeping]
    nodeMod --> nodeAct2[Action: Routine safety measures]
    nodeInt --> nodeAct3[Action: Enhanced monitoring and interlocks]
    nodeHeavy --> nodeAct4[Action: Major loss prevention investment]
    nodeVery --> nodeAct5[Action: Plant relocation or redesign]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These diagrams are **engineered for KTU exam answer sheets** — examiners often reward students who include a labelled flowchart alongside their calculation. Reproducing even a simplified version of the above is a **scoring differentiator**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
*Define the term "Material Factor" as used in the Dow Fire and Explosion Hazard Index. State the NFPA ratings for acetone and calculate its Material Factor.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
The **Material Factor (MF)** is a numerical score (ranging from 1 to 40) that quantifies the **intrinsic fire and explosion energy release potential** of a chemical substance. It is the foundational input to the Dow Fire and Explosion Hazard Index.

- **NFPA Ratings for acetone**: $N_f = 3$ (Flammability), $N_r = 0$ (Reactivity).
- From Dow's MF lookup chart for $(N_f=3, N_r=0)$ → $\boxed{MF_{\text{acetone}} = 16}$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
*List the hazard severity categories defined by the Dow F&EI with their numerical ranges.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
The Dow F&EI classifies process units into **five hazard categories**:

| Category | F&EI Range |
|---|---|
| Light | $1 - 60$ |
| Moderate | $61 - 96$ |
| Intermediate | $97 - 127$ |
| Heavy | $128 - 158$ |
| Very Heavy / Extreme | $\geq 159$ |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Major Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice A]**

**(a)** *Explain in detail the three foundational pillars of the Dow Fire and Explosion Hazard Index. Discuss how each is determined and combined. **[(7 marks)]***

**Model Solution:**

The Dow F&EI rests on **three multiplicative pillars**:

**Pillar 1 — Material Factor ($MF$) [2 marks]:**  
The MF is the intrinsic fire/explosion energy score of the chemical. It is derived from the **NFPA 704 ratings** — specifically the **flammability ($N_f$)** and **reactivity ($N_r$)** ratings. The MF is read off Dow's tabulated chart. Typical values: Acetone ($MF=16$), Methanol ($MF=16$), Hydrogen ($MF=21$), Toluene ($MF=16$).

**Pillar 2 — General Process Hazards ($GPH$) [2 marks]:**  
GPH captures *plant-level* unsafe features that apply broadly to all units. Six categories: Exothermic reactions, Endothermic processes, Material handling/transfer, Enclosed/indoor units, Access restrictions, and Drainage/spill control. Each category carries a penalty ranging from $0$ to $1.75$. The penalties are summed, and the GPH multiplier becomes $1 + \sum GPH_i$.

**Pillar 3 — Special Process Hazards ($SPH$) [2 marks]:**  
SPH captures *unit-specific* high-risk conditions. Twelve categories: Toxic materials, Sub-atmospheric pressure, Operation near flammable range, Dust explosion, Relief pressure, Low temperature, Quantity of flammable material, Corrosion/erosion, Joint leakage, Fired heaters, Hot-oil systems, and Rotating equipment. Each penalty is summed, and the SPH multiplier becomes $1 + \sum SPH_i$.

**Combination [1 mark]:**
$$F\&EI = MF \times (1 + \sum GPH_i) \times (1 + \sum SPH_i)$$

The **multiplicative combination** ensures that worsening any single pillar magnifies the total hazard disproportionately — this is the **safety-engineering rationale** for the Dow formulation.

---

**(b)** *A reactor processes **toluene** ($N_f=3, N_r=0$, $MF=16$). The unit applies the following penalties:*

- *GPH: Exothermic reaction 0.75, Material handling 0.50, Indoor unit 0.45*
- *SPH: Operation in flammable range 0.50, Quantity 0.40, Joint leakage 0.30*

*Compute the **base F&EI**, **classify the hazard**, and calculate the **radius of exposure in feet**. **[(7 marks)]***

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sum GPH penalties [1 mark]:**
$$\sum GPH = 0.75 + 0.50 + 0.45 = 1.70$$
$$GPH_{\text{factor}} = 1 + 1.70 = 2.70$$

**Step 2 — Sum SPH penalties [1 mark]:**
$$\sum SPH = 0.50 + 0.40 + 0.30 = 1.20$$
$$SPH_{\text{factor}} = 1 + 1.20 = 2.20$$

**Step 3 — Compute Base F&EI [2 marks]:**
$$F\&EI_{\text{base}} = MF \times GPH_{\text{factor}} \times SPH_{\text{factor}}$$
$$= 16 \times 2.70 \times 2.20$$
$$= 16 \times 5.94 = \boxed{95.04}$$

**Step 4 — Hazard classification [1 mark]:**  
Since $95.04$ lies between $61$ and $96$, the unit is classified as **"Moderate"** hazard.

**Step 5 — Radius of exposure [2 marks]:**
$$R = 0.84 \times \sqrt{F\&EI \times 1000} = 0.84 \times \sqrt{95040}$$
$$= 0.84 \times 308.29 \approx 258.96 \; \text{ft} \; (\approx 78.9 \; \text{m})$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes (KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout):**
> 1. **Forgetting to add 1** to $\sum GPH$ and $\sum SPH$ when forming the multipliers — a costly 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Using the actual F&EI instead of the base F&EI** in the radius formula — the radius is computed from the *unmitigated* $F\&EI_{\text{base}}$.
> 3. **Reporting the wrong hazard category** by mixing up cutoff boundaries (96 vs 97, 127 vs 128). Memorize: **60, 96, 127, 158**.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice B]**

**(a)** *With the help of a flowchart, explain the **step-by-step procedure** for computing the Dow Fire and Explosion Hazard Index. State the role of **Loss Control Credit Factors (LCCF)**. **[(7 marks)]***

**Model Solution:**

**Step-by-Step Dow F&EI Procedure [5 marks]:**

1. **Identify the process unit** — smallest independent unit (e.g., one reactor, one distillation column).
2. **Determine the Material Factor** ($MF$) from NFPA flammability ($N_f$) and reactivity ($N_r$) ratings.
3. **Identify & apply General Process Hazards (GPH)** penalties from six categories.
4. **Identify & apply Special Process Hazards (SPH)** penalties from twelve categories.
5. **Compute GPH multiplier** $= 1 + \sum GPH_i$.
6. **Compute SPH multiplier** $= 1 + \sum SPH_i$.
7. **Compute Base F&EI** $= MF \times GPH_{\text{factor}} \times SPH_{\text{factor}}$.
8. **Determine Loss Control Credit Factors** ($C_1$ for process control, $C_2$ for material isolation, $C_3$ for fire protection).
9. **Compute Actual F&EI** $= F\&EI_{\text{base}} \times C_1 \times C_2 \times C_3$.
10. **Determine Radius of Exposure** and **Damage Factor** for plant layout.

(Students should accompany this with a labelled flowchart — see Section 4.1 of these notes for a model diagram.)

**Role of Loss Control Credit Factors (LCCF) [2 marks]:**  
The LCCF *reduce* the actual operating hazard by crediting safety investments made in **process control** (DCS, alarms, ESD), **material isolation** (emergency block valves), and **fire protection** (sprinklers, foam, gas detection). Each $C$ value ranges from $0.61$ to $0.98$, with $1.00$ indicating *no protection*. The LCCF acknowledges that the **base F&EI is a worst-case scenario**; with engineered protections, the actual hazard is lower.

$$LCCF = C_1 \times C_2 \times C_3$$
$$F\&EI_{\text{actual}} = F\&EI_{\text{base}} \times LCCF$$

---

**(b)** *For the toluene unit in Question A (b), the plant installs: (i) a DCS with emergency shutdown ($C_1 = 0.85$), (ii) remote-operated block valves ($C_2 = 0.95$), and (iii) a foam-based fire suppression system ($C_3 = 0.80$). Compute the **actual F&EI**, and comment on the **effectiveness** of these mitigation measures. **[(7 marks)]***

**Model Solution:**

Given from previous computation: $F\&EI_{\text{base}} = 95.04$.

**Step 1 — Composite LCCF [2 marks]:**
$$LCCF = C_1 \times C_2 \times C_3 = 0.85 \times 0.95 \times 0.80$$
$$= 0.85 \times 0.76 = 0.646$$

**Step 2 — Actual F&EI [2 marks]:**
$$F\&EI_{\text{actual}} = F\&EI_{\text{base}} \times LCCF$$
$$= 95.04 \times 0.646 \approx \boxed{61.40}$$

**Step 3 — Classification after mitigation [1 mark]:**  
Since $61.40$ lies between $61$ and $96$, the unit remains **"Moderate"** hazard, but is **just above the Light threshold**. The improvements have lowered the index by $\approx 35.4\%$.

**Step 4 — Engineering Comment [2 marks]:**  
The mitigation measures (DCS, remote block valves, foam system) collectively reduce the F&EI by **~35%**, moving the unit from the upper edge of Moderate toward the Light category. To enter the **Light** band ($F\&EI < 60$), the plant must additionally address the *base hazards* — for instance, by reducing inventory (lower Quantity penalty), outdoor siting (lower Indoor penalty), or replacing toluene with a lower-MF solvent. **Loss controls alone cannot reduce the radius of exposure**, which remains governed by $F\&EI_{\text{base}} = 95.04$ and equals ~259 ft.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:**
> 1. **Multiplying the LCCF into the radius formula** — incorrect; $R$ is always from $F\&EI_{\text{base}}$.
> 2. **Confusing $F\&EI_{\text{base}}$ and $F\&EI_{\text{actual}}$** when classifying hazard — the *rank* is typically read off the *base* value; the *actual* value is used for risk-management decisions and insurance.
> 3. **Forgetting units** in the radius result — always specify feet or meters explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD KTU REVISION BULLETS — MEMORIZE BEFORE EXAM:**

- [ ] The **Dow F&EI** is a *quantitative* (not qualitative) hazard rating method developed by the **Dow Chemical Company**.
- [ ] The **Material Factor ($MF$)** comes from NFPA $N_f$ and $N_r$ ratings; range **1–40**.
- [ ] The Dow Index equation is: $F\&EI = MF \times (1 + \sum GPH_i) \times (1 + \sum SPH_i)$.
- [ ] **GPH** has **6** categories; **SPH** has **12** categories.
- [ ] Hazard severity bands: **Light (1–60), Moderate (61–96), Intermediate (97–127), Heavy (128–158), Very Heavy (≥159).**
- [ ] Loss Control Credits: $C_1$ (process control), $C_2$ (isolation), $C_3$ (fire protection). All $C$ values are $\leq 1$.
- [ ] **Actual F&EI** $= F\&EI_{\text{base}} \times C_1 \times C_2 \times C_3$.
- [ ] Radius of exposure (ft): $R = 0.84 \sqrt{F\&EI_{\text{base}} \times 1000}$. Radius is **always** computed from $F\&EI_{\text{base}}$, never from $F\&EI_{\text{actual}}$.
- [ ] Area of exposure: $A = \pi R^2$.
- [ ] **Damage Factor ($DF$)** is read from Dow's chart and ranges from $0$ (no damage) to $\sim 1.0$ (total destruction).
- [ ] **MPPD** $=$ Replacement Cost $\times DF$, with LCCF adjustments reducing the actual loss estimate.
- [ ] A unit with $F\&EI > 128$ typically requires **blast-resistant control rooms** and **major loss-prevention investment**.
- [ ] **Inventory reduction** is the single most effective mitigation lever.
- [ ] The Dow Index is a **screening tool**, not a substitute for full QRA, HAZOP, or LOPA.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
