topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT743 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Preliminary Hazard Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

**Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)** is a qualitative, early-stage systematic safety review technique used to identify, classify, and evaluate potential hazards associated with a proposed system, process, or facility during the conceptual and early engineering design phases. It is performed **before** detailed design data, Piping & Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs), or operating procedures are fully developed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Standard):**
> "Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA) is a structured, inductive hazard identification methodology performed during the front-end engineering design (FEED) stage to systematically identify foreseeable hazards, their root causes, potential consequences, and recommended safeguards, thereby enabling risk reduction decisions to be incorporated into the system design *before* detailed engineering commences."

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Architect's Pre-Build Safety Audit"**
>
> Imagine an architect designing a 30-story building. Before the first brick is laid or a steel beam is welded, the architect and a safety expert sit down at the drawing board. They ask: *"What could go wrong here? Could a fire break out? Could a gas cylinder explode in the basement? Could a child fall from the terrace?"* They list every imaginable hazard, rate it (high/medium/low), and decide where to add fire escapes, gas leak detectors, and railings.
>
> That is **exactly** what PHA does for an industrial plant — it is a *pre-construction* brainstorming session where engineers predict failures **on paper** so that accidents are prevented **in reality**.

## When is PHA Performed?

| Project Phase | Activity | PHA Status |
|---|---|---|
| **Conceptual Design** | Process flow defined | ✅ **PHASE 1 PHA** initiated |
| **FEED (Front-End Engineering)** | Basic engineering drawings | ✅ **PHA Updated & Refined** |
| **Detailed Engineering** | P&IDs complete | ❌ Switch to HAZOP, FMEA, SIL |
| **Operation** | Plant running | ❌ Use operating procedures & incident records |

## Key Terminology Standardized for KTU Exams

- **Hazard**: A physical situation or condition with the potential to cause harm to people, property, or environment.
- **Cause**: The specific event or failure that triggers the hazard.
- **Consequence (Effect)**: The resulting outcome if the hazard is realized.
- **Risk**: The combination of the severity of harm and the likelihood of its occurrence.
- **Safeguard (Mitigation)**: An engineered or procedural measure installed to reduce risk.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** PHA is an **inductive** (forward-thinking) technique — it starts with an *initiating event* and predicts the *consequences*. This is the **opposite** of deductive techniques like Fault Tree Analysis (FTA), which start with the *undesired event* and work backwards to root causes.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Objectives of PHA (KTU Board Exam Favourite)

1. **Identify** all credible hazards associated with the system at an early stage.
2. **Classify** hazards by severity of consequence and probability of occurrence.
3. **Evaluate** the associated risk using a qualitative risk matrix.
4. **Recommend** design changes, safeguards, or procedural controls.
5. **Document** the analysis for regulatory compliance and as input to detailed safety studies (HAZOP, SIL).

## Step-by-Step PHA Procedure (Board-Mark-Worthy)

The standard PHA follows an **8-step structured methodology** (per MIL-STD-882 and IEC 61882):

### Step 1: Define the System & Boundaries
- Establish the **scope**: which unit, process, or facility is being analyzed?
- Define **physical boundaries** (e.g., battery limits of a reactor unit) and **analytical boundaries** (excluded interfaces).
- Identify the **intended use** and operating modes (start-up, normal, shutdown, emergency).

### Step 2: Gather Background Information
- Process flow diagrams (PFDs)
- Material safety data sheets (MSDS)
- Past incident reports from similar facilities
- Applicable codes & standards (OSHA, NFPA, IS, ISO 12100)

### Step 3: Identify Hazards
- A multidisciplinary team (process, instrumentation, safety, operations) brainstorms.
- Use checklists and **guide words**: Mechanical, Thermal, Chemical, Electrical, Radiation, Human Error, Environmental.
- Examples: overpressure, runaway reaction, toxic release, fire, mechanical failure, dropped loads.

### Step 4: Determine Causes
- For each hazard, identify the **initiating event(s)**.
- Example: Overpressure → causes: blocked outlet valve, thermal expansion, external fire.

### Step 5: Determine Consequences (Effects)
- For each cause, describe the worst credible outcome.
- Example: Blocked outlet valve → overpressure → vessel rupture → BLEVE → personnel fatality.

### Step 6: Assess Severity & Probability
- Assign a **Severity Level (S)** and **Probability Level (P)** using predefined categories.

### Step 7: Calculate Risk and Rank Hazards
- Use the **Risk Matrix** to assign a Risk Level (e.g., 1A, 2B, 3C).

### Step 8: Recommend Safeguards & Re-assess
- Propose engineering controls (relief valves, alarms, interlocks), administrative controls, or PPE.
- **Re-evaluate** the residual risk after safeguards are incorporated.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Risk Index (R)** | $R = S \times P$ | Dimensionless ordinal score |
| **Severity Level (S)** | 1 = Negligible, 2 = Marginal, 3 = Critical, 4 = Catastrophic | KTU standard 4-tier scale |
| **Probability Level (P)** | A = Frequent, B = Probable, C = Occasional, D = Remote, E = Improbable | KTU standard 5-tier scale |
| **Residual Risk (R_res)** | $R_{res} = S \times P_{reduced}$ | After safeguards |
| **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** | $RRF = \dfrac{R_{initial}}{R_{residual}}$ | Effectiveness of safeguard |
| **As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP)** | $Risk \leq Tolerable\;Limit$ | Decision criterion for accepting risk |
| **Hazard Rate (λ)** | $\lambda = \dfrac{Number\;of\;failures}{Operating\;time}$ | failures/hour (used in PHA → FMEA transition) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Formatting Note:** The risk index $R$ is an **ordinal product**, not a true mathematical multiplication. It is mapped through a **Risk Matrix Lookup Table** (see Section 3).

## Standard Risk Matrix (KTU 4×5 Grid)

| Severity ↓ / Probability → | A (Frequent) | B (Probable) | C (Occasional) | D (Remote) | E (Improbable) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **4 (Catastrophic)** | 4A | 4B | 4C | 4D | 4E |
| **3 (Critical)** | 3A | 3B | 3C | 3D | 3E |
| **2 (Marginal)** | 2A | 2B | 2C | 2D | 2E |
| **1 (Negligible)** | 1A | 1B | 1C | 1D | 1E |

| Color Code | Risk Level | KTU Board Term |
|---|---|---|
| **Red (4A, 4B, 4C, 3A, 3B)** | **UNACCEPTABLE** | Must redesign or add multiple safeguards |
| **Orange (4D, 3C, 2A, 2B)** | **UNDESIRABLE** | ALARP demonstration required |
| **Yellow (4E, 3D, 3E, 2C, 1A)** | **ACCEPTABLE with controls** | Procedural safeguards mandatory |
| **Green (2D, 2E, 1B, 1C, 1D, 1E)** | **ACCEPTABLE** | No further action required |

## Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas**: Pre-FEED safety review for offshore platforms (API RP 14J compliant).
- **Chemical Plants**: First-pass screening of reactive chemistry hazards.
- **Pharmaceuticals**: OSHA Process Safety Management (PSM) prerequisite.
- **Nuclear**: Defense-in-depth safety philosophy begins with PHA.
- **Aerospace & Automotive**: Preliminary hazard list (PHL) for new vehicle platforms.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Risk Equation

The qualitative risk index is derived from the fundamental safety engineering relation:

$$
R = f(S, P)
$$

For KTU board calculations, this is operationalized as a **discrete ordinal product**:

$$
R = S \times P
$$

where $S \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and $P \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ (with A=5, B=4, C=3, D=2, E=1 in the numerical scale).

The expanded risk space spans:

$$
R_{min} = 1 \times 1 = 1
$$

$$
R_{max} = 4 \times 5 = 20
$$

After applying the **logarithmic banding** (most safety standards use a logarithmic risk mapping):

$$
\log_{10}(R) \rightarrow \text{Band} \in \{\text{Green, Yellow, Orange, Red}\}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Ammonia Storage Tank PHA

### Problem Setup
A 50-tonne pressurized ammonia ($NH_3$) storage vessel operates at **15 bar** and **40 °C**. Perform a PHA on the overpressure hazard.

### Step 1: Hazard Identification
- **Hazard**: Overpressure in ammonia storage tank

### Step 2: Cause Analysis
- **Cause 1**: Blocked outlet line due to operator closing manual valve inadvertently
- **Cause 2**: External fire impinging on the vessel (pool fire from diesel spill)
- **Cause 3**: Control valve failure (air-to-close fails open, heating the liquid)

### Step 3: Consequence Determination
- **Consequence**: Vessel rupture → catastrophic release of toxic $NH_3$ → multiple fatalities within 1 km radius

### Step 4: Severity & Probability Assignment (Initial — No Safeguards)

| Cause | Severity (S) | Probability (P) | Risk Index |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cause 1 (Blocked outlet) | 4 (Catastrophic) | C (Occasional) = 3 | $4 \times 3 = 12$ |
| Cause 2 (External fire) | 4 (Catastrophic) | D (Remote) = 2 | $4 \times 2 = 8$ |
| Cause 3 (Control valve fail) | 4 (Catastrophic) | C (Occasional) = 3 | $4 \times 3 = 12$ |

### Step 5: Risk Matrix Mapping (Before Safeguards)

$$
R_{Cause\;1} = 4C \rightarrow \text{RED (UNACCEPTABLE)}
$$

$$
R_{Cause\;2} = 4D \rightarrow \text{ORANGE (UNDESIRABLE)}
$$

$$
R_{Cause\;3} = 4C \rightarrow \text{RED (UNACCEPTABLE)}
$$

### Step 6: Recommended Safeguards

| Hazard Cause | Recommended Safeguard | Type | S × P after safeguard |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cause 1 (Blocked outlet) | Pressure Relief Valve (PSV) set at 16 bar + flow indicator in outlet line | Engineering | $4 \times 1 = 4 \rightarrow 4E$ (Green) |
| Cause 2 (External fire) | Bund wall + water spray deluge system + thermal insulation | Engineering | $4 \times 1 = 4 \rightarrow 4E$ (Green) |
| Cause 3 (Control valve fail) | High-pressure interlock + redundant air-to-close solenoid + SIS (SIL 2) | Instrumented | $4 \times 1 = 4 \rightarrow 4E$ (Green) |

### Step 7: Risk Reduction Factor Calculation

For Cause 1:

$$
RRF = \dfrac{R_{initial}}{R_{residual}} = \dfrac{12}{4} = 3
$$

This means the safeguard (PSV + flow indicator) **reduces the risk index by a factor of 3**, taking the hazard from UNACCEPTABLE to ACCEPTABLE.

## 3.3 PHA Worksheet (Standard KTU Format)

The official PHA worksheet is a tabular form with the following columns (memorize for board exam):

| Column | Content |
|---|---|
| 1 | System / Subsystem identifier |
| 2 | Hazard description |
| 3 | Potential cause |
| 4 | Potential consequence (effect) |
| 5 | Severity (S) |
| 6 | Probability (P) |
| 7 | Risk Level (R) |
| 8 | Recommended safeguard |
| 9 | Residual S × P |
| 10 | Action item / Responsibility / Status |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "R = S × P" is qualitative, not quantitative:**
> The equation gives a *relative ranking*, not a true probability. You **cannot** say "Risk is 12 fatalities per year" from this. Quantitative risk assessment (QRA) is a separate, much more complex exercise (FTAs, event trees, failure databases like OREDA).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: PHA Workflow — 8-Step Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: Define System and Boundaries"] --> B["Step 2: Gather Background Data and Codes"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Identify Hazards via Brainstorming"]
    C --> D["Step 4: Determine Root Causes"]
    D --> E["Step 5: Determine Consequences and Effects"]
    E --> F["Step 6: Assign Severity S and Probability P"]
    F --> G["Step 7: Map Risk Index R into Risk Matrix"]
    G --> H["Step 8: Recommend Safeguards and Recalculate R Residual"]
    H --> I{"Is Residual Risk ALARP?"}
    I -- Yes --> J["Document PHA Report and Close Action Items"]
    I -- No --> K["Redesign System and Re-enter Step 3"]
    K --> C
    J --> L["Output: PHA Worksheet and Recommendations"]
```

## Diagram 2: PHA Position in the Safety Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Conceptual Design"] --> P2["FEED Engineering"]
    P2 --> P3["Detailed Engineering"]
    P3 --> P4["Construction and Commissioning"]
    P4 --> P5["Operation and Maintenance"]
    P5 --> P6["Decommissioning"]

    P1 -.->|Triggers| H1["PHA Preliminary Hazard Analysis"]
    P2 -.->|Refines| H1
    P3 -.->|Triggers| H2["HAZOP and FMEA"]
    P3 -.->|Triggers| H3["SIL Classification"]
    P4 -.->|Triggers| H4["Construction Safety Review"]
    P5 -.->|Triggers| H5["Incident Investigation and Audit"]
```

## Diagram 3: Risk Matrix Heat-Map (Visual Aid for Board Answers)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Red["RED ZONE - UNACCEPTABLE - Redesign Mandatory"]
        R1["4A Catastrophic Frequent"]
        R2["4B Catastrophic Probable"]
        R3["3A Critical Frequent"]
    end
    subgraph Orange["ORANGE ZONE - UNDESIRABLE - ALARP Required"]
        O1["4C Catastrophic Occasional"]
        O2["4D Catastrophic Remote"]
        O3["3B Critical Probable"]
        O4["2A Marginal Frequent"]
    end
    subgraph Yellow["YELLOW ZONE - TOLERABLE - Procedural Controls"]
        Y1["4E Catastrophic Improbable"]
        Y2["3C Critical Occasional"]
        Y3["3D Critical Remote"]
        Y4["2B Marginal Probable"]
        Y5["1A Negligible Frequent"]
    end
    subgraph Green["GREEN ZONE - ACCEPTABLE - No Action Needed"]
        G1["3E Critical Improbable"]
        G2["2C Marginal Occasional"]
        G3["2D Marginal Remote"]
        G4["2E Marginal Improbable"]
        G5["1B Negligible Probable"]
        G6["1C Negligible Occasional"]
        G7["1D Negligible Remote"]
        G8["1E Negligible Improbable"]
    end
```

## Diagram 4: Hazard → Cause → Consequence → Safeguard Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1["Hazard: Overpressure in Reactor"] --> C1["Cause: Blocked Outlet"]
    H1 --> C2["Cause: External Fire"]
    H1 --> C3["Cause: Control Valve Failure"]
    C1 --> E1["Consequence: Vessel Rupture"]
    C2 --> E1
    C3 --> E1
    E1 --> S1["Safeguard 1: Pressure Relief Valve"]
    E1 --> S2["Safeguard 2: High Pressure Alarm"]
    E1 --> S3["Safeguard 3: Emergency Shutdown Interlock"]
    S1 --> RR["Residual Risk Reduced to Green Zone"]
    S2 --> RR
    S3 --> RR
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA). List any four objectives of PHA.**

**Model Answer (Board-Standard):**
Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA) is a structured, qualitative, **inductive safety review technique** performed during the early stages of system design to systematically identify, classify, and evaluate potential hazards, their causes, consequences, and recommended safeguards before detailed engineering begins.

**Four Objectives of PHA:**
1. To identify all credible hazards associated with a proposed system at the conceptual design stage.
2. To classify each hazard by severity of consequence and probability of occurrence.
3. To evaluate the associated risk using a qualitative risk matrix (R = S × P).
4. To recommend engineering and procedural safeguards that reduce the residual risk to an ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) level, and to document these recommendations for downstream safety studies such as HAZOP and SIL classification.

*(Word count: 110 — fits 3-mark answer pattern)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Differentiate between PHA and HAZOP. Mention any three differences.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No | Parameter | PHA | HAZOP |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Stage of application** | Performed at conceptual and FEED design stage | Performed at detailed engineering stage when P&IDs are available |
| 2 | **Information needed** | PFDs, MSDS, preliminary layouts | Complete P&IDs, line lists, instrument data sheets |
| 3 | **Technique type** | Inductive — starts from cause to consequence | Deductive-inductive — uses guide words (NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE) on deviations |
| 4 | **Granularity** | High-level, broad hazard identification | Node-by-node, line-by-line detailed review |
| 5 | **Team size** | Smaller team (3–5 experts) | Larger multidisciplinary team (6–10) |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students write generic definitions of PHA without mentioning **early-stage / conceptual** application — examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 2. Confusing **Severity** with **Probability** while filling the risk matrix is a classic error costing 2 marks.
> 3. Forgetting to **recompute residual risk** after recommending safeguards = 2 marks lost.
> 4. Failing to mention **ALARP** while justifying safeguards = 1 mark lost.
> 5. Writing HAZOP guide words in a PHA question (cross-technique contamination) = 2 marks lost.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail the step-by-step procedure for conducting a Preliminary Hazard Analysis. Mention the typical worksheet format used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flammable solvent storage tank operates at atmospheric pressure and ambient temperature. Identify any four credible hazards, their causes, consequences, severity, probability, and recommend appropriate safeguards. Use the standard 4×5 risk matrix. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PHA Procedure (7 marks):**
1. **Define system & boundaries** (1 mark): Establish scope, physical limits (battery limits), operating modes.
2. **Gather background data** (1 mark): PFDs, MSDS, codes (OSHA, NFPA, IS), past incident data.
3. **Identify hazards** (1 mark): Multidisciplinary brainstorming using checklists covering mechanical, thermal, chemical, electrical, radiation, human, environmental categories.
4. **Determine causes** (0.5 mark): Initiating event analysis for each hazard.
5. **Determine consequences** (0.5 mark): Worst credible outcome assessment.
6. **Assign severity & probability** (1 mark): Use standardized S (1–4) and P (A–E) scales.
7. **Map to risk matrix** (1 mark): Calculate R = S × P and assign color band (Green/Yellow/Orange/Red).
8. **Recommend safeguards & recompute residual risk** (1 mark): Engineering (PSV, alarm, interlock), administrative (procedures, training), PPE; recalculate R_residual and check ALARP.

**Worksheet Format (included for full 7 marks):** A 10-column table containing: System ID, Hazard, Cause, Consequence, S, P, R (initial), Safeguard, R (residual), Action/Responsibility/Status.

**(b) Solvent Storage Tank PHA (7 marks):**

| # | Hazard | Cause | Consequence | S | P | R | Safeguard | Residual R |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pool fire at tank | Static electricity during filling | Tank wall failure, escalation to adjacent tanks | 4 | C | 4C (Red) | Earth bonding + flame arrestor + foam pourer | 4E (Green) |
| 2 | Toxic vapor release | Manual valve left open | Worker inhalation injury | 3 | C | 3C (Orange) | Vapor recovery system + toxic gas detector | 3E (Green) |
| 3 | Overfill / spillage | Level indicator failure | Slip hazard, environmental contamination | 2 | B | 2B (Orange) | High-level interlock + bund wall (110% capacity) | 2E (Green) |
| 4 | Lightning strike | No lightning protection | Ignition of vapors, tank farm fire | 4 | D | 4D (Orange) | Lightning arrestors + flame arrestors on vents | 4E (Green) |

**Risk Reduction Justification:** All hazards have been reduced from Red/Orange zones to Green zone, satisfying **ALARP** criteria.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the concept of a risk matrix used in PHA. Construct the standard 4×5 risk matrix with severity and probability categories. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) bullet is fitted with a single pressure relief valve. Perform a PHA on the overpressure scenario and discuss whether one PSV is sufficient or whether redundancy is needed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Risk Matrix (7 marks):**
A risk matrix is a **semi-quantitative** tool that combines severity (S) of a hazardous event with its probability of occurrence (P) to produce a relative risk index $R = S \times P$. It allows prioritization of hazards for mitigation.

**Construction (standard 4×5):**

**Severity Categories (S):**
- **S = 4 — Catastrophic**: Death, multiple injuries, loss exceeding ₹10 crore, permanent environmental damage.
- **S = 3 — Critical**: Serious injury, major property damage, significant environmental release.
- **S = 2 — Marginal**: Minor injury, localized property damage.
- **S = 1 — Negligible**: First-aid injury, no significant loss.

**Probability Categories (P):**
- **A — Frequent**: > 1 per year
- **B — Probable**: 0.1 to 1 per year
- **C — Occasional**: 0.01 to 0.1 per year
- **D — Remote**: 0.001 to 0.01 per year
- **E — Improbable**: < 0.001 per year

**Risk Bands (matrix lookup):**
- **Red** (4A, 4B, 4C, 3A, 3B) → Unacceptable
- **Orange** (4D, 3C, 2A, 2B) → Undesirable (ALARP)
- **Yellow** (4E, 3D, 3E, 2C, 1A) → Tolerable with controls
- **Green** (remaining) → Acceptable

**(b) LPG Bullet Overpressure PHA (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Hazard:** Overpressure in LPG bullet due to blocked outlet or external fire (BLEVE precursor).

**Step 2 — Causes:** (i) Blocked discharge line from polymer buildup, (ii) External pool fire heating the LPG above its design temperature, (iii) Pump dead-head with valve closure.

**Step 3 — Consequence:** Catastrophic BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion) → 50 m fireball → multiple fatalities, domino effect on nearby bullets.

**Step 4 — Initial Risk Assessment:**
- Severity S = 4 (Catastrophic)
- Probability (without redundancy) P = C (Occasional, ≈ 0.05/yr)
- Risk Index R = 4 × 3 = 12 → **4C = RED (UNACCEPTABLE)**

**Step 5 — Safeguard Evaluation:**
- **Option 1**: Single PSV (current design). Risk Reduction Factor typically RRF = 2 (PSV failure rate λ ≈ 0.001/yr from OREDA database).
- **Option 2**: Dual PSV (100% capacity each) with isolation valves. RRF = 10.
- **Option 3**: PSV + rupture disc combination. RRF = 20.

**Step 6 — Recommendation:**
A **single PSV is INSUFFICIENT** for an LPG bullet of this size because:
1. The initial risk (4C) is in the RED zone.
2. PSVs are mechanical devices that can fail (λ ≈ 10⁻³/hr).
3. LPG service requires **API 521/520** compliance, which mandates redundancy for catastrophic hazards.
4. The residual risk with a single PSV becomes 4E (Green) but is borderline; defense-in-depth philosophy demands **2 × 100% PSVs** plus a **rupture disc** to achieve acceptable ALARP.

**Conclusion:** Recommend **dual full-capacity PSVs with isolation valves** plus a **flame arrestor on the vent line** and **deluge system for external fire**. The final residual risk becomes 1E (Green), which is fully ALARP-compliant.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep):**

- ✅ PHA stands for **Preliminary Hazard Analysis** — performed at the **conceptual / FEED** stage of a project.
- ✅ PHA is an **inductive (forward)** technique: Cause → Consequence.
- ✅ Standard PHA has **8 procedural steps** (memorize: Define → Gather → Identify → Cause → Consequence → Assess → Map → Safeguard).
- ✅ Risk equation: **R = S × P**, where S ∈ {1, 2, 3, 4} and P ∈ {A, B, C, D, E}.
- ✅ Risk Matrix is **4×5** (Severity rows × Probability columns) producing **4 color bands**: Red (Unacceptable) → Orange (Undesirable, ALARP) → Yellow (Tolerable with controls) → Green (Acceptable).
- ✅ After recommending safeguards, always **recompute residual risk** and verify it lies in the Green or Yellow band (ALARP).
- ✅ PHA **differs from HAZOP**: PHA is high-level and early; HAZOP is detailed and uses **guide words** (NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN).
- ✅ PHA **differs from FMEA**: PHA is qualitative and system-level; FMEA is component-level and identifies failure modes.
- ✅ Standards reference: **MIL-STD-882**, **IEC 61882**, **API RP 14J** (offshore), **OSHA PSM 1910.119** (chemical).
- ✅ PHA worksheet contains **10 standard columns** (System ID, Hazard, Cause, Consequence, S, P, R, Safeguard, Residual R, Action).
- ✅ Safeguard hierarchy (in order of preference): **Inherent safety → Engineering control → Instrumented protective function → Administrative → PPE**.
- ✅ Always conclude a PHA answer with an **ALARP statement**: *"The residual risk is reduced to the ALARP level, hence the system is acceptable for further detailed engineering."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP) - Methodology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT743 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP) - Methodology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)** is a **structured, systematic, and creative team-based risk analysis technique** used to examine a process or operation in detail, section by section, to identify potential process hazards and operability problems that may otherwise have been overlooked during the design phase. The technique was originally developed by the **Chemical Industries Association (CIA)** and the **Imperial Chemical Industries (ICI)** in the United Kingdom in the **1960s**, and is now codified as one of the most widely applied Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) tools in the chemical, petrochemical, oil & gas, and pharmaceutical industries worldwide.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** HAZOP is a qualitative, **multi-disciplinary brainstorming technique** that uses a fixed set of "guide words" combined with process parameters to discover deviations from the design intent.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **driving a long, unfamiliar road at night** with your friends. To be safe, you and your co-passengers decide to verbally call out any *deviation* you notice:

- "**Less** speed than the limit" → Why? Because the road is slippery.
- "**No** light" → Why? Because a streetlamp is fused.
- "**As well as** obstacles" → A stray dog on the road.

Each observation is matched with a **cause** and a **consequence**, and a **safeguard/action** is proposed. This is *exactly* how a HAZOP team examines a chemical plant: they walk through the **Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)**, section by section, and ask "what if things go wrong?"

| Real-World Analogy Component | HAZOP Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| The unfamiliar road | Process plant line / P&ID section |
| The car driver and passengers | Multi-disciplinary HAZOP team |
| Verbal "deviation" callouts | **Guide Words** (NO, MORE, LESS, etc.) |
| Road features (lights, signs) | Process **Parameters** (flow, temp, pressure) |
| Danger events | **Consequences** |
| Horn, brakes, headlights | **Safeguards / Recommendations** |

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU-Vocabulary (Frequently Asked in Board Exams):**
> - **Node:** A specific section of a process (vessel, line, column) where parameters are evaluated.
> - **Design Intent:** The original operational purpose of the node (e.g., "transfer 100 m³/hr of propane from vessel A to B at 8 bar").
> - **Deviation:** A departure from the design intent created by combining a Guide Word with a Parameter (e.g., "NO FLOW").
> - **Cause:** The reason why the deviation may occur (e.g., blocked strainer, pump failure).
> - **Consequence:** The outcome of the deviation (e.g., pump cavitation, downstream starvation).
> - **Safeguard:** Existing protection (relief valve, alarm, interlock).
> - **Recommendation/Action:** Suggested new safeguard to reduce risk.
> - **PHA (Process Hazard Analysis):** The umbrella term; HAZOP is one method under it.

**Standard Metrics Used in HAZOP Risk Ranking:**

$$\text{Risk} = f(\text{Severity}, \text{Likelihood})$$

- **Severity (S):** 1 to 5 scale — **S1 (Minor) → S5 (Catastrophic)**.
- **Likelihood (L):** 1 to 5 scale — **L1 (Rare) → L5 (Frequent)**.
- **Risk Level (R):** $\text{R} = \text{S} \times \text{L}$ — score from **1 to 25**.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners expect you to remember that HAZOP is **qualitative** (and sometimes semi-quantitative when a risk matrix is added). It is **not** a quantitative tool like a Fault Tree.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Heart of HAZOP: Guide Words

The genius of HAZOP lies in its **standardized vocabulary**. The **Guide Words** are applied to **Process Parameters** to generate every conceivable *deviation*. This ensures **completeness** of the review.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Guide Words (Must be memorized for KTU exams):**

| Guide Word | Meaning | Example Deviation (with FLOW) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **NO / NOT** | Complete negation of the design intent | **NO FLOW** |
| **MORE** | Quantitative increase | **MORE FLOW** |
| **LESS** | Quantitative decrease | **LESS FLOW** |
| **REVERSE** | Logical opposite | **REVERSE FLOW** |
| **AS WELL AS** | An additional activity occurs | **AS WELL AS FLOW** (two-phase?) |
| **PART OF** | Only a part of the design intent is achieved | **PART OF FLOW** (composition change) |
| **OTHER THAN** | Complete substitution | **OTHER THAN FLOW** (wrong fluid) |

### 2.2 Standard Process Parameters

The parameters are chosen to represent the design intent of the node.

- **Flow** (rate, quantity)
- **Pressure** (high, low, vacuum)
- **Temperature** (high, low)
- **Level** (in vessels, tanks)
- **Composition / Phase** (single phase, two-phase, contamination)
- **Reaction / Mixing** (in reactors)
- **Time / Sequence** (for batch operations)
- **Service / Utility** (cooling water, steam, instrument air)
- **Erosion, Corrosion, Vibration, Noise, Maintenance, Sampling**

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Methodology (The 7-Phase HAZOP Procedure)

The KTU syllabus expects a clear procedural walk-through. The HAZOP study typically proceeds as follows:

**Phase 1 — Define Scope and Objectives:**
Clearly state the boundaries of the study. Which P&IDs? Which section of the plant? What is the start-up operating mode (commissioning, normal, shutdown, emergency)?

**Phase 2 — Assemble the Multi-Disciplinary Team:**
A typical HAZOP team is **5 to 8 members** and must be cross-functional. The HAZOP study cannot be done by a single individual.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard HAZOP Team Composition (High-Yield for KTU):**
> - **Facilitator / Chairman:** Leads the meeting, ensures guide-word discipline.
> - **Scribe / Secretary:** Records findings on the HAZOP worksheet.
> - **Process Engineer:** Explains the design intent.
> - **Operations / Plant Operator:** Provides practical field experience.
> - **Instrument / Control Engineer:** Explains control loops, interlocks.
> - **Safety Engineer:** Assesses risk, ranks severity/likelihood.
> - **Maintenance Engineer:** Highlights known mechanical issues.

**Phase 3 — Gather Information (Documentation Review):**
The team must review: **P&IDs**, PFDs, cause & effect matrices, operating procedures, equipment data sheets, HAZOP worksheets from previous phases, MSDS, and control philosophy.

**Phase 4 — Divide the Plant into Nodes (Node Definition):**
A **node** is a manageable process section such as a single pipeline, a vessel, a heat exchanger, a reactor, or a column section. The boundary is defined by *start and end points* (e.g., from pump discharge to a control valve inlet).

**Phase 5 — Apply Guide Words and Identify Deviations:**
For every node, the team systematically applies each guide word to each parameter. The result is a **Deviation** (e.g., "No Flow", "High Pressure").

**Phase 6 — Determine Causes, Consequences, and Safeguards:**
For every deviation, the team asks:
- **What is the CAUSE?** (e.g., pump trip, valve closed)
- **What is the CONSEQUENCE?** (e.g., overpressure, overheating)
- **What SAFEGUARDS exist?** (e.g., relief valve, low-flow alarm, interlock)
- **What are the ACTIONS / RECOMMENDATIONS?** (e.g., install a high-high temperature interlock)

**Phase 7 — Documentation, Follow-up, and Close-out:**
The scribe records all findings in a HAZOP worksheet. A **HAZOP Action Tracking Register** is maintained to ensure every recommendation is assigned a responsible person and a deadline.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Risk Score | $R = S \times L$ | Dimensionless; **R ranges 1 to 25** |
| Severity Categories (Typical 5-tier) | $S \in \lbrace 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 \rbrace$ | $S5$ Catastrophic, $S1$ Minor |
| Likelihood Categories (Typical 5-tier) | $L \in \lbrace 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 \rbrace$ | $L5$ Frequent, $L1$ Rare |
| Acceptable Risk Threshold | $R \le 6$ (typical) | $R \ge 15$ → **Mandatory Action** |
| Standard HAZOP Guide Words | $GW = \lbrace NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN \rbrace$ | **Memorize the set of 7** |
| Node Definition | Node = physical section bounded by clear design intent | 1 deviation per (Guide Word $\times$ Parameter) |
| Team Size | $N_{team} = 5 \text{ to } 8$ | **Less than 3 = invalid HAZOP** |
| Document Set | P&ID, PFD, Cause & Effect, MSDS, Operating Procedure | All must be **current revision** |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"What is the role of the facilitator?"* or *"List the guide words and give one example deviation each."* Use the table above for crisp answers.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

HAZOP is the **gold standard** for Process Hazard Analysis in the **chemical, petrochemical, oil & gas, nuclear, and pharmaceutical** industries. It is mandated by regulations such as:
- **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** (Process Safety Management of Highly Hazardous Chemicals) in the USA.
- **COMAH (Control of Major Accident Hazards) Regulations** in the UK/EU.
- **Indian Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, 1989** (as amended).

> [!WARNING]
> HAZOP is **NOT** a substitute for quantitative methods like Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) or Event Tree Analysis (ETA). It is **complementary** — HAZOP identifies *what* can go wrong; FTA/ETA quantify *how often*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Constructing a HAZOP Worksheet — Worked Example

We will derive the structure of a HAZOP worksheet by analyzing a small but realistic process node.

> [!NOTE]
> **Process Description:** A centrifugal pump **P-101** transfers water from a storage tank **TK-100** to a header through a manual isolation valve **MOV-101**. The line has a flow indicator **FI-101** and a pressure transmitter **PT-101**. The pump has a discharge check valve **CV-101**.
> 
> **Node 1:** Suction line from TK-100 to Pump P-101 inlet.
> **Node 2:** Pump P-101 itself.
> **Node 3:** Discharge line from P-101 to MOV-101.

**Step 1 — Apply the Formula for Risk:**

$$\text{Risk Score} = S \times L$$

**Step 2 — Define a Typical Severity Scale (industry standard):**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_1 & : \text{Minor injury / nuisance (e.g., small spill, no injury)} \\
S_2 & : \text{Recordable injury / small environmental release} \\
S_3 & : \text{Serious injury / significant release} \\
S_4 & : \text{Major injury / offsite impact potential} \\
S_5 & : \text{Fatalities / catastrophic release} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Define a Typical Likelihood Scale:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_1 & : \text{Very rare (less than } 10^{-4} \text{ per year)} \\
L_2 & : \text{Rare (between } 10^{-3} \text{ and } 10^{-4} \text{ per year)} \\
L_3 & : \text{Occasional (between } 10^{-2} \text{ and } 10^{-3} \text{ per year)} \\
L_4 & : \text{Likely (between } 10^{-1} \text{ and } 10^{-2} \text{ per year)} \\
L_5 & : \text{Frequent (more than } 10^{-1} \text{ per year)} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply HAZOP to Node 3 (Discharge line, P-101 → MOV-101):**

We choose the deviation **"NO FLOW"** as our worked derivation example.

**Step 4a — Deviation Construction:**

$$\text{Deviation} = \text{Guide Word (NO)} \times \text{Parameter (FLOW)} = \text{NO FLOW}$$

**Step 4b — Identify Causes:**
A cause is a *definite, single, identifiable event* that produces the deviation. The team brainstorms:

$$\text{Cause List} = \lbrace \text{Pump trip}, \text{MOV-101 closed}, \text{CV-101 stuck closed}, \text{Line rupture} \rbrace$$

**Step 4c — Identify Consequences:**
The consequence is the *outcome* if the deviation occurs and is not stopped by safeguards.

$$\text{Consequence} = \text{Pump dry-run} \rightarrow \text{Seal failure} \rightarrow \text{Fire risk}$$

**Step 4d — Identify Existing Safeguards:**

$$\text{Safeguards} = \lbrace \text{Low-flow alarm on FI-101}, \text{Low-low flow pump trip interlock} \rbrace$$

**Step 4e — Compute Risk and Recommend Action:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Without interlock:} \quad R_{old} &= S_4 \times L_3 = 12 \;\; (\text{Action Required}) \\
\text{With interlock:}\quad R_{new} &= S_4 \times L_1 = 4 \;\; (\text{Acceptable}) \\
\end{aligned}$$

This is the *typical analytical logic* KTU examiners want to see — **deviation → cause → consequence → safeguard → risk score → recommendation**.

### 3.2 Exhaustive HAZOP Worksheet (Sample of Node 3)

| # | Guide Word | Parameter | Deviation | Cause | Consequence | Safeguard | S | L | R | Recommendation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | NO | Flow | NO FLOW | Pump trip | Pump damage, process stop | LL flow trip | 4 | 2 | 8 | Add pump current trip |
| 2 | MORE | Flow | MORE FLOW | FCV failure open | Line erosion, vibration | HI flow alarm | 3 | 3 | 9 | Add HI-HI flow trip |
| 3 | LESS | Flow | LESS FLOW | Strainer block | Reduced output | LO flow alarm | 2 | 4 | 8 | Schedule cleaning SOP |
| 4 | REVERSE | Flow | REVERSE FLOW | Check valve fails | Backflow contamination | CV-101 check | 3 | 2 | 6 | None |
| 5 | MORE | Pressure | HIGH PRESSURE | Downstream block | Line rupture | PSV-101 relief | 5 | 2 | 10 | Re-rate PSV to 110% |
| 6 | LESS | Pressure | LOW PRESSURE | Pump cavitation | Seal damage | PI local gauge | 2 | 3 | 6 | None |
| 7 | AS WELL AS | Flow | TWO PHASE FLOW | Air ingress | Pump vibration | Vacuum vent | 3 | 3 | 9 | Add NRV on suction |
| 8 | OTHER THAN | Composition | WRONG FLUID | Operator error | Process contamination | Line ID tags | 4 | 2 | 8 | Add color-coded tags |

> [!TIP]
> **Matrix Logic for KTU:** Note how we paired **each of the 7 guide words** with **each relevant parameter**. This is the *completeness* principle of HAZOP. A node is **NOT** fully reviewed if even one valid (guide-word $\times$ parameter) pair is skipped.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 HAZOP Methodology Flowchart (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The diagram below maps the **functional architecture** of the HAZOP process from project inception to action close-out.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Project Kick-off]) --> A1[Define Scope and Objectives]
    A1 --> A2[Assemble Multi-Disciplinary Team]
    A2 --> A3[Gather Documentation P&ID PFD MSDS]
    A3 --> A4[Divide Plant into Nodes]
    A4 --> A5[Select First Node]
    A5 --> A6[Define Design Intent of Node]
    A6 --> A7[Apply Guide Word to Parameter]
    A7 --> A8[Generate Deviation]
    A8 --> A9[Identify Causes]
    A9 --> A10[Identify Consequences]
    A10 --> A11[Check Existing Safeguards]
    A11 --> A12{Risk Acceptable?}
    A12 -->|No| A13[Issue Recommendation]
    A12 -->|Yes| A14[Record in Worksheet]
    A13 --> A14
    A14 --> A15{More Guide Word Parameter Pairs?}
    A15 -->|Yes| A7
    A15 -->|No| A16{More Nodes?}
    A16 -->|Yes| A5
    A16 -->|No| A17[Issue HAZOP Report]
    A17 --> A18[Action Tracking Register]
    A18 --> A19([Study Close-out and Verification])
```

### 4.2 Cause–Consequence–Safeguard–Recommendation Logic Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DEVI[Deviation e.g. NO FLOW] --> CAUSE[Cause e.g. Pump Trip]
    CAUSE --> CONSE[Consequence e.g. Pump Damage]
    CONSE --> SAFE[Existing Safeguard e.g. LL Flow Trip]
    SAFE --> RISK{Risk S x L}
    RISK -->|High| REC[New Recommendation e.g. Current Trip]
    RISK -->|Low| CLOSE[Close Item]
    REC --> DOC[HAZOP Worksheet]
    SAFE --> DOC
    CLOSE --> DOC
```

### 4.3 HAZOP Team Interaction Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FAC[Facilitator Chairman] --- SCR[Scribe Secretary]
    FAC --- PE[Process Engineer]
    FAC --- OP[Operations Specialist]
    FAC --- IE[Instrument Engineer]
    FAC --- SE[Safety Engineer]
    FAC --- ME[Maintenance Engineer]
    SCR -. Records .-> HAZOP_DOC[HAZOP Worksheet]
    PE -. Design Intent .-> FAC
    OP -. Field Reality .-> FAC
    IE -. Loop and Interlock Info .-> FAC
    SE -. Severity Likelihood .-> FAC
    ME -. Equipment History .-> FAC
```

### 4.4 Guide Word × Parameter Matrix (Visual Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph G1[Guide Words]
        GWN[NO]
        GWM[MORE]
        GWL[LESS]
        GWR[REVERSE]
        GWA[AS WELL AS]
        GWP[PART OF]
        GWO[OTHER THAN]
    end
    subgraph P1[Parameters]
        PF[Flow]
        PP[Pressure]
        PT[Temperature]
        PL[Level]
        PC[Composition]
    end
    GWN --- PF
    GWN --- PP
    GWM --- PF
    GWL --- PT
    GWR --- PC
    GWA --- PL
    GWP --- PC
    GWO --- PF
```

> [!NOTE]
> The four Mermaid diagrams above satisfy the **KTU 2024 visual literacy expectation** for Module 2. They are intentionally rendered as **block-level functional architectures** (per KTU protocol) to avoid ambiguity in physical representation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
*Define HAZOP. List any four standard guide words used in HAZOP study with one example deviation each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- HAZOP is a structured, systematic, team-based hazard analysis technique used to identify potential hazards and operability problems in a process plant by comparing the design intent with deviations generated from guide words applied to process parameters. **(1 mark)**
- Four standard guide words with deviations: **(2 marks, 0.5 each)**

| Guide Word | Deviation (with FLOW) |
| :--- | :--- |
| NO / NOT | NO FLOW |
| MORE | MORE FLOW |
| LESS | LESS FLOW |
| REVERSE | REVERSE FLOW |
| AS WELL AS | SLUG FLOW / TWO-PHASE FLOW |
| PART OF | PART OF FLOW (composition change) |
| OTHER THAN | WRONG FLUID FLOW |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**
*State the typical composition of a HAZOP team. Why is a HAZOP study never done by a single person?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A typical HAZOP team consists of **5 to 8 members** including: Facilitator/Chairman, Scribe, Process Engineer, Operations/Plant Operator, Instrument/Control Engineer, Safety Engineer, and Maintenance Engineer. **(2 marks)**
- HAZOP requires **diverse expertise** — no single person can possess complete knowledge of process design, operations, instrumentation, and safety. The **brainstorming principle** and the **multi-disciplinary cross-check** are essential to uncover all deviations, causes, and consequences. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Exam) convention, Module 2 questions may appear with internal choice. **Attempt any ONE** of Question A or Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 (Model Paper)]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **step-by-step methodology of conducting a HAZOP study**. Mention the role of each team member. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A centrifugal pump transfers hexane from a storage tank to a reactor through a flow control valve (FCV) and a magnetic flow indicator (FI). The line has a pressure safety valve (PSV) set at 6 bar(g). The pump discharge pressure is 5 bar(g). Apply the HAZOP technique to identify at least **six deviations** for this node, listing their causes, consequences, safeguards, and risk scores (using $R = S \times L$). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer — Question A:**

**(a) HAZOP Methodology (7 marks):**

1. **Define scope and objectives (0.5 mark):** Boundaries of the study, operating modes, applicable P&IDs.
2. **Assemble multi-disciplinary team (1 mark):** Facilitator, Scribe, Process Engineer, Operator, Instrument Engineer, Safety Engineer, Maintenance Engineer.
3. **Documentation review (0.5 mark):** P&IDs, PFDs, Cause & Effect, MSDS, Operating Procedures, equipment data sheets.
4. **Node definition (1 mark):** Divide the plant into manageable sections (vessel, line, column).
5. **Define design intent (0.5 mark):** State clearly what the node is supposed to do (e.g., "transfer 50 m³/hr hexane at 5 bar").
6. **Apply guide words systematically (2 marks):** For each parameter, apply each guide word to generate deviations (NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN).
7. **Identify causes, consequences, safeguards, and recommendations (1 mark):** For every deviation, complete the worksheet entries and compute risk.
8. **Documentation, action tracking, and close-out (0.5 mark):** Final HAZOP report and action register with assigned owners and deadlines.

**(b) HAZOP Application on Pump–Reactor Line (7 marks):**

| # | Deviation | Cause | Consequence | Safeguard | S | L | R |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | NO FLOW | Pump trip | Pump dry-run, fire | LL flow trip | 4 | 2 | 8 |
| 2 | MORE FLOW | FCV fails open | Line erosion, hexane spill | HI flow alarm | 3 | 3 | 9 |
| 3 | LESS FLOW | Strainer block | Reduced production | LO flow alarm | 2 | 4 | 8 |
| 4 | HIGH PRESSURE | FCV closes downstream | Line rupture | PSV at 6 bar | 5 | 2 | 10 |
| 5 | LOW PRESSURE | Suction vapour | Pump cavitation | Vacuum vent | 2 | 3 | 6 |
| 6 | REVERSE FLOW | Downstream check fails | Hexane backflow | Internal check valve | 3 | 2 | 6 |
| 7 | AS WELL AS FLOW | Two-phase flow | Pump vibration | Suction NRV | 3 | 3 | 9 |

(6 deviations = 6 marks + summary risk comment = 1 mark)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **guide words** in HAZOP. List all standard guide words with their meaning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **HAZOP and FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)**. When is HAZOP preferred over FMEA? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer — Question B:**

**(a) Guide Words (7 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Guide words are simple, predefined words used in HAZOP to interrogate a process design intent and generate *deviations* when combined with a *parameter*.
- **Purpose (1 mark):** They ensure **completeness, consistency, and creativity** of the review. They act as a checklist to force the team to consider every possible way the design intent may not be achieved.
- **Standard 7 Guide Words (5 marks, ~0.7 each):**

| Guide Word | Meaning | Example Deviation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **NO / NOT** | Complete negation | NO FLOW |
| **MORE** | Quantitative increase | HIGH TEMPERATURE |
| **LESS** | Quantitative decrease | LOW LEVEL |
| **REVERSE** | Logical opposite | REVERSE FLOW |
| **AS WELL AS** | Additional activity | TWO-PHASE FLOW |
| **PART OF** | Only a portion achieved | PART OF FLOW (composition) |
| **OTHER THAN** | Complete substitution | WRONG CHEMICAL |

---

**(b) HAZOP vs FMEA (7 marks):**

| Basis | HAZOP | FMEA |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Primary focus (1 mark)** | Process deviations in continuous flow systems | Equipment / component failure modes |
| **Method (1 mark)** | Guide Words × Parameters | Failure Mode × Component |
| **Team (1 mark)** | Multi-disciplinary (process, ops, safety) | Often design / reliability engineers |
| **Application (1 mark)** | Chemical, petrochemical, nuclear processes | Mechanical, electrical, automotive |
| **Output (1 mark)** | Deviations, causes, consequences, recommendations | Failure modes, RPN = S × O × D |
| **When preferred (2 marks)** | When the process is **flow-based, continuous, and chemically hazardous**; when deviations from design intent are the primary concern; when **regulatory mandates** (OSHA PSM, COMAH) require a process-safety review. FMEA is preferred for **discrete components and non-flow systems** (e.g., electronic circuits, mechanical assemblies). |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons Students Lose Marks in HAZOP Questions:**
> 1. **Listing guide words without an example deviation** — always pair the guide word with a parameter (e.g., "NO FLOW", not just "NO").
> 2. **Confusing HAZOP with FMEA** — examiners explicitly test this distinction. Remember: HAZOP is **process-based**; FMEA is **equipment-based**.
> 3. **Writing "HAZOP is quantitative"** — it is **qualitative** (or semi-quantitative at best).
> 4. **Forgetting the role of the Scribe** — the Scribe is critical for documentation; missing this loses 0.5 mark.
> 5. **Not stating the risk formula** $R = S \times L$ — examiners often demand this explicitly.
> 6. **Mixing up "More Pressure" vs "High Pressure"** — always use the *guide word + parameter* format strictly.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **HAZOP = Hazard and Operability Study** — a **qualitative, structured, team-based** PHA technique.
- **Developed by ICI, UK, in the 1960s** — used in process industries worldwide.
- **7 Standard Guide Words:** **N**o, **M**ore, **L**ess, **R**everse, **A**s Well As, **P**art Of, **O**ther Than.
- **Standard Parameters:** Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition, Phase, Service.
- **Deviation Formula:** $\text{Deviation} = \text{Guide Word} \times \text{Parameter}$.
- **Risk Formula:** $\text{Risk} = S \times L$; typical thresholds: $R \ge 15$ = must act; $R \le 6$ = acceptable.
- **Team Size:** **5 to 8 members** — facilitator, scribe, process engineer, operator, instrument engineer, safety engineer, maintenance engineer.
- **Phases:** Define scope → Assemble team → Gather docs → Define nodes → Define design intent → Apply guide words → Identify causes/consequences/safeguards → Recommend → Document.
- **Output:** HAZOP Worksheet + HAZOP Report + Action Tracking Register.
- **HAZOP ≠ FMEA** — process deviations vs component failures.
- **HAZOP is NOT quantitative** — no fault-tree math involved.
- **Mandatory in OSHA PSM and Indian MSIHC Rules** for hazardous chemical plants.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Criticality Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT743 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Criticality Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Criticality Analysis (CA)** is a systematic, structured, and prioritized engineering methodology used in **Process Safety Management (PSM)** and **Loss Prevention Engineering** to evaluate, classify, and rank the relative significance of identified failure modes, hazards, or unwanted events in an industrial system based on the combination of their **consequence severity** and **frequency of occurrence**.

The primary output of a Criticality Analysis is a numerical or categorical **Criticality Rating (Cr)** or **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** that allows safety engineers, plant managers, and process safety professionals to allocate finite safety resources (budget, engineering hours, instrumentation upgrades) to the *most* dangerous failure scenarios first.

> [!NOTE] **Core Distinction — FMEA vs Criticality Analysis**
> - **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** focuses on *identifying* failure modes and their *local effects* on subsystems.
> - **Criticality Analysis** goes one step further — it *quantifies* the *significance* of each failure mode using numerical scales and ranks them to drive decision-making.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a **hospital emergency-room triage doctor**. A hundred patients walk in simultaneously — some have mild colds, some have broken bones, and a few are having heart attacks. You do not have the luxury of treating everyone in the order they arrived. You must **rank** them by the *combination* of:
- **How bad is the injury?** (Severity)
- **How frequently does that injury occur?** (Probability)
- **How easily can you detect it before it becomes critical?** (Detectability)

You treat heart-attack patients first — not because they are most common, but because their *combination* of severity + probability makes them the most *critical*.

**Criticality Analysis is exactly this triage — but for industrial plant components** such as pressure vessels, control valves, sensors, and reactors. A small leak in a high-pressure hydrogen line is more critical than a noisy pump, even if the pump fails more often, because the *consequence* of the hydrogen leak is catastrophic.

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE] **Bolded Key Terms (Examinable Vocabulary)**
> - **Severity (S):** The consequence magnitude of a failure mode, rated typically on a scale of **1 to 10**.
> - **Occurrence (O) / Probability (P):** The likelihood that the failure mode will actually happen, rated on a scale of **1 to 10** or expressed as a failure rate **λ (failures per 10⁶ hours)**.
> - **Detection (D):** The difficulty of detecting the failure *before* it reaches the end-user/plant, rated on a scale of **1 to 10** (where 10 = impossible to detect).
> - **Risk Priority Number (RPN):** A composite numerical score = S × O × D, ranging from **1 to 1000**.
> - **Criticality (Cr):** The product of severity, failure mode ratio, conditional probability, and system failure rate.
> - **SIL (Safety Integrity Level):** Categorical risk-reduction level from **SIL 1 to SIL 4** per **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511** standards.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Board Tip**
> Examiners frequently test the **difference between Severity, Occurrence, and Detection**. Memorize that **Detection = 10 means "no current control can catch this failure"** — it is the most dangerous category.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The "Why" Behind Criticality Analysis

Industrial plants contain thousands of components. A complete safety redesign of *every* component is economically and physically impossible. Criticality Analysis solves this by answering four fundamental engineering questions:

1. **What can go wrong?** (Failure Mode Identification)
2. **How bad is it if it goes wrong?** (Severity Assessment)
3. **How often does it go wrong?** (Occurrence / Probability Assessment)
4. **Can we detect it before disaster?** (Detectability Assessment)

By multiplying or matrix-mapping the answers, we obtain a **single composite number (RPN or Cr)** that drives engineering priority.

---

### 2.2 The Two Major Quantitative Approaches

#### Approach 1: Risk Priority Number (RPN) — Used in FMEA

This is the most widely taught method in KTU 2024 scheme courses. The RPN is calculated as:

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

Where:
- $S$ = Severity rating (1–10)
- $O$ = Occurrence rating (1–10)
- $D$ = Detection rating (1–10)

The **maximum RPN is 10 × 10 × 10 = 1000** and the **minimum is 1 × 1 × 1 = 1**.

#### Approach 2: Criticality Rating (Cr) — Used in FMECA (Failure Mode, Effects, and Criticality Analysis)

Used in aerospace, nuclear, and military standards. The formula is:

$$
C_r = S \times \alpha \times \beta \times \lambda_p \times t
$$

Where:
- $S$ = Severity category (loss magnitude in monetary or hazard units)
- $\alpha$ = Failure mode ratio (fraction of failures of the item that result in this specific mode)
- $\beta$ = Conditional probability of mission loss given this failure mode (0 to 1)
- $\lambda_p$ = Component failure rate (failures per hour or per 10⁶ hours)
- $t$ = Mission duration or operating period (hours)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why Two Formulas?**
> - **RPN** is *qualitative-ranking* — used when exact failure rates are unavailable.
> - **Cr** is *quantitative-absolute* — used when reliability data is available from databases (e.g., OREDA, MIL-HDBK-217, NPRD).

---

### 2.3 Categorical (Qualitative) Criticality Matrix

When a pure numerical scale is impractical (e.g., during HAZOP studies or initial design), engineers use a **Severity × Frequency Matrix**:

| | **Low Severity** | **Medium Severity** | **High Severity** | **Catastrophic Severity** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Frequent** | Medium | High | Critical | Critical |
| **Probable** | Low | Medium | High | Critical |
| **Occasional** | Low | Medium | High | Critical |
| **Remote** | Negligible | Low | Medium | High |
| **Improbable** | Negligible | Negligible | Low | Medium |

> [!WARNING] **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse "Criticality" with "Severity." Severity is *one dimension* of criticality. Criticality = Severity × Probability. **A frequent minor leak can be more critical than a rare explosion** if measured purely by RPN — but the matrix above forces engineers to think in *two* dimensions.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE] **All formulas below are exhaustively examinable under Module 2 of PEEIT743.**

| **Formula Name** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Variables Explained** | **Typical Range / Unit** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Risk Priority Number | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | $S$ = Severity, $O$ = Occurrence, $D$ = Detection | 1 to 1000 | FMEA-based ranking |
| Criticality Rating | $C_r = S \times \alpha \times \beta \times \lambda_p \times t$ | $S$ = Severity cost, $\alpha$ = mode ratio, $\beta$ = conditional prob., $\lambda_p$ = failure rate, $t$ = time | 0 to mission loss | FMECA aerospace/nuclear |
| Failure Mode Ratio | $\alpha_i = \dfrac{\lambda_i}{\lambda_{\text{total}}}$ | $\lambda_i$ = failure rate of mode $i$ | 0 to 1 | Allocation of failures |
| Conditional Probability Sum | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i = 1$ | Sum of all mode ratios for one component | Equals 1 | Consistency check |
| System Failure Rate | $\lambda_{\text{sys}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ | Series system sum of failure rates | Failures / hour | Reliability block diagrams |
| Mean Time Between Failures | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\lambda$ = failure rate | Hours | Reliability engineering |
| Hazard Rate (Weibull) | $h(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta} \left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | $\beta$ = shape, $\eta$ = scale | 1/time | Lifetime modeling |
| Probability of Failure on Demand | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \lambda_{DU} \times T_i$ | $\lambda_{DU}$ = dangerous undetected failure rate, $T_i$ = proof test interval | 10⁻² to 10⁻⁵ | SIL calculations |
| Safety Integrity Level | $\text{SIL} = -\log_{10}(\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}})$ | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}$ = avg. probability of failure | SIL 1 to SIL 4 | IEC 61508 compliance |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Criticality Analysis is **mandatory** in the following standards and industries:

- **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511** — Functional Safety of E/E/PE systems (process industry)
- **ISO 12100** — Machinery Safety Risk Assessment
- **ISO 26262** — Automotive Functional Safety
- **DO-178C / DO-254** — Avionics Software/Hardware Criticality
- **MIL-STD-882** — System Safety (US Department of Defense)
- **API RP 14C** — Offshore Platform Safety Analysis
- **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** — Process Safety Management (PSM)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Industrial Use Case:** In a **LNG (Liquefied Natural Gas) terminal**, Criticality Analysis is used to rank the risk of cryogenic pipe rupture, gas detection system failure, and ESD (Emergency Shutdown) valve non-closure. The components with the highest Cr values are mandated to have **redundant SIL-3 instrumentation**.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Criticality Rating Formula

The Criticality Rating $C_r$ is derived from **first principles of reliability engineering** and the **law of total probability**. Below is the full step-by-step derivation that a KTU board examiner may award full marks for.

---

#### **Derivation 1: Criticality Rating (Cr) from Failure Rate Decomposition**

**Step 1 — Start with the total failure rate of a component.**

Let $\lambda_p$ be the *total* failure rate of a component (e.g., a control valve) in failures per million hours. This is obtained from field failure databases such as **OREDA** or **NPRD**.

**Step 2 — Decompose the total failure rate into individual failure modes.**

Each component can fail in multiple ways. For a valve, these modes may include: *fails to open on demand*, *fails to close on demand*, *passes leakage*, *stem breakage*, *actuator failure*, etc. The total failure rate is the sum:

$$
\lambda_p = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i
$$

where $\lambda_i$ is the failure rate of mode $i$.

**Step 3 — Define the failure mode ratio $\alpha_i$.**

The fraction of all failures that result in mode $i$ is:

$$
\alpha_i = \frac{\lambda_i}{\lambda_p}
$$

By construction:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i = 1
$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the conditional probability $\beta_i$.**

Not every occurrence of failure mode $i$ leads to mission loss or hazardous consequence. The conditional probability $\beta_i$ represents the probability that mode $i$ *causes the hazardous event* (e.g., loss of containment, fire, explosion). The value of $\beta_i$ lies between **0 and 1**.

**Step 5 — Define severity category $S_i$.**

The severity category quantifies the consequence magnitude. It is often expressed in monetary loss (USD) or as a categorical index (Catastrophic = 4, Critical = 3, Marginal = 2, Minor = 1).

**Step 6 — Define mission time $t$.**

The operating period over which the component is expected to perform its safety function, typically 1 year, 10 years, or 40 years depending on the plant.

**Step 7 — Combine all factors.**

The expected loss (or criticality contribution) from failure mode $i$ over mission time $t$ is the product of the *frequency of the failure*, the *probability it leads to a hazardous event*, the *severity*, and the *time exposed*:

$$
C_{r,i} = S_i \times \alpha_i \times \beta_i \times \lambda_p \times t
$$

This is the **Criticality Rating for failure mode $i$**.

**Step 8 — Total criticality for the component.**

The total criticality of the component (summing over all modes) is:

$$
C_{r,\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_{r,i}
$$

**Step 9 — Final compact form.**

For a single dominant failure mode, the simplified expression is:

$$
\boxed{C_r = S \times \alpha \times \beta \times \lambda_p \times t}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Conversion Logic Summary:**
> - $\lambda_p \times t$ = expected number of failures during mission time (dimensionless).
> - $\alpha \times \beta$ = probability the failure mode results in hazardous consequence (dimensionless).
> - $S$ = consequence magnitude (USD or category).
> - Therefore $C_r$ has units of *consequence magnitude per mission* (e.g., USD per mission).

---

#### **Derivation 2: Relationship between RPN and Cr**

**Step 1 — Start with RPN definition.**

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that Occurrence rating $O$ is a *qualitative* log-scale representation of failure rate.**

The Occurrence rating maps the failure rate to a discrete 1–10 scale (per industry standards such as the AIAG FMEA手册). For example:
- $O = 1$ corresponds to $\lambda \approx 10^{-6}$ failures/hour
- $O = 10$ corresponds to $\lambda \geq 10^{-1}$ failures/hour

**Step 3 — Recognize that Detection rating $D$ is qualitatively related to the conditional probability $\beta$ of hazardous outcome and the test interval.**

A high $D$ means low detection capability, which approximately corresponds to a high $\beta$ in the Cr formula.

**Step 4 — Conclude the qualitative equivalence.**

$$
\text{RPN}_{\text{qualitative}} \;\longleftrightarrow\; C_{r,\text{quantitative}}
$$

**Both measure the same engineering concept — "how critical is this failure?" — but RPN is on a discrete 1–1000 scale and Cr is on a continuous scale tied to actual failure rates.**

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Insight:** Modern safety standards (post-2019 AIAG-VDA FMEA) have started **moving away from RPN** because it is sensitive to scale selection. The trend is to use the **Action Priority (AP)** approach — High / Medium / Low — but KTU 2024 syllabus still tests the classical RPN formula.

---

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Board Question)

**Problem:** A pressure transmitter in a chemical reactor has the following FMEA data:

- Severity rating $S = 9$ (catastrophic — overpressure event)
- Occurrence rating $O = 5$ (occasional failure)
- Detection rating $D = 6$ (moderate detection difficulty)

**Step 1 — Calculate the RPN.**

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D = 9 \times 5 \times 6
$$

$$
\text{RPN} = 270
$$

**Step 2 — Interpret the result.**

An RPN of **270** is in the **upper quartile** of the 1–1000 scale. This places the pressure transmitter in the **High Priority** band, requiring immediate engineering action — typically installation of a **redundant SIL-2 transmitter** with **diagnostic coverage ≥ 90%**.

**Step 3 — Recommend mitigation.**

- Install a **1oo2 (one-out-of-two) voted redundant transmitter pair**.
- Increase proof-test interval $T_i$ to detect dangerous failures.
- Reduce Detection rating from 6 to 3 by adding **continuous diagnostics**.

**Step 4 — Recalculate improved RPN.**

$$
\text{RPN}_{\text{new}} = 9 \times 5 \times 3 = 135
$$

**Step 5 — Quantify risk reduction.**

$$
\text{Risk Reduction} = \frac{270 - 135}{270} \times 100\% = 50\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Conclusion:** A **50% risk reduction** is achieved solely by improving detection capability — without any change in hardware failure rate. This demonstrates why the Detection dimension is so heavily weighted in modern SIL calculations.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Component-wise Criticality Comparison

> [!NOTE] **This type of table is highly examinable — examiners often ask students to "rank" components or "identify the most critical."**

| **Component** | **Failure Mode** | **S** | **O** | **D** | **RPN** | **Rank** | **Required Action** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure Safety Valve | Fails to open on overpressure | 10 | 3 | 4 | **120** | 3 | Increase proof test frequency |
| Level Transmitter | Drifts out of calibration | 8 | 6 | 7 | **336** | **1 (Critical)** | Install redundant transmitter |
| Control Valve | Fails to close on demand | 9 | 4 | 5 | **180** | 2 | Add SIL-3 ESD function |
| Temperature Sensor | Slow response | 6 | 5 | 6 | **180** | 2 | Replace with faster RTD |
| Flow Indicator | Sticking at zero | 5 | 7 | 8 | **280** | 1 (Critical) | Add diagnostics + bypass |

> [!WARNING] **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often rank by RPN alone and forget that **any RPN > 200 with Severity ≥ 9** is **automatically High Priority** regardless of the other two factors. This is a *threshold-based override rule* in the AIAG FMEA standard.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Criticality Analysis

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional flow** of how a Criticality Analysis is conducted within an industrial safety lifecycle. Each block represents a discrete analytical stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define System Boundary] --> B[Identify Components Subsystems]
    B --> C[List Failure Modes per Component]
    C --> D[Assign Severity Score S 1 to 10]
    C --> E[Assign Occurrence Score O 1 to 10]
    C --> F[Assign Detection Score D 1 to 10]
    D --> G[Compute RPN S times O times D]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{RPN Exceeds Threshold 200}
    H -->|Yes| I[Mark as High Priority]
    H -->|No| J[Mark as Acceptable]
    I --> K[Recommend Mitigation Actions]
    K --> L[Recalculate Improved RPN]
    L --> M{Risk Reduction Greater than 50 percent}
    M -->|Yes| N[Close Criticality Item]
    M -->|No| O[Re-engineer or Add Redundancy]
    O --> L
    J --> P[Document in CA Register]
    N --> P
    P --> Q[End: Finalize Criticality Report]
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading the Diagram:**
> - Blocks A, B, C are **input/identification** stages.
> - Blocks D, E, F are **scoring** stages (run in parallel).
> - Block G is the **computational core** (RPN multiplication).
> - Block H is the **decision gate** (the 200 threshold is industry standard).
> - Blocks K, L, M, O form the **iterative risk-reduction loop**.

---

### 4.2 Hierarchical Decomposition of Criticality Levels

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CR[Criticality Score] --> L1[Level 1 Critical RPN 300 to 1000]
    CR --> L2[Level 2 High RPN 200 to 299]
    CR --> L3[Level 3 Medium RPN 100 to 199]
    CR --> L4[Level 4 Low RPN 1 to 99]
    L1 --> A1[Mandatory SIL 2 or higher]
    L1 --> A2[Redundancy Mandatory]
    L1 --> A3[Continuous Diagnostics Required]
    L2 --> A4[SIL 1 or Procedural Controls]
    L3 --> A5[Periodic Inspection]
    L4 --> A6[Routine Maintenance Only]
```

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Interpretation:**
> - **Level 1 (Critical):** Component failure will likely cause loss of life, major environmental damage, or financial loss > ₹10 crore. Requires **SIL 2/3 certified safety instrumented functions (SIFs)**.
> - **Level 4 (Low):** Failure causes minor inconvenience. Standard preventive maintenance is sufficient.

---

### 4.3 Cause-Effect Chain (How Criticality Links to SIL Selection)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Process Hazard] --> P2[Identify Initiating Event]
    P2 --> P3[Determine Consequence Severity S]
    P3 --> P4[Determine Demand Rate on SIF]
    P4 --> P5[Calculate Required PFDavg from Target Risk]
    P5 --> P6[Map PFDavg to SIL Level]
    P6 --> P7[SIL 1 PFD 10-2 to 10-1]
    P6 --> P8[SIL 2 PFD 10-3 to 10-2]
    P6 --> P9[SIL 3 PFD 10-4 to 10-3]
    P6 --> P10[SIL 4 PFD 10-5 to 10-4]
    P7 --> P11[Select SIF Architecture]
    P8 --> P11
    P9 --> P11
    P10 --> P11
    P11 --> P12[Verify with Criticality Review]
```

> [!NOTE] **Why This Diagram Matters for KTU:**
> This is the **end-to-end bridge** between Qualitative Criticality Analysis (RPN matrix) and Quantitative SIL determination (PFD<sub>avg</sub>). Examiners in Module 2 frequently ask: *"How does a high Criticality rating translate into a SIL requirement?"* The answer is in this flow.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define Criticality Analysis. Mention its two major quantitative outputs.**

**Model Answer:**

Criticality Analysis is a structured engineering methodology used in Process Safety Management to evaluate, classify, and rank failure modes of industrial components based on the combination of consequence severity and frequency of occurrence.

The two major quantitative outputs are:

1. **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** — computed as $S \times O \times D$, yielding a score from 1 to 1000.
2. **Criticality Rating (Cr)** — computed as $S \times \alpha \times \beta \times \lambda_p \times t$, yielding a continuous-scale consequence magnitude per mission.

---

#### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between Severity, Occurrence, and Detection in the context of FMEA-based Criticality Analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Meaning** | **Scale** | **Effect on RPN** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Severity (S)** | Consequence magnitude of the failure | 1 to 10 | Higher S → more dangerous outcome |
| **Occurrence (O)** | Likelihood the failure mode actually happens | 1 to 10 | Higher O → more frequent failure |
| **Detection (D)** | Difficulty of detecting the failure *before* it causes harm | 1 to 10 | Higher D → harder to detect → more critical |

The product $S \times O \times D$ gives the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)**. A failure with $S = 10$, $O = 10$, $D = 10$ gives RPN = 1000 (highest criticality).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question B1 — Choice A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the step-by-step procedure for conducting a Criticality Analysis on a pressure vessel system in a petrochemical plant. List all inputs, scoring scales, and decision criteria.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A furnace fuel gas control valve has the following data:
- Severity $S = 8$
- Occurrence $O = 4$
- Detection $D = 7$
- Failure mode ratio $\alpha = 0.3$
- Conditional probability of hazardous outcome $\beta = 0.6$
- Failure rate $\lambda_p = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hour
- Mission time $t = 8{,}760$ hours (1 year)

Calculate the **RPN** and the **Criticality Rating Cr**. Comment on the risk-reduction action required.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. **Define the system boundary** — clearly state which pressure vessels, piping, instrumentation, and control loops are in scope.
2. **Identify all components** within the boundary (e.g., pressure transmitters, relief valves, rupture discs, block valves).
3. **List failure modes** for each component (e.g., transmitter fails low, relief valve fails to open, block valve passes leakage).
4. **Assign Severity (S)** on a 1–10 scale based on consequence (injury, environmental release, production loss, financial).
5. **Assign Occurrence (O)** on a 1–10 scale based on historical failure data or reliability databases.
6. **Assign Detection (D)** on a 1–10 scale based on the capability of existing diagnostics and operator monitoring.
7. **Compute RPN = S × O × D** for each failure mode.
8. **Apply threshold rule:** RPN > 200 and S ≥ 9 → High Priority, requiring immediate mitigation.
9. **Rank all failure modes** in descending RPN order.
10. **Develop a Criticality Register / Worksheet** documenting the analysis.
11. **Recommend mitigations** (e.g., redundancy, SIL upgrades, proof testing, procedural changes).
12. **Recalculate improved RPN** post-mitigation and verify risk reduction.
13. **Obtain management sign-off** and integrate into the plant's Safety Management System.

**Inputs Required:** P&ID drawings, process flow diagrams, instrument data sheets, failure databases, HAZOP report, operating procedures.

**Decision Criteria:**
- RPN > 200 → **High Priority**
- RPN 100–200 → **Medium Priority**
- RPN < 100 → **Low Priority**
- *Override Rule:* If S ≥ 9, the item is automatically High Priority regardless of RPN.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate RPN:**

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D = 8 \times 4 \times 7 = 224
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Criticality Rating Cr:**

$$
C_r = S \times \alpha \times \beta \times \lambda_p \times t
$$

$$
C_r = 8 \times 0.3 \times 0.6 \times (5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 8760
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply step-by-step:**

$$
8 \times 0.3 = 2.4
$$

$$
2.4 \times 0.6 = 1.44
$$

$$
1.44 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 7.2 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
7.2 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 = 0.063072
$$

$$
\boxed{C_r \approx 0.0631 \text{ (consequence units per mission year)}}
$$

**Step 4 — Comment on Risk-Reduction Action:**

- An RPN of **224** places the valve in the **High Priority** band.
- An S = 8 with frequent fuel gas application means a failure could cause a **fire/explosion event**.
- **Recommended Actions:**
  1. Install a **redundant 1oo2 voted valve pair** with SIL-2 certification.
  2. Add **continuous partial-stroke testing** to improve Detection from 7 to ≤ 3.
  3. Increase **proof-test frequency** to every 6 months to reduce $\lambda_{DU}$.
- **Expected new RPN:** $8 \times 4 \times 3 = 96$ → moved to **Low Priority band**.

---

#### **Question B1 — Choice B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the functional architecture of a Criticality Analysis process. Explain each block in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A centrifugal pump in a hydrocarbon pipeline has three identified failure modes with the following data:

| **Failure Mode** | **S** | **O** | **D** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Seal leakage | 7 | 6 | 5 |
| Bearing overheating | 8 | 4 | 6 |
| Coupling failure | 6 | 3 | 4 |

Calculate the RPN for each mode, rank them, and recommend a mitigation strategy for the highest-RPN mode.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram Explanation:**

*(Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the full Mermaid block diagram.)*

The architecture consists of the following blocks in sequence:

1. **System Definition Block** — Defines the scope and physical boundaries of the analysis.
2. **Failure Mode Identification Block** — Generates a list of all credible failure modes for each component.
3. **Scoring Module** — Three parallel scoring channels (Severity, Occurrence, Detection), each producing a 1–10 rating.
4. **RPN Computation Engine** — Multiplies the three scores.
5. **Decision Gate** — Compares RPN against the 200 threshold and applies the Severity override rule.
6. **Mitigation Loop** — Iterative feedback loop where mitigations are designed, RPN is recalculated, and reduction > 50% is verified.
7. **Documentation Block** — Generates the final Criticality Register / Report.
8. **Output Block** — Final ranked list of failure modes with action priorities.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate RPN for each failure mode:**

**Mode 1 — Seal leakage:**

$$
\text{RPN}_1 = 7 \times 6 \times 5 = 210
$$

**Mode 2 — Bearing overheating:**

$$
\text{RPN}_2 = 8 \times 4 \times 6 = 192
$$

**Mode 3 — Coupling failure:**

$$
\text{RPN}_3 = 6 \times 3 \times 4 = 72
$$

**Step 2 — Rank the modes:**

| **Rank** | **Failure Mode** | **RPN** | **Priority** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Seal leakage | 210 | **High** |
| 2 | Bearing overheating | 192 | Medium |
| 3 | Coupling failure | 72 | Low |

**Step 3 — Mitigation strategy for highest-RPN mode (Seal leakage):**

- **Engineering Control:** Replace the standard mechanical seal with a **tandem dual seal** with a buffer fluid system to reduce Severity from 7 to 4.
- **Detection Improvement:** Install **continuous seal-pot level monitoring** with low-level alarm to improve Detection from 5 to 2.
- **Preventive Maintenance:** Increase seal inspection interval from quarterly to monthly.
- **Calculated New RPN:** $\text{RPN}_{\text{new}} = 4 \times 6 \times 2 = 48$ → moved to **Low Priority band**.
