# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture of a Safety Instrumented System (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    procA[Process Plant - Reactor / Tank / Pipeline] --> sensA[Sensor Layer - PT TT LT FT]
    sensA --> logicA[Logic Solver - Safety PLC]
    logicA --> fceA[Final Control Element - Shutdown Valve / Breaker]
    fceA --> procA
    bpcsA[BPCS Layer - DCS / PID Controller] -.Independent Path.- procA
    opA[Human Operator - SCADA HMI] -.Read Only Access.- logicA
    logicA --> tripA[Trip Action - Fail Safe Shutdown]
    classDef safe fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class sensA,logicA,fceA,tripA safe
    class procA proc
    class bpcsA,opA ctrl
```

**Diagram Explanation:**
- The **Process Plant** (light blue) is the entity being protected.
- The **Sensor**, **Logic Solver**, and **Final Control Element** blocks (gold) form the **SIS loop**.
- The **BPCS** (green) operates on the *same process* but via an *independent path* (dashed line).
- The **Operator** (green) has read-only access to the SIS — preventing accidental bypass.

---

## 4.2 Layers of Protection Analysis (LOPA) — Onion Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L6[Layer 6 - Community Emergency Response - Evacuation Plans] --> L5
    L5[Layer 5 - Physical Protection - Bunds, Firewalls, Dikes] --> L4
    L4[Layer 4 - Mechanical Relief - PSVs, Rupture Discs] --> L3
    L3[Layer 3 - Safety Instrumented System - SIS / ESD] --> L2
    L2[Layer 2 - Basic Process Control System - BPCS / DCS] --> L1
    L1[Layer 1 - Process Design - Inherently Safer Design]
    classDef core fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    class L3 core
    classDef def fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#808080,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    class L1,L2,L4,L5,L6 def
```

**Reading the Diagram (Top-Down):**
- **Top layer (L6)** = Last resort, after the incident has occurred
- **Bottom layer (L1)** = First line of defense, prevention through design
- **Highlighted L3 (gold)** = The SIS sits at the **3rd layer** — automatic, independent, reliable

---

## 4.3 Voting Logic Architectures (Redundancy)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Process Variable] --> B1[Channel 1 - Sensor A]
    A1 --> B2[Channel 2 - Sensor B]
    A1 --> B3[Channel 3 - Sensor C]
    B1 --> V{Voting Logic Block}
    B2 --> V
    B3 --> V
    V -->|Trip Decision| O[Shutdown Valve / Breaker]
    V -->|1oo2 any trips| N1[Output 1 - Trip]
    V -->|2oo3 majority trips| N2[Output 2 - Trip]
    V -->|1oo2D diagnostic trips| N3[Output 3 - Trip]
    classDef sensor fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:1px
    classDef logic fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    class B1,B2,B3 sensor
    class V,O logic
```

**Architecture Notes:**
- Three identical sensor channels feed into a **voting block**.
- Depending on the configuration, the system can demand **1-out-of-2**, **2-out-of-3**, or **1-out-of-2D** agreement.
- Each voting scheme offers a **different trade-off** between *safety* (probability of failing to trip) and *availability* (probability of spurious trip).

---

## 4.4 BPCS vs SIS — Functional Boundary Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph subControl[BPCS - Basic Process Control System]
        B1[DCS Controller - PID Loop] --> B2[Control Valve - Modulating]
        B2 --> BP[Process - Normal Operation]
        B1 -.Feedback.- B2
    end
    subgraph subSafety[SIS - Safety Instrumented System]
        S1[Safety Transmitter - Independent Sensor] --> S2[Safety PLC - Logic Solver]
        S2 --> SV[Shutdown Valve - On-Off]
        SV -.Fail Safe on Demand.- BP
    end
    BP -.Monitored by Both.- subControl
    BP -.Monitored by Both.- subSafety
    classDef bpcs fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sis fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef proc fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    class B1,B2 bpcs
    class S1,S2,SV sis
    class BP proc
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **green BPCS sub-graph** runs the plant under normal conditions.
- The **orange SIS sub-graph** is dormant until a hazardous demand occurs.
- The **blue Process block** is the single entity being monitored by **two independent systems** — achieving *true redundancy*.

---

## 4.5 Safety Lifecycle (IEC 61511) — SIS Implementation Roadmap

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ph1[Phase 1 - Process Hazard Analysis] --> ph2[Phase 2 - Risk Assessment and SIL Determination]
    ph2 --> ph3[Phase 3 - SIF Design and Engineering]
    ph3 --> ph4[Phase 4 - Installation and Commissioning]
    ph4 --> ph5[Phase 5 - Validation]
    ph5 --> ph6[Phase 6 - Operation and Maintenance]
    ph6 --> ph7[Phase 7 - Modification Management]
    ph7 --> ph6
    ph6 --> ph8[Phase 8 - Decommissioning]
    classDef phase fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    class ph1,ph2,ph3,ph4,ph5,ph6,ph7,ph8 phase
```

**Key Lifecycle Insight:**
- Phase 2 (**SIL Determination**) is where LOPA and Risk Graphs are applied.
- Phase 5 (**Validation**) requires a documented **Site Acceptance Test (SAT)** with proof of PFDavg.
- Phase 6 includes mandatory **Proof Testing** to maintain the SIL throughout the plant's operational life.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Safety Instrumented System (SIS). State any two standards governing its design.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** is an engineered system composed of sensors, logic solvers, and final control elements that automatically takes a process to a safe state when predetermined unsafe conditions are detected.

**Governing Standards:**
1. **IEC 61508** — "Functional Safety of Electrical/Electronic/Programmable Electronic Safety-Related Systems" (the umbrella standard).
2. **IEC 61511** — "Functional Safety: Safety Instrumented Systems for the Process Industry Sector" (the sector-specific implementation).

> [!TIP]
> Always write *both* the full title and the standard number for full marks. Mentioning only the code (e.g., "IEC 61511") without context may attract a ½-mark deduction.

---

### Question 2: List four differences between BPCS and SIS.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks — tabular form):**

| S.No | BPCS | SIS |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Maintains normal operation | Provides protective shutdown |
| 2 | Achieves SIL 0 (no formal SIL) | Achieves SIL 1 to SIL 4 |
| 3 | Components can fail in any direction | Components must fail to **safe state** |
| 4 | Optional redundancy | Mandatory redundancy (1oo2, 2oo3) for high SIL |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **architecture of a Safety Instrumented System (SIS)**, clearly identifying the three main subsystems and their functions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **need for SIS** in modern process industries, citing at least three major industrial accidents that justified the adoption of independent protection layers. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

### Part (a) — Architecture of SIS (7 Marks)

A Safety Instrumented System (SIS) consists of **three independent subsystems** that together implement one or more Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs):

**1. Sensor Subsystem (Input Stage) — 2 Marks**

The sensor continuously monitors the process variable and converts it into a standardized signal (typically 4–20 mA with HART, or digital via Foundation Fieldbus). Common sensors include:
- Pressure transmitters (PT)
- Temperature sensors (RTD/thermocouple)
- Level transmitters (LT) and switches (LSH/LSL)
- Flow meters (FT)
- Flame and gas detectors

For SIL 2 and above, **redundant sensors** (two or three identical units) are mandatory to meet the PFD target.

**2. Logic Solver (Decision Stage) — 2 Marks**

The logic solver is the "brain" of the SIS. It receives input from sensors, executes pre-programmed safety logic (typically in Function Block Diagrams or Ladder Logic), and issues trip commands to final elements. Examples include:
- **Safety PLCs** (e.g., Honeywell Safety Manager, Siemens AS 410, Yokogawa ProSafe-RS, Triconex Tricon)
- **Solid-state logic units** (for SIL 3)
- **Relay-based logic** (for simple SIL 1 applications)

The logic solver must be **TÜV-certified** to IEC 61508 SIL 2 or SIL 3.

**3. Final Control Element (Output Stage) — 2 Marks**

The final element physically executes the protective action. Examples include:
- **Solenoid-operated shutdown valves** (SDV) — fail-closed or fail-open
- **Motor-operated valves** (MOV) with spring return
- **Circuit breakers** and **contactor relays**
- **Variable frequency drives** (VFDs) in emergency stop mode

The final element must be **fail-safe** — i.e., de-energizing it must drive the process toward the safe state.

**Integration with BPCS — 1 Mark**

The SIS operates **independently** of the BPCS. The two systems may share the same physical process but use **separate sensors, separate wiring, separate power supplies, and separate logic solvers**. This independence is critical to avoid common-cause failures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always draw the block diagram and **label each block with a specific industrial example** (e.g., "PT-101 on reactor overhead"). This earns the "Application" mark.

---

### Part (b) — Need for SIS (7 Marks)

**Reason 1 — Human Limitation (2 Marks)**

Plant operators require **30–60 seconds** to recognize an alarm, diagnose the cause, and take corrective action. Many process upsets (e.g., runaway exothermic reactions, vapor cloud explosions) develop in **fractions of a second**. The SIS provides automatic, sub-second response that humans cannot match.

**Reason 2 — BPCS Limitations (2 Marks)**

The Basic Process Control System is designed for **continuous modulation**, not fail-safe shutdown. If a BPCS sensor fails, the entire control loop is compromised — there is no backup. Furthermore, BPCS components can fail in **unsafe directions** (e.g., a control valve stuck fully open during overpressure). The SIS provides a **layer of redundancy** that is independent of BPCS failure modes.

**Reason 3 — Regulatory & Economic Necessity (2 Marks)**

- **Bhopal Disaster (1984, India):** Methyl isocyanate release killed 3,800+ people. The plant's safety systems were either disabled or non-functional. Led to the **OSHA Process Safety Management (PSM)** standard worldwide.
- **BP Texas City Refinery (2005, USA):** Raffinate splitter tower explosion killed 15 workers. The independent high-level alarm (a candidate SIS) had been disabled. Led to **CSB recommendations** for SIS independence.
- **Flixborough Disaster (1974, UK):** Nypro caprolactam plant explosion killed 28. Showed that even "minor" modifications can defeat BPCS safeguards.

These incidents catalyzed the development of **IEC 61508 (1998)** and **IEC 61511 (2003)**.

**Reason 4 — Quantitative Risk Reduction (1 Mark)**

Modern plants use **LOPA** to demonstrate that residual risk (after SIS implementation) is below tolerable levels. Without an SIS, the residual risk of major incidents often exceeds regulatory thresholds, making plant operation legally impermissible.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a comparison table, explain **six key differences between BPCS and SIS**. Discuss why SIS is considered a separate, independent layer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **components of an SIS in detail** with reference to redundancy, voting logic (1oo2, 2oo3, 1oo2D), and fail-safe design principles. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

### Part (a) — BPCS vs SIS Comparison (7 Marks)

**Detailed Comparison (6 Marks — Table):**

| Parameter | BPCS | SIS |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Function** | Continuous control (e.g., flow, temperature, pressure regulation) | Protective shutdown on demand |
| **Reliability Metric** | SIL 0 (no formal target) | SIL 1 to SIL 4 (PFDavg mandated) |
| **Independence** | Standalone | **Independent** of BPCS |
| **Failure Direction** | May fail unsafe or safe — no requirement | **Must fail to safe state** |
| **Hardware Standard** | General IEC 61131-3 PLCs | IEC 61508 / IEC 61511 certified |
| **Redundancy** | For uptime only | For safety (1oo2, 2oo3, 1oo2D) |
| **Operation Mode** | Active, modulating | **Quiescent**, acts on demand |
| **Response Time** | Slow, deliberate | Fast, deterministic (< 1 s) |
| **Documentation** | Standard P&ID | Full Safety Lifecycle Documentation |
| **Proof Testing** | Routine calibration | Mandatory periodic proof testing |

**Why SIS Must Be Independent (1 Mark):**

The SIS must be **physically, electrically, and logically separate** from the BPCS to prevent **common-cause failures** — events that could disable both control and protection simultaneously (e.g., a fire in the DCS cabinet, a shared power supply failure, or a software bug). Independence guarantees that even if the BPCS fails completely, the SIS remains capable of executing its protective function.

---

### Part (b) — Components of SIS: Voting Logic and Fail-Safe Design (7 Marks)

**1. Redundancy Architecture (3 Marks)**

Redundancy means using more than one component to perform the same function so that no single failure can disable the SIF. Common architectures:

- **1oo2 (1-out-of-2):** Two parallel channels; system trips if *either* detects a fault. Used for **high-safety, low-availability** applications (e.g., emergency shutdown where spurious trips are tolerable).
- **2oo3 (2-out-of-3):** Three parallel channels; system trips only if *two or more* agree. Used for **balanced safety and availability** — most common in high-integrity applications.
- **1oo2D (1-out-of-2 with Diagnostics):** One channel active, one in continuous diagnostic mode. Detects faults without causing spurious trips.

**2. Voting Logic (2 Marks)**

The voting block continuously compares the outputs of redundant channels:
- In **1oo2**, the OR-gate logic decides trip.
- In **2oo3**, a majority-voter (essentially two OR-gates feeding an AND-gate) decides trip.
- Voting logic is implemented either in **hardware relays** (simple, robust, SIL 1–2) or in **safety PLC firmware** (flexible, SIL 3).

**3. Fail-Safe Design Principle (2 Marks)**

A fail-safe system defaults to the **safe state** upon loss of:
- **Power** (de-energize to trip)
- **Signal** (loss of signal = trip)
- **Air supply** (for pneumatic valves)

This is achieved by:
- Using **spring-return actuators** that close valves when air pressure is lost.
- Using **normally-closed solenoid valves** that open on energization (or the reverse, depending on safe state).
- Using **fail-safe sensors** that output a defined safe-direction signal on internal fault.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Trick Question:** *"Can a fail-open valve ever be considered safe?"* — **Yes**, if the safe state for that specific process requires *opening* (e.g., a cooling water valve that must open to prevent overheating on pump failure). The key is that the **failure direction must align with the safe state**, regardless of whether it is open or closed.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callouts
>
> **Pitfall 1 — Conflating SIL with Hardware Brand:** Students often answer *"SIS uses Triconex"* — this is a brand, not a definition. Always define **SIL** as a *probabilistic reliability target*, not a hardware specification.
>
> **Pitfall 2 — Skipping the Standard References:** A 14-mark question without mentioning **IEC 61508** or **IEC 61511** will lose at least 2 marks. Always cite the standard.
>
> **Pitfall 3 — Drawing BPCS and SIS as the Same Box:** Many students draw a single block for "control and safety." This is **architecturally wrong** and indicates a fundamental misunderstanding. They MUST be shown as **separate blocks with separate signal paths**.
>
> **Pitfall 4 — Confusing PFD with PFH:** Low-demand mode → **PFD**; High-demand/continuous mode → **PFH**. Mixing them up signals confusion between IEC 61508 and IEC 61511.
>
> **Pitfall 5 — Forgetting Units in Numerical Problems:** When asked to calculate PFDavg, always state that it is **dimensionless** (a probability). When asked for failure rate $\lambda$, state **per hour** or **FIT** (1 FIT = $10^{-9}$ /h).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ### 📋 High-Density Revision Checklist

**Core Definitions:**
- ☐ SIS = engineered system for protective shutdown (IEC 61511)
- ☐ SIF = Safety Instrumented Function — a single safety loop
- ☐ SIL = Safety Integrity Level — probabilistic reliability (1 to 4)
- ☐ PFDavg = Average Probability of Failure on Demand
- ☐ RRF = Risk Reduction Factor = 1 / PFDavg
- ☐ LOPA = Layers of Protection Analysis

**Three Subsystems of SIS:**
- ☐ **Sensor** (input) — detects the unsafe condition
- ☐ **Logic Solver** (decision) — Safety PLC / relay
- ☐ **Final Control Element** (output) — valve / breaker

**Key SIL Bands (Low-Demand Mode):**
- ☐ SIL 1: $10^{-2} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-1}$ → RRF 10–100
- ☐ SIL 2: $10^{-3} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-2}$ → RRF 100–1000
- ☐ SIL 3: $10^{-4} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-3}$ → RRF 1000–10,000
- ☐ SIL 4: $10^{-5} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-4}$ → RRF 10,000–100,000

**Master Formulas:**
- ☐ $\text{PFDavg}_{1oo1} = \dfrac{\lambda_D \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$
- ☐ $\text{PFD}_{SIF} = \text{PFD}_S + \text{PFD}_L + \text{PFD}_{FE}$
- ☐ $\text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PFDavg}}$
- ☐ $\text{Risk} = f \times C$
- ☐ $\text{RRR} = \dfrac{\text{Actual Risk}}{\text{Tolerable Risk}}$

**Voting Logic:**
- ☐ 1oo2 = trip on first fault (high safety, low availability)
- ☐ 2oo3 = trip on second agreement (balanced)
- ☐ 1oo2D = diagnostic-voted (high coverage)

**BPCS vs SIS — Must-Remember Differences:**
- ☐ BPCS = continuous control, **SIL 0**
- ☐ SIS = protective shutdown, **SIL 1–4**
- ☐ BPCS and SIS are **independent** (separate hardware, power, logic)
- ☐ SIS must be **fail-safe**
- ☐ SIS requires **mandatory proof testing**

**Critical Standards to Quote:**
- ☐ **IEC 61508** — Generic functional safety standard
- ☐ **IEC 61511** — Process industry sector standard
- ☐ **ISA 84** — North American equivalent
- ☐ **OSHA 1910.119** — US Process Safety Management regulation

**LOPA Layers (Bottom to Top):**
- ☐ L1: Inherent safe design
- ☐ L2: BPCS
- ☐ L3: SIS ← **SIS belongs here**
- ☐ L4: Mechanical relief (PSVs, rupture discs)
- ☐ L5: Physical protection (bunds, dikes)
- ☐ L6: Emergency response

**Industrial Disaster Examples (Use at least one in essays):**
- ☐ **Bhopal 1984** — Methyl isocyanate release
- ☐ **Flixborough 1974** — Nypro caprolactam explosion
- ☐ **BP Texas City 2005** — Refinery explosion
- ☐ **Piper Alpha 1988** — North Sea oil platform

**Last-Minute Mnemonic:**
> **"SIS = Sense, Interpret, Stop"**
> - **S**ense → Sensor
> - **I**nterpret → Logic Solver
> - **S**top → Final Control Element

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Risk - How to Measure Risk, Risk Tolerance, Safety Integrity Level
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT743 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Risk - How to Measure Risk, Risk Tolerance, Safety Integrity Level

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Risk?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (IEC 61511 / ISO 31000):**
> **Risk** is the *combination of the probability of an event occurring and the severity of the consequences (harm)* if it occurs. Mathematically expressed as:
>
> $$Risk = Probability \times Consequence$$

In KTU 2024 syllabus parlance, **Risk** is the quantified measure of the *threat* that a hazard poses to people, environment, assets, and reputation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Rain on Your Picnic"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Explanation:**
> Imagine planning an outdoor picnic. There are two things you worry about:
> 1. **Will it rain?** → That's the *Probability* (How often?)
> 2. **What happens if it rains?** → That's the *Consequence* (Wet sandwiches, sick guests, ruined day)
>
> If rain is **rare** and you only get a *little wet* → **Low Risk**. You might just bring an umbrella.
> If rain is **likely** and it will *flood the park* → **High Risk**. You cancel the picnic entirely.
> Risk = (Chance of bad thing) × (How bad the thing is).

This is exactly how industrial engineers think about a chemical reactor that might overheat: How often? → How bad? → That product is the **Risk**.

### 1.3 Core Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Definition | Industrial Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Hazard** | Source with potential to cause harm | Hot steam line, high voltage cable |
| **Hazardous Event** | The incident that releases the hazard | Pipe rupture, short circuit |
| **Severity** | Outcome/consequence magnitude | Fatality, injury, environmental damage |
| **Frequency** | How often the event occurs per unit time | Once in 1000 years, once per shift |
| **Risk** | Frequency × Severity (combination) | Quantified danger measure |
| **Risk Tolerance** | Maximum risk an organization will accept | Decision threshold for action |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 How to Measure Risk – The Three Methodological Tiers

Risk measurement is not a one-size-fits-all approach. The KTU syllabus covers three escalating levels of rigor:

#### **Tier 1 – Qualitative Risk Assessment**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Uses **descriptive categories** (e.g., High / Medium / Low) for both probability and severity. Best for screening hazards early in a project where precise numbers are unavailable.

- **Technique:** Risk Matrix (5×5 grid)
- **Output:** Risk Rating = Severity Category × Likelihood Category
- **Used When:** Early design phase, hazard identification workshops (HAZID), limited data available

#### **Tier 2 – Semi-Quantitative Risk Assessment**

> [!NOTE]
> Assigns **numerical scores** to categories (e.g., Severity = 1 to 5, Probability = 1 to 5). Produces a numeric risk score that can be ranked.

- **Technique:** Risk Score = Likelihood Score × Severity Score
- **Output:** Numerical ranking enabling prioritization
- **Used When:** Comparing many similar hazards, ranking mitigation options

#### **Tier 3 – Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Uses **actual numerical values** for frequency (events/year) and consequence (fatalities, $ loss). The most rigorous method, used for high-hazard industries like oil & gas, nuclear, chemical processing.

- **Technique:** Fault Tree Analysis (FTA), Event Tree Analysis (ETA), Bow-Tie Analysis
- **Output:** Individual Risk (IR) and Societal Risk (F-N curves)
- **Used When:** Major Hazard Facilities, COMAH / Seveso regulations, designing Safety Instrumented Systems

### 2.2 Risk Metrics – Individual Risk vs. Societal Risk

**Individual Risk (IR):** The frequency at which an individual may be expected to sustain a given level of harm.

$$IR = f \times P_{fatal}$$

where $f$ is the frequency of the hazardous event and $P_{fatal}$ is the probability of fatality given exposure.

**Societal Risk:** The relationship between frequency and the number of people suffering a specified level of harm. Usually plotted on an **F-N Curve**.

$$F(N) = \text{Frequency of accidents causing N or more fatalities}$$

### 2.3 Risk Tolerance (Acceptable Risk)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Risk Tolerance** is the *maximum level of risk that an organization, regulator, or society is willing to accept* in pursuit of its objectives.

It is governed by the **ALARP Principle** – *As Low As Reasonably Practicable* (sometimes called ALARA – As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

#### **The ALARP Triangle / Cost-Benefit Logic**

A risk is *Tolerable* only if:
1. Further risk reduction is **impractical** (cost grossly disproportionate to benefit), OR
2. The cost of further reduction is **excessively high** compared to the safety gain.

Mathematically:

$$Cost\ of\ Further\ Reduction \gg Benefit\ of\ Further\ Reduction \Rightarrow ALARP\ Achieved$$

#### **Risk Tolerance Criteria Bands**

| Risk Level | Description | Action Required |
|---|---|---|
| **Negligible / Acceptable** | Risk is so low it can be ignored | No further action |
| **Tolerable (ALARP region)** | Risk is acceptable only if ALARP demonstrated | Reduce risk if reasonably practicable |
| **Unacceptable / Intolerable** | Risk is too high regardless of cost | Must reduce risk, regardless of cost |

### 2.4 Safety Integrity Level (SIL) – The Heart of Functional Safety

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SIL** is a *discrete level* (1, 2, 3, or 4) used to specify the **safety integrity requirements** of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) within a Safety Instrumented System (SIS). Defined by **IEC 61508** (generic) and **IEC 61511** (process industries).

SIL answers one question: *"How reliably must our safety system work to bring risk down to a tolerable level?"*

#### **Two Operating Modes of Safety Systems**

1. **Low Demand Mode** – Safety system is rarely tested (less than once per year demand). Integrity measured by **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)**.
2. **High Demand / Continuous Mode** – Safety system operates continuously. Integrity measured by **Probability of Dangerous Failure per Hour (PFH)**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet – Risk, ALARP & SIL

| # | Concept / Quantity | Formula / Expression | Unit / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Risk (Basic) | $R = f \times C$ | events/year × consequence |
| 2 | Risk Score (Semi-Quantitative) | $R = L \times S$ | Dimensionless score |
| 3 | Individual Risk | $IR = f \cdot P_{fatal}$ | per year (fatalities/person/year) |
| 4 | Risk Reduction Factor | $RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Achieved Risk | $R_{achieved} = f \times PFD$ | events/year |
| 6 | SIL (Low Demand) | $PFD_{avg}$ | $10^{-1} \to 10^{-5}$ |
| 7 | SIL (High Demand) | $PFH$ | $10^{-5} \to 10^{-9}$ per hour |
| 8 | Cost-Benefit (ALARP) | $\dfrac{Cost_{prevention}}{Benefit_{reduction}} \gg 1$ | Ratio |
| 9 | Mean Time To Failure | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Hours |
| 10 | Failure Rate | $\lambda = \lambda_{D} + \lambda_{S}$ | per hour |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols used:**
> $f$ = frequency of hazardous event, $C$ = consequence, $L$ = likelihood score, $S$ = severity score, $PFD$ = Probability of Failure on Demand, $PFH$ = Probability of Dangerous Failure per Hour, $\lambda_{D}$ = dangerous failure rate, $\lambda_{S}$ = safe failure rate.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas:** Determining SIL level for Emergency Shutdown (ESD) valves on offshore platforms.
- **Chemical Plants:** SIL rating for burner management systems, overpressure protection.
- **Power Plants:** SIL for reactor trip systems, turbine overspeed protection.
- **Manufacturing:** SIL for robotic cell safety interlocks, press braking systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 – From Event Frequency to Tolerable Risk

**Given:**
- A hazardous event (e.g., overpressure in a reactor) has a frequency $f$ of **once every 100 years**.
- Without any protection, the consequence is **5 fatalities**.
- The regulator sets a tolerable Individual Risk of **$10^{-5}$ fatalities per person-year**.
- 50 people are continuously exposed.

**Step 1 – Convert frequency to per-year units**

$$f = \dfrac{1}{100} = 0.01 \text{ events/year}$$

**Step 2 – Calculate the Unmitigated Risk**

We assume (worst case) that if the event occurs, all 50 exposed people are killed:

$$R_{unmitigated} = f \times N_{exposed} = 0.01 \times 50 = 0.5 \text{ fatalities/year}$$

**Step 3 – Compare against tolerable Individual Risk threshold**

Convert to per-person risk:

$$IR_{unmitigated} = \dfrac{0.5}{50} = 1 \times 10^{-2} \text{ fatalities/person/year}$$

**Step 4 – Determine the Required Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)**

$$RRF_{required} = \dfrac{IR_{unmitigated}}{IR_{tolerable}} = \dfrac{10^{-2}}{10^{-5}} = 1000$$

**Step 5 – Determine Required SIL**

Since $PFD_{required} = \dfrac{1}{RRF_{required}}$:

$$PFD_{required} = \dfrac{1}{1000} = 1 \times 10^{-3}$$

Looking at the SIL table (low demand mode):

$$10^{-3} \leq PFD < 10^{-2} \Rightarrow \mathbf{SIL\ 2}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 – ALARP Cost-Benefit Calculation

**Given:**
- An existing plant has a residual fatality risk of $5 \times 10^{-4}$ per year.
- A proposed additional safeguard would reduce this to $1 \times 10^{-5}$ per year.
- Statistical Value of a Prevented Fatality (VPF) = ₹ 5 Crore.
- Cost of implementing the safeguard = ₹ 1.5 Crore.

**Step 1 – Calculate Risk Reduction**

$$\Delta R = R_{before} - R_{after} = (5 \times 10^{-4}) - (1 \times 10^{-5}) = 4.9 \times 10^{-4} \text{ fatalities/year}$$

**Step 2 – Calculate Expected Benefit (lives saved × value)**

$$\text{Annual Benefit} = \Delta R \times VPF = 4.9 \times 10^{-4} \times 5 \times 10^{7} = ₹\ 24{,}500 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 3 – Compute Cost-Benefit Ratio (Gross Disproportion)**

$$Disproportion\ Ratio = \dfrac{Cost\ of\ Measure}{Annual\ Benefit} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{7}}{2.45 \times 10^{4}} \approx 612$$

**Step 4 – Apply ALARP Test**

> [!NOTE]
> In UK HSE practice, a *gross disproportion* factor of **between 2 and 10** is typically considered acceptable for ALARP determination. A factor of **612** means the cost is hugely disproportionate to the benefit → Risk is **already ALARP**, no further measures required.

$$\therefore Cost\ Reduction \gg Benefit\ Reduction \Rightarrow ALARP\ Demonstrated$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix – SIL Levels at a Glance

| SIL | $PFD_{avg}$ (Low Demand) | $PFH$ (High Demand) | Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) | Equivalent Failure Rate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SIL 1** | $10^{-2} \leq PFD < 10^{-1}$ | $10^{-6} \leq PFH < 10^{-5}$ | 10 to 100 | Moderate |
| **SIL 2** | $10^{-3} \leq PFD < 10^{-2}$ | $10^{-7} \leq PFH < 10^{-6}$ | 100 to 1000 | Low |
| **SIL 3** | $10^{-4} \leq PFD < 10^{-3}$ | $10^{-8} \leq PFH < 10^{-7}$ | 1000 to 10,000 | Very Low |
| **SIL 4** | $10^{-5} \leq PFD < 10^{-4}$ | $10^{-9} \leq PFH < 10^{-8}$ | 10,000 to 100,000 | Extremely Low |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** The target failure measure is **TIGHTER** for higher SIL numbers. SIL 4 is the highest and rarest; it is mainly used in nuclear and aerospace. Most process industries operate with **SIL 1, SIL 2, or SIL 3** systems.

### 3.4 Derivation 3 – Bow-Tie Risk Reduction Architecture

A **Bow-Tie diagram** connects causes (left) to consequences (right) through a central **Top Event**, with **Preventive** barriers (left side) and **Mitigative** barriers (right side).

**Total PFD of the safety function** with multiple protective layers:

$$PFD_{total} = PFD_{layer\ 1} \times PFD_{layer\ 2} \times PFD_{layer\ 3}$$

**Example:** A process has three independent protection layers (IPLs):
- BPCS control loop: $PFD_1 = 1 \times 10^{-1}$
- Alarm + Operator intervention: $PFD_2 = 1 \times 10^{-1}$
- SIS (Safety Instrumented System): $PFD_3 = 1 \times 10^{-2}$

$$PFD_{total} = 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2} = 1 \times 10^{-4} \Rightarrow \mathbf{SIL\ 3}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Risk Assessment Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Identify Hazards] --> stepB[Assess Consequences]
    stepB --> stepC[Assess Likelihood]
    stepC --> stepD[Calculate Risk Score]
    stepD --> stepE{Compare with Criteria}
    stepE -->|Negligible| endF[Accept - No Action]
    stepE -->|Tolerable ALARP| stepG[Apply Cost-Benefit]
    stepE -->|Unacceptable| stepH[Implement Safeguards]
    stepG -->|Disproportion| endF
    stepG -->|Reasonable| stepH
    stepH --> stepI[Reassess Risk]
    stepI --> stepE
```

### 4.2 ALARP / Risk Tolerance Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeU[Intolerable Risk Zone]:::top
    nodeT[Tolerable if ALARP Zone]:::mid
    nodeN[Negligible Risk Zone]:::bot

    nodeU -->|Reduce at any cost| nodeT
    nodeT -->|Cost grossly disproportionate| nodeN
    nodeT -.->|Reduce by reasonable means| nodeT

    classDef top fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#900,color:#fff
    classDef mid fill:#ffd166,stroke:#a60,color:#000
    classDef bot fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#063,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Bow-Tie Risk Reduction Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cause1[Cause A: Pump Failure]:::c
    cause2[Cause B: Valve Stuck]:::c
    cause3[Cause C: Sensor Drift]:::c

    topEvent[Top Event: Overpressure]:::te

    cons1[Consequence 1: Fire]:::co
    cons2[Consequence 2: Explosion]:::co

    p1[Layer 1: BPCS Control]:::pl
    p2[Layer 2: SIS SIL 2]:::pl
    p3[Layer 3: Relief Valve]:::pl
    p4[Layer 4: Bund / Dike]:::pl

    cause1 --> topEvent
    cause2 --> topEvent
    cause3 --> topEvent

    topEvent --> cons1
    topEvent --> cons2

    p1 -.prevents.-> topEvent
    p2 -.prevents.-> topEvent
    p3 -.prevents.-> topEvent
    p4 -.mitigates.-> cons1
    p4 -.mitigates.-> cons2

    classDef c fill:#a3c4f3,stroke:#003
    classDef te fill:#ff595e,stroke:#900,color:#fff
    classDef co fill:#ffca3a,stroke:#a60
    classDef pl fill:#8ac926,stroke:#060
```

### 4.4 SIL Determination Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Hazard Identified] --> stepB[Determine Consequence Severity]
    stepB --> stepC[Estimate Event Frequency]
    stepC --> stepD[Calculate Unmitigated Risk]
    stepD --> stepE[Subtract Existing IPLs]
    stepE --> stepF[Compute Residual Risk]
    stepF --> stepG{Tolerable?}
    stepG -->|Yes| endH[No SIS needed]
    stepG -->|No| stepI[Required Risk Reduction]
    stepI --> stepJ[Calculate Required PFD]
    stepJ --> stepK[Map to SIL Table]
    stepK --> endL[Design SIL n SIS]
```

### 4.5 ALARP Cost-Benefit Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Residual Risk Computed] --> stepB[Identify Further Reduction Options]
    stepB --> stepC[Cost of Each Option]
    stepC --> stepD[Benefit of Each Option]
    stepD --> stepE{Cost Disproportionate?}
    stepE -->|Yes| endF[ALARP Achieved - Stop]
    stepE -->|No| stepG[Implement Option]
    stepG --> stepH[Re-evaluate Residual Risk]
    stepH --> stepE
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1. Define risk. Explain the three methods used to measure risk.**

**Model Answer:**

Risk is the combination of the *probability* of a hazardous event occurring and the *severity* of its consequences. It is mathematically expressed as $R = f \times C$.

The three methods are:

1. **Qualitative:** Uses descriptive categories (Low/Medium/High) for likelihood and severity, typically displayed on a risk matrix. Used in early design when numerical data is scarce.
2. **Semi-Quantitative:** Assigns numerical scores to categories (1–5) and computes a Risk Score = Likelihood × Severity. Useful for ranking many hazards.
3. **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA):** Uses actual numerical frequency (events/year) and consequence (fatalities, ₹ loss). Uses tools like Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) and Event Tree Analysis (ETA). Used in high-hazard industries such as oil & gas and nuclear.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. What is Safety Integrity Level (SIL)? State the PFD ranges for SIL 1, 2, 3 and 4 in low demand mode.**

**Model Answer:**

SIL is a discrete level (1 to 4) defined by **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511** that specifies the safety integrity requirements of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF).

In **low demand mode**, the integrity is measured by the Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD):

| SIL | PFD Range |
|---|---|
| **SIL 1** | $10^{-2} \leq PFD < 10^{-1}$ |
| **SIL 2** | $10^{-3} \leq PFD < 10^{-2}$ |
| **SIL 3** | $10^{-4} \leq PFD < 10^{-3}$ |
| **SIL 4** | $10^{-5} \leq PFD < 10^{-4}$ |

Higher SIL means **lower probability of failure**, i.e., higher reliability.

---

### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Module 3 – Choice A]**

**Q. A) (a)** Define risk tolerance. Explain the ALARP principle with a suitable cost-benefit illustration. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hazardous event has a frequency of **0.05 events/year**. Without any protection, it would result in **3 fatalities**. Calculate the required PFD if the tolerable risk is **$1 \times 10^{-4}$ fatalities/year**. What SIL level is required? **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – Risk Tolerance & ALARP**

Risk Tolerance is the maximum level of risk that an organization or society is willing to accept in pursuit of its objectives. It is the boundary between acceptable and unacceptable risk.

The **ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable)** principle states that residual risk must be reduced to a level that is "as low as reasonably practicable" – meaning any further reduction would involve cost grossly disproportionate to the safety benefit.

**Cost-Benefit Illustration:**

A plant has a residual fatality risk of $2 \times 10^{-4}$/year. A safeguard reduces it to $5 \times 10^{-5}$/year. Cost of safeguard = ₹2 Crore. VPF = ₹5 Crore.

- $\Delta R = (2 - 0.5) \times 10^{-4} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ fatalities/year
- Annual Benefit $= 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 5 \times 10^{7} = ₹7{,}500/$ year
- Disproportion Ratio $= 2 \times 10^{7} / 7.5 \times 10^{3} \approx 2667$

Since 2667 ≫ 10 (HSE gross-disproportion test), the risk is **ALARP** – no further measures needed.

**Part (b) – SIL Calculation**

**Step 1: Unmitigated Risk**

$$R_{unmitigated} = 0.05 \times 3 = 0.15 \text{ fatalities/year}$$

**Step 2: Required Risk Reduction Factor**

$$RRF = \dfrac{R_{unmitigated}}{R_{tolerable}} = \dfrac{0.15}{1 \times 10^{-4}} = 1500$$

**Step 3: Required PFD**

$$PFD_{required} = \dfrac{1}{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{1500} \approx 6.67 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4: Map to SIL Table**

Since $6.67 \times 10^{-4}$ lies in the range $10^{-4} \leq PFD < 10^{-3}$, the required SIL is **SIL 3**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, Module 3 – Choice B]**

**Q. B) (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Bow-Tie Method** of risk assessment. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An industrial process has three independent protection layers with $PFD_1 = 1 \times 10^{-1}$, $PFD_2 = 1 \times 10^{-2}$, and $PFD_3 = 1 \times 10^{-1}$. Calculate the overall $PFD_{total}$ and identify the equivalent SIL level. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – Bow-Tie Method**

The Bow-Tie method is a visual risk assessment tool that combines **Fault Tree Analysis** (causes on the left) and **Event Tree Analysis** (consequences on the right), joined at the central **Top Event**.

- **Left side (Threats → Top Event):** Identifies all possible causes leading to the hazardous event.
- **Right side (Top Event → Consequences):** Identifies all possible outcomes if the event occurs.
- **Preventive barriers (left):** Stop the event from happening (e.g., BPCS, alarms, SIS).
- **Mitigative barriers (right):** Reduce the severity of the consequences (e.g., fire suppression, bunds, evacuation).

The Bow-Tie clearly shows the *pathway* from cause to consequence and identifies the *safety barriers* (IPL – Independent Protection Layers) that interrupt the chain.

**Part (b) – Combined PFD Calculation**

For independent protection layers in series, the total PFD is the product of individual PFDs:

$$PFD_{total} = PFD_1 \times PFD_2 \times PFD_3$$

Substituting:

$$PFD_{total} = (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1 \times 10^{-1})$$

$$PFD_{total} = 1 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Equivalent SIL:**

Since $1 \times 10^{-4}$ lies in the range $10^{-4} \leq PFD < 10^{-3}$, the combined protection layers achieve **SIL 3** integrity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing SIL with PFD direction:** Higher SIL = LOWER PFD. Students often write it backwards.
> 2. **Ignoring the "boundary" condition:** If $PFD = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ *exactly*, it belongs to SIL 3 (lower bound inclusive). Off-by-one errors cost marks.
> 3. **Skipping the unit conversion:** Always convert event frequency to **per-year** units before computing RRF.
> 4. **Mixing high-demand and low-demand SIL tables:** The $PFH$ table is *not* the same as the $PFD_{avg}$ table. Pick the correct one based on demand mode.
> 5. **Forgetting to state ALARP verdict explicitly:** You must say "ALARP demonstrated" or "ALARP not demonstrated" – not just compute the ratio.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Risk, Risk Tolerance & SIL:**

- ☐ **Risk = Frequency × Consequence** – the universal industrial definition.
- ☐ Three measurement methods: **Qualitative, Semi-Quantitative, Quantitative (QRA)**.
- ☐ **Individual Risk** = risk to one person; **Societal Risk** = risk to a group (F-N curves).
- ☐ **ALARP = As Low As Reasonably Practicable** – applies cost-benefit test.
- ☐ Risk Tolerance bands: **Negligible → Tolerable (ALARP) → Unacceptable**.
- ☐ **SIL** is defined by **IEC 61508** (generic) and **IEC 61511** (process industries).
- ☐ **Low demand mode** → use $PFD_{avg}$; **High demand mode** → use $PFH$.
- ☐ Memorize the $PFD$ ranges: SIL 1 = $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$, SIL 2 = $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$, SIL 3 = $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$, SIL 4 = $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$.
- ☐ **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) = 1/PFD**.
- ☐ **Bow-Tie method** combines fault tree (causes) + event tree (consequences) around a Top Event.
- ☐ **Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)** combine multiplicatively: $PFD_{total} = \prod PFD_i$.
- ☐ When $PFD$ is required to be tighter (smaller), the required SIL is higher.
- ☐ Most process industry SIFs operate at **SIL 1, SIL 2, or SIL 3** – SIL 4 is rare and used in nuclear/aerospace.
- ☐ Always convert frequency to **per-year units** before any RRF or PFD calculation in the exam.
- ☐ Mention **ALARP verdict explicitly** in cost-benefit numerical answers to fetch full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
