> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT743 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ANDSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Safety Instrumented Functions - Standards and Regulation - HSE-PES, AICHE-CCPS, IEC 61508, ANSI/ISA-84.00.01-2004 (IEC 61511 Mod) and ANSI/ISA-84.01-1996, NFPA 85, API RP 556, API RP 14C, OSHA (29 CFR 1910.119) - Process Safety Management of Highly Hazardous Chemicals - SIS Design Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)

**Formal KTU Definition:** A **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** is a single safety loop designed to achieve a specific Safety Instrumented Level (SIL) and to bring the process to a safe state when a hazardous condition is detected. A SIF consists of one or more sensors, a logic solver, and one or more final control elements that together implement the required safety function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A SIF is *one* complete safety loop (sensor → logic solver → final element). An **SIS (Safety Instrumented System)** is the *complete implementation* of one or more SIFs.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a SIF like the airbag system in a car:
- **Sensors** = crash impact sensors
- **Logic Solver** = the airbag control unit
- **Final Element** = the airbag inflator
- **Safe State** = inflated airbag protecting passengers

When the "hazard" (crash) is detected, the system *automatically* moves the car to a "safe state" (cushioned crash). It does NOT need the driver — it acts independently. That's exactly what a SIF does in a chemical plant.

---

### 1.2 Standards and Regulatory Bodies — Overview

| Body | Standard / Publication | Scope |
|------|------------------------|-------|
| HSE (UK) | PES (Programmable Electronic Systems) | General guidance for PES in safety-related applications |
| AIChE | CCPS Guidelines | Process safety management best practices |
| IEC | IEC 61508 | Functional safety of E/E/PE systems (umbrella standard) |
| ISA | ANSI/ISA-84.00.01-2004 (IEC 61511 Mod) | Functional safety in process industries |
| ISA | ANSI/ISA-84.01-1996 | Earlier standard superseded by 84.00.01 |
| NFPA | NFPA 85 | Boiler and Combustion Systems Hazards Code |
| API | RP 556 | Instrumentation and Control for Fired Heaters |
| API | RP 14C | Offshore production platform safety systems |
| OSHA | 29 CFR 1910.119 | Process Safety Management of Highly Hazardous Chemicals (US federal law) |

---

### 1.3 Process Safety Management (PSM) — Core Definition

**OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** defines PSM as the application of management principles, systems, and practices to ensure the integrity of operating decisions and manufacturing processes through the management of risk.

> [!NOTE]
> **14 Elements of PSM (OSHA 1910.119)** — A favorite KTU 2-mark question:
> 1. Employee Participation
> 2. Process Safety Information
> 3. Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)
> 4. Operating Procedures
> 5. Training
> 6. Contractors
> 7. Pre-Startup Safety Review
> 8. Mechanical Integrity
> 9. Hot Work Permit
> 10. Management of Change (MOC)
> 11. Incident Investigation
> 12. Emergency Planning and Response
> 13. Compliance Audits
> 14. Trade Secrets
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 IEC 61508 — The Functional Safety Umbrella

IEC 61508 is the **international umbrella standard** for functional safety of Electrical/Electronic/Programmable Electronic (E/E/PE) safety-related systems. It is generic and applies across ALL industries (process, nuclear, machinery, medical).

**Key Concepts:**
- **Safety Lifecycle:** Covers the entire life from concept to decommissioning
- **Safety Integrity Level (SIL):** Discrete levels (1–4) defining the required risk reduction
- **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD):** Average probability the SIF fails to operate when demanded

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T PFD(t) \, dt$$

**SIL Targets (IEC 61508):**

| SIL | PFDavg Target (Low Demand) | Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) |
|:---:|:-------------------------:|:--------------------------:|
| 1 | $\geq 10^{-2}$ to $< 10^{-1}$ | 10 – 100 |
| 2 | $\geq 10^{-3}$ to $< 10^{-2}$ | 100 – 1000 |
| 3 | $\geq 10^{-4}$ to $< 10^{-3}$ | 1000 – 10,000 |
| 4 | $\geq 10^{-5}$ to $< 10^{-4}$ | 10,000 – 100,000 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** "**1 in 100**" → SIL 1, "**1 in 1000**" → SIL 2, "**1 in 10,000**" → SIL 3, "**1 in 100,000**" → SIL 4.

---

### 2.2 IEC 61511 (ANSI/ISA-84.00.01-2004)

This is the **process-sector application** of IEC 61508. It provides:
- SIS Safety Lifecycle requirements
- SIL determination methodology
- Documentation requirements
- Management of Change and Operational procedures

**ISA 84.01-1996** was the **predecessor** that defined SIL concepts but lacked prescriptive requirements. **ISA 84.00.01-2004** adopted IEC 61511 and added rigor.

---

### 2.3 HSE-PES (Programmable Electronic Systems)

The UK Health and Safety Executive (HSE) published PES guidelines in 1987 for the use of programmable electronics in safety-critical applications. **Key principle:** A safety-related PES must have the **same or higher level of integrity** as the system it replaces.

---

### 2.4 AIChE-CCPS Guidelines

The **Center for Chemical Process Safety (CCPS)** of the American Institute of Chemical Engineers publishes:
- *Guidelines for Process Safety* (series of books)
- *Guidelines for Engineering Design for Process Safety*
- **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** — semi-quantitative method for SIL selection

**LOPA Equation (Core KTU Formula):**

$$\text{Required Risk Reduction} = \frac{\text{Initiating Event Frequency} \times \text{Consequence Severity}}{\text{Tolerable Risk}}$$

This gives the **PFD** required, which maps directly to a SIL.

---

### 2.5 NFPA 85 — Boiler and Combustion Systems Hazards Code

Applies to **boilers, furnaces, and combustion systems**. Key chapters:
- Chapter 4 — Single Burner Boilers
- Chapter 6 — Stoker-Fired Boilers
- Chapter 7 — Pulverized Fuel Systems
- Chapter 9 — Fired Heaters (related to API 556)

Defines **BMS (Burner Management System)** requirements including flame monitoring, permissives, and interlocks.

---

### 2.6 API RP 556 & API RP 14C

**API RP 556:** Instrumentation and Control for Fired Heaters — recommends safety instrumentation for fired process heaters including emergency shutdown (ESD) systems.

**API RP 14C:** Analysis, Design, Installation, and Testing of Basic Surface Safety Systems for Offshore Production Platforms. Introduces the **"1-out-of-N" voting concept** and standardizes shutdown logic for offshore platforms.

---

### 2.7 OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 — PSM Application

PSM applies to processes involving **threshold quantities** of listed highly hazardous chemicals (e.g., 10,000 lbs of flammable liquids/gases, 1,500 lbs of chlorine).

**Applicability Threshold:** A process containing more than the threshold quantity of a regulated substance requires a full PSM program.

---

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Reference |
|:------:|:--------|:-------------------|
| $PFD_{avg}$ | Average Probability of Failure on Demand | $\frac{\lambda_D \cdot T}{2}$ (for simple SIF) |
| $RRF$ | Risk Reduction Factor | $\frac{1}{PFD_{avg}}$ |
| $SIL$ | Safety Integrity Level | $\log_{10}(RRF)$ target band |
| $PFD_{SIF}$ | Total SIF PFD | $PFD_{S} + PFD_{L} + PFD_{FE}$ (sensor + logic + final element) |
| $\lambda_D$ | Dangerous Failure Rate | failures per hour |
| $T$ | Proof Test Interval | hours |
| $MTTF$ | Mean Time To Failure | $\frac{1}{\lambda}$ |
| $SFF$ | Safe Failure Fraction | $\frac{\lambda_S}{\lambda_S + \lambda_D}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A SIF is *one* loop with *one* SIL. If you stack multiple SIFs, each maintains its OWN SIL — they don't add together.

---

### 2.9 SIS Design Cycle — The 12 Phases

The IEC 61511 / ISA 84 safety lifecycle consists of:

1. **Concept & Scope Definition** — Identify the need for SIS
2. **Hazard & Risk Assessment (PHA/LOPA)** — Determine required risk reduction
3. **Allocation of Safety Functions to Protection Layers**
4. **SIL Determination** — Assign SIL 1–4 to each SIF
5. **SIS Design & Engineering** — Architecture, redundancy, voting
6. **Installation, Commissioning & Validation**
7. **Operation & Maintenance**
8. **Modification & Management of Change (MOC)**
9. **Decommissioning**

The **Operating Phase** includes:
- Proof Testing
- Functional Safety Audits
- Documentation updates
- Incident investigation
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of PFD for a Simple 1oo1 (One-out-of-One) Architecture

**Step 1:** Define dangerous failure rate
A dangerous failure is one that prevents the SIF from executing its safety action on demand. Let $\lambda_D$ be the constant dangerous failure rate.

**Step 2:** Probability of failure at time $t$
For a non-repaired component, the unreliability (probability it has failed dangerously by time $t$) is:

$$R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_D t}$$

**Step 3:** Linearize for small $\lambda_D t$
Since $\lambda_D \cdot t \ll 1$ over typical mission times:

$$R(t) \approx \lambda_D \cdot t$$

**Step 4:** Average over the proof test interval $T$
Assuming the component is repaired/replaced after each test, the average probability of failure over the interval $[0, T]$ is:

$$PFD_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T \lambda_D \cdot t \, dt$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the integral

$$\begin{aligned}
PFD_{avg} &= \frac{1}{T} \cdot \left[ \frac{\lambda_D t^2}{2} \right]_0^T \\
&= \frac{1}{T} \cdot \frac{\lambda_D T^2}{2} \\
&= \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6:** Final Result

$$\boxed{PFD_{avg} = \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T}{2}}$$

**Real-world interpretation:** Doubling the proof test interval ($T$) *doubles* the PFD, *worsening* the SIL by approximately one order of magnitude.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of PFD for a 1oo2 (One-out-of-Two) Architecture

**Step 1:** Architecture description
Two identical channels operate in parallel. The system fails ONLY if both channels fail dangerously (since either one can perform the safety action).

**Step 2:** Unreliability of each channel
For each channel: $R_1(t) \approx \lambda_D t$ and $R_2(t) \approx \lambda_D t$.

**Step 3:** System unreliability (both failed)
For independent failures:

$$R_{system}(t) = R_1(t) \cdot R_2(t) = (\lambda_D t)^2$$

**Step 4:** Average PFD

$$\begin{aligned}
PFD_{avg,1oo2} &= \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T (\lambda_D t)^2 \, dt \\
&= \frac{1}{T} \cdot \lambda_D^2 \cdot \left[ \frac{t^3}{3} \right]_0^T \\
&= \frac{1}{T} \cdot \lambda_D^2 \cdot \frac{T^3}{3} \\
&= \frac{\lambda_D^2 \cdot T^2}{3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Final Result

$$\boxed{PFD_{avg,1oo2} = \frac{\lambda_D^2 \cdot T^2}{3}}$$

**Comparison:**
- 1oo1: $PFD \propto T$
- 1oo2: $PFD \propto T^2$ — much safer for long proof test intervals.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-mark style)

**Problem:** A pressure transmitter in a SIF has $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hour. The proof test interval is 2 years.
- (a) Calculate $PFD_{avg}$ assuming 1oo1 voting.
- (b) Determine the achievable SIL.
- (c) If a redundant 1oo2 architecture is used with the same transmitter, calculate the new $PFD_{avg}$ and SIL.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):**
$T = 2 \text{ years} = 2 \times 8760 = 17{,}520 \text{ hours}$

$$PFD_{avg,1oo1} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-6})(17{,}520)}{2} = \frac{0.0876}{2} = 0.0438$$

**Part (b):**
$0.0438$ lies in the range $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$, so this corresponds to **SIL 1**.

**Part (c):**

$$PFD_{avg,1oo2} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-6})^2 \cdot (17{,}520)^2}{3}$$

$$= \frac{(25 \times 10^{-12})(3.0695 \times 10^{8})}{3}$$

$$= \frac{7.674 \times 10^{-3}}{3} = 2.56 \times 10^{-3}$$

This lies in the range $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ → **SIL 2** is achieved!

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Adding redundancy (1oo2 instead of 1oo1) moved the system from SIL 1 to SIL 2 *without* changing the transmitter itself — just by changing the voting architecture.

---

### 3.4 LOPA-Based SIL Determination (Analytical Matrix)

**Step-by-step LOPA procedure:**

| Step | Action | Equation / Logic |
|:----:|:-------|:----------------|
| 1 | Identify the Scenario | e.g., Pump overpressure → vessel rupture |
| 2 | Determine Consequence Severity | e.g., $1 \times 10^{-3}$ fatalities/yr tolerable |
| 3 | Find Initiating Event Frequency (IEF) | e.g., pump failure = $0.1$/yr |
| 4 | Subtract Independent Protection Layers (IPLs) | Alarms (0.1), Operator (0.1), Relief Valve (0.01) |
| 5 | Compute Frequency with Existing IPLs | $0.1 \times 0.1 \times 0.1 \times 0.01 = 1 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| 6 | Compare with Tolerable Frequency | $1 \times 10^{-5} < 1 \times 10^{-3}$ → already tolerable → no SIF needed |
| 7 | If not tolerable, compute required PFD | $PFD_{req} = \frac{f_{tolerable}}{f_{mitigated}}$ |

**Final SIL Selection Formula:**

$$SIL = \left\lceil -\log_{10}(PFD_{req}) \right\rceil$$

where the ceiling brackets map the PFD range to discrete SIL bands.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IEC 61508 / 61511 Safety Lifecycle (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Phase 1: Concept] --> node2[Phase 2: Scope Definition]
    node2 --> node3[Phase 3: Hazard & Risk Analysis]
    node3 --> node4[Phase 4: Overall Safety Requirements]
    node4 --> node5[Phase 5: SIS Safety Requirements]
    node5 --> node6[Phase 6: SIS Design & Engineering]
    node6 --> node7[Phase 7: Installation Commissioning Validation]
    node7 --> node8[Phase 8: Operation and Maintenance]
    node8 --> node9[Phase 9: Modification Management of Change]
    node9 --> node10[Phase 10: Decommissioning]
    node8 --> node11[Phase 11: Proof Testing]
    node8 --> node12[Phase 12: Functional Safety Audit]
```

### 4.2 Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA) — Independent Protection Layers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIE[Initiating Event] --> nodeIPL1[Layer 1 Process Design]
    nodeIPL1 --> nodeIPL2[Layer 2 Basic Process Control]
    nodeIPL2 --> nodeIPL3[Layer 3 Critical Alarm Operator]
    nodeIPL3 --> nodeIPL4[Layer 4 Safety Instrumented System]
    nodeIPL4 --> nodeIPL5[Layer 5 Relief Devices]
    nodeIPL5 --> nodeIPL6[Layer 6 Post Release Containment]
    nodeIPL6 --> nodeOutcome[Final Consequence]
```

### 4.3 SIF Block Diagram — Sensor, Logic Solver, Final Element

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeFS[Field Sensor Pressure Transmitter] --> nodeLS[Safety Logic Solver PLC or Relay]
    nodeLS --> nodeFE[Final Control Element Block Valve]
    nodeFE --> nodeSS[Safe State Isolated Process]
    nodeH[Process Hazard] -.-> nodeFS
```

### 4.4 SIS Design Cycle — Iterative Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Identify Hazards PHA] --> nodeB[Risk Assessment QRA or LOPA]
    nodeB --> nodeC{SIF Required}
    nodeC -->|Yes| nodeD[SIL Determination]
    nodeC -->|No| nodeE[Document Justification]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Architecture Selection Voting]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Component Selection and SFF]
    nodeG --> nodeH[PFD Calculation]
    nodeH --> nodeI{SIL Achieved}
    nodeI -->|Yes| nodeJ[Validation and Verification]
    nodeI -->|No| nodeF
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Operation Maintenance Proof Testing]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Management of Change]
    nodeL --> nodeA
```

### 4.5 Standards Hierarchy Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeIEC8[IEC 61508 Umbrella Standard E/E/PE] --> nodeIEC11[IEC 61511 Process Industries]
    nodeIEC8 --> nodeIEC9[IEC 62061 Machinery]
    nodeIEC8 --> nodeIEC13[IEC 61513 Nuclear]
    nodeIEC11 --> nodeISA1[ANSI or ISA 84.00.01 2004 USA]
    nodeIEC11 --> nodeISA2[ANSI or ISA 84.01 1996 Legacy]
    nodeIEC11 --> nodeAPI1[API RP 14C Offshore]
    nodeIEC11 --> nodeAPI2[API RP 556 Fired Heaters]
    nodeNFPA[NFPA 85 Boilers Combustion] -.-> nodeIEC11
    nodeOSHA[OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 PSM Federal Law] -.-> nodeIEC11
    nodeHSE[HSE PES Guidelines UK] -.-> nodeIEC8
    nodeCCPS[AIChE CCPS LOPA Guidelines] -.-> nodeIEC11
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows indicate direct derivation (e.g., IEC 61511 derives from IEC 61508). Dashed arrows indicate cross-references / regulatory linkage.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
Define a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) and list its three main components.

**Model Answer:**
A Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) is a single, complete safety loop designed to bring a process to a safe state when a specified hazardous condition is detected. It implements a specific Safety Integrity Level (SIL).

The three main components are:
1. **Sensor(s)** — detect the hazardous condition (e.g., pressure transmitter)
2. **Logic Solver** — processes sensor inputs and decides the safety action (e.g., safety PLC)
3. **Final Control Element(s)** — execute the safety action (e.g., shut-down valve)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
List the 14 elements of OSHA Process Safety Management (PSM).

**Model Answer:**
According to OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119, the 14 elements are:
1. Employee Participation
2. Process Safety Information
3. Process Hazard Analysis
4. Operating Procedures
5. Training
6. Contractors
7. Pre-Startup Safety Review
8. Mechanical Integrity
9. Hot Work Permit
10. Management of Change
11. Incident Investigation
12. Emergency Planning and Response
13. Compliance Audits
14. Trade Secrets

---

### 📕 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the IEC 61508 functional safety standard in detail. Discuss the relationship between Safety Integrity Level (SIL) and Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD). *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A safety instrumented function uses a level transmitter with dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D = 3 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour. The proof test interval is 1 year.
- (i) Calculate $PFD_{avg}$ for a 1oo1 architecture.
- (ii) Calculate $PFD_{avg}$ for a 1oo2 architecture.
- (iii) Identify the SIL achieved in each case. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) IEC 61508 Explanation:**

IEC 61508 is the international umbrella standard for **functional safety** of Electrical, Electronic, and Programmable Electronic (E/E/PE) safety-related systems. Published by the International Electrotechnical Commission, it provides a generic framework applicable across all industries — process, machinery, nuclear, and medical.

**Key Features:**
- **Safety Lifecycle Approach:** Defines 16 phases covering concept, design, implementation, operation, and decommissioning.
- **Risk-Based SIL Allocation:** Requires risk assessment to determine required safety function and assign a Safety Integrity Level.
- **SIL Definition:** A discrete level (1 to 4) corresponding to a range of target failure probabilities. Higher SIL = greater risk reduction.
- **PFD Relationship:** For low-demand operation, PFDavg quantifies the average probability that the SIF fails when a demand occurs. SIL is mapped from PFDavg bands:
  - SIL 1: $10^{-2} \leq PFD < 10^{-1}$
  - SIL 2: $10^{-3} \leq PFD < 10^{-2}$
  - SIL 3: $10^{-4} \leq PFD < 10^{-3}$
  - SIL 4: $10^{-5} \leq PFD < 10^{-4}$

The **lower the PFD**, the **higher the SIL** — they are inversely related logarithmically.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $\lambda_D = 3 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr; $T = 1 \text{ year} = 8760$ hours.

**(i) 1oo1 Architecture:**

$$PFD_{avg,1oo1} = \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T}{2} = \frac{(3 \times 10^{-6})(8760)}{2} = \frac{0.02628}{2} = 0.01314$$

**(ii) 1oo2 Architecture:**

$$PFD_{avg,1oo2} = \frac{\lambda_D^2 \cdot T^2}{3} = \frac{(3 \times 10^{-6})^2 \cdot (8760)^2}{3}$$

$$= \frac{(9 \times 10^{-12})(7.6738 \times 10^{7})}{3} = \frac{6.906 \times 10^{-4}}{3} = 2.302 \times 10^{-4}$$

**(iii) SIL Identification:**

- **1oo1:** $PFD_{avg} = 0.01314 \approx 1.3 \times 10^{-2}$ → falls in $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ band → **SIL 1**
- **1oo2:** $PFD_{avg} = 2.3 \times 10^{-4} \approx 2.3 \times 10^{-4}$ → falls in $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ band → **SIL 2**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall:**
> 1. **Unit confusion** — Always convert proof test interval $T$ to **hours** (8760 hr/yr), NOT years. (Lose 1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting to square the failure rate in 1oo2** — Students often write $(\lambda_D \cdot T)^2 / 3$ incorrectly. The correct form is $(\lambda_D^2 \cdot T^2)/3$. (Lose 2 marks)
> 3. **SIL boundary confusion** — A $PFD$ of exactly $10^{-2}$ belongs to SIL 2 (lower bound inclusive), NOT SIL 1. The range is $\geq$ lower bound, $<$ upper bound.

---

**Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Describe the SIS Design Cycle as per IEC 61511. Explain each phase briefly. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of LOPA, explain how the required SIL for a SIF is determined. A reactor overpressure scenario has an initiating event frequency of $0.05$ per year. The tolerable risk frequency is $1 \times 10^{-4}$ per year. Existing independent protection layers include: Process Control (PFD = 0.1) and Operator Intervention (PFD = 0.1). Determine the required SIL for the SIF. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) SIS Design Cycle (IEC 61511):**

The IEC 61511 Safety Lifecycle consists of the following phases:

1. **Concept Phase:** Identify the need for safety instrumentation. Define scope and objectives.
2. **Scope Definition:** Define boundaries of the equipment under consideration (EUC) and EUC control system.
3. **Hazard & Risk Assessment:** Conduct PHA, HAZOP, or QRA to identify hazards and estimate risk.
4. **Overall Safety Requirements:** Specify overall risk reduction targets and allocate safety functions.
5. **Safety Requirement Specification:** Define SIL for each SIF, response time, and operating conditions.
6. **SIS Design & Engineering:** Select architecture (1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo3), components, voting logic.
7. **Installation, Commissioning & Validation:** Verify that installed SIS meets design specifications.
8. **Operation & Maintenance:** Document procedures, training, proof testing.
9. **Modification & Management of Change (MOC):** Formal process for any changes affecting safety.
10. **Decommissioning:** Safe removal of SIS at end of life.

**Key Concept:** The lifecycle is **iterative** — any change in operation triggers a return to phase 3 (hazard re-assessment).

**(b) LOPA Calculation:**

Given:
- Initiating Event Frequency (IEF) = $0.05$/yr
- Tolerable Frequency (TF) = $1 \times 10^{-4}$/yr
- IPL 1 (Process Control) PFD = $0.1$
- IPL 2 (Operator Intervention) PFD = $0.1$

**Step 1:** Calculate mitigated frequency with existing IPLs:

$$f_{mitigated} = IEF \times PFD_{IPL1} \times PFD_{IPL2} = 0.05 \times 0.1 \times 0.1 = 5 \times 10^{-4} / yr$$

**Step 2:** Compare with tolerable:

$$f_{mitigated} = 5 \times 10^{-4} > 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ (tolerable)}$$

→ **Risk too high — additional protection layer (SIF) needed.**

**Step 3:** Calculate required PFD for the SIF:

$$PFD_{req} = \frac{TF}{f_{mitigated}} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-4}}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.2$$

**Step 4:** Map to SIL:
$PFD_{req} = 0.2 = 2 \times 10^{-1}$ → falls in range $10^{-1}$ to $10^{0}$ (i.e., higher than $10^{-1}$).

Since $PFD_{req} \geq 0.1$ and $< 1$, this is **below SIL 1** (i.e., a basic process control or alarm level is sufficient). However, by KTU convention, the SIF would be classified as **SIL 0 (or non-SIF)**, meaning a properly engineered basic control loop is enough.

If the tolerable frequency were **$1 \times 10^{-5}$/yr**, then:
$PFD_{req} = (1 \times 10^{-5})/(5 \times 10^{-4}) = 0.02$ → $2 \times 10^{-2}$ → **SIL 1**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall:**
> 1. **LOPA multiplication order** — IPL PFDs are multiplied, NOT added. (Lose 2 marks if added)
> 2. **Comparison direction** — If $f_{mitigated} < TF$, NO SIF is needed. Many students compute but forget to compare.
> 3. **SIL 0 confusion** — When $PFD_{req} > 0.1$, the requirement is below SIL 1. This means a basic system suffices.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

✅ **SIF = one safety loop; SIS = implementation of one or more SIFs.**

✅ **IEC 61508 = Umbrella standard; IEC 61511 = Process industries application.**

✅ **ISA 84.00.01-2004 = IEC 61511 modified for USA; ISA 84.01-1996 = legacy standard.**

✅ **PFD Formulae:**
- 1oo1: $PFD = \frac{\lambda_D T}{2}$
- 1oo2: $PFD = \frac{\lambda_D^2 T^2}{3}$

✅ **SIL Mapping (Low Demand):**
- SIL 1: $10^{-2} \leq PFD < 10^{-1}$
- SIL 2: $10^{-3} \leq PFD < 10^{-2}$
- SIL 3: $10^{-4} \leq PFD < 10^{-3}$
- SIL 4: $10^{-5} \leq PFD < 10^{-4}$

✅ **OSHA 1910.119 = US Federal Law; CCPS = best practices; HSE = UK guidance; NFPA 85 = boilers; API 14C = offshore; API 556 = fired heaters.**

✅ **LOPA Equation:** $PFD_{req} = \frac{Tolerable\ Frequency}{Initiating\ Event\ Frequency \times IPL\ PFDs}$

✅ **14 PSM Elements** — Memorize for sure-shot 3-mark questions.

✅ **SIS Design Cycle Phases** — Mention "iterative" and "management of change" for full marks.

✅ **Redundancy improves SIL** — 1oo2 is safer than 1oo1 by a factor of $\sim \frac{3}{2\lambda_D T}$.

✅ **Proof testing is mandatory** — Doubling T doubles PFD in 1oo1 architecture.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Containment Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT743 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Containment Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Containment System** in the context of industrial safety is a deliberately engineered physical barrier—comprising structures, vessels, coatings, seals, and engineered passive elements—designed to **prevent, control, capture, and isolate the unintended release of hazardous substances** (flammable liquids, toxic chemicals, radioactive materials, pressurized gases, or fire-extinguishing agents) into the workplace, the surrounding community, or the natural environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Containment is a *defense-in-depth* engineering strategy where successive independent layers of physical barriers are erected around a hazard such that, even if one barrier fails, the next layer continues to control the hazard — limiting the **escalation pathway** of an accident (fire → explosion → toxic release → environmental contamination).

The principal function of containment is to **break the accident propagation chain** by providing physical confinement, secondary capture, and tertiary control for:

- **Loss of Containment (LOC)** of hydrocarbons, solvents, acids, alkalis, cryogens, and LPG
- **Runaway chemical reactions** producing runaway pressure or temperature
- **Pool fires, jet fires, and vapor cloud explosions (VCE)**
- **Uncontrolled dispersion of toxic vapors and aerosols**
- **Leakage from underground storage tanks (UST)** into soil and groundwater

## 1.2 Hierarchical Classification of Containment

The **Three-Tier Hierarchy of Containment** (per KTU Module 4 syllabus) is summarized below.

| Tier | Name | Function | Typical Element | Failure Consequence |
|------|------|----------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **Tier 1** | Primary Containment | First barrier directly holding the process fluid | Pressure vessel, storage tank, piping, gasket | Triggers Tier 2 activation |
| **Tier 2** | Secondary Containment | Backup barrier if Tier 1 fails | Bund wall, dike, double-walled tank, catch basin | Triggers Tier 3 activation |
| **Tier 3** | Tertiary Containment | Final catchment at plant boundary | Retention pond, levee, foam blanket, vapor suppression curtain | Prevents off-site impact |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Nested Russian Doll / Matryoshka:**
>
> Imagine you are transporting a leaking bottle of acid in your car.
> - **Primary containment** = the bottle itself (it directly holds the acid).
> - **Secondary containment** = a plastic tray placed inside the car boot (catches drips if the bottle cracks).
> - **Tertiary containment** = the entire garage where the car is parked (catches acid if the tray overflows).
>
> If the bottle leaks → tray saves the car. If the tray overflows → garage saves the neighborhood. **This is precisely how industrial containment systems are layered.**

A second analogy: a **nuclear power plant's containment dome** — a thick steel-reinforced concrete shell that physically traps radioactive gases even if the reactor core fails catastrophically. The same principle scales down to a process plant's bund wall.

## 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics

- **Standard Containment Capacity:** **110%** of the volume of the *largest single tank* in a bundled area (NFPA 30 / EPA SPCC standard). With sprinklers active, this rises to **133%**.
- **Freeboard (Safety Margin):** **150 mm** minimum vertical clearance between bund wall top and stored liquid surface.
- **Design Spill Quantity (Q):** Typically taken as the **largest credible inventory release**.
- **Rainfall Accumulation Allowance:** Containment must hold a **100-year, 24-hour rainfall event** in addition to the spill (typically 100–250 mm regional design rainfall).
- **Firewater Spray Allowance:** Allowance for sprinkler water + hose streams = typically **10 L/min·m²** of protected area.

## 1.5 Why Containment Is Indispensable — Industry Statistics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Approximately **76% of major process industry accidents** begin with a *loss of containment* event (source: Marsh & McLennan, industry loss database).
> - **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.106(e)(2)(ii)** and **EPA 40 CFR 112** *mandate* secondary containment for flammable liquids above specified threshold quantities (≥ 1320 gallons of Class I liquids).
> - Inadequate bunding contributed directly to the **Buncefield (UK, 2005)** and **Jaipur (India, 2009)** petroleum depot incidents — both catastrophic because *secondary containment was absent or undersized*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Primary Containment — The First Line of Defense

Primary containment refers to the *designed envelope* directly contacting the process fluid. Its integrity is governed by mechanical design codes.

**Engineering Components:**

1. **Pressure Vessels** — designed per ASME BPVC Section VIII (Div. 1 for normal fluids, Div. 2 for high-integrity)
2. **Storage Tanks** — per API 650 (welded), API 620 (low-temperature/cryogenic), or API 12F (small shop-fabricated)
3. **Process Piping** — per ASME B31.3 (Process Piping)
4. **Pumps and Compressers** — sealed with mechanical seals (API 682 Plan 52/53A)
5. **Valves and Fittings** — bolted bonnet, welded, or quarter-turn
6. **Gaskets and Seals** — spiral-wound, ring-type-joint, kammprofile

**Design Philosophy:**
- *Leak-tightness class:* Class I (gaseous, toxic) vs. Class II (liquid, lower hazard)
- *Corrosion allowance:* typically **1.5 mm to 6 mm**
- *Material selection:* low-temperature carbon steel, stainless steel (304/316), alloy 825, Hastelloy
- *Inspection interval:* per RBI (Risk-Based Inspection) framework

## 2.2 Secondary Containment — Bunds, Dikes, and Double Walls

Secondary containment activates *only* when primary containment fails. It is the KTU Module 4 focus area.

### 2.2.1 Bund Wall (Dike) — Most Common Form

A bund is an impermeable retaining wall constructed around a tank farm or process area. The captured volume must equal:

$$V_{\text{bund}} \;\geq\; V_{\text{largest tank}} + V_{\text{rainfall}} + V_{\text{firewater}}$$

**Material of construction:**
- Reinforced concrete (most durable, ≥ 150 mm thick, sulfate-resistant cement)
- Masonry with chemical-resistant mortar
- Earthen embankment with HDPE/LLDPE geomembrane liner (for large farm bunds)
- Steel plate (for offshore modules)

**Bund Capacity Rules (NFPA 30 / EPA 40 CFR 112.8):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **V ≤ 100 m³:** Bund capacity **≥ 110%** of largest tank (no sprinklers) or **133%** (with sprinklers).
> - **V > 100 m³:** Bund capacity **≥ 110%** of *aggregate* of all tanks OR largest tank alone, depending on local code.

### 2.2.2 Double-Walled Tanks (DWT)

A tank within a tank — the outer shell provides integral secondary containment. The annular space may be:
- **Vacuum-monitored** (continuous interstitial monitoring at ~ −50 kPa gauge; alarm on leak)
- **Liquid-filled** (glycol-water or brine, dielectric sensor detects leak)
- **Dry-monitored** (pressure switch in nitrogen-purged annulus)

Used for petroleum retail stations (UL 1316 / ULC S615), chemical process tanks storing highly toxic fluids, and underground storage tanks (USTs).

### 2.2.3 Catch Basins and Sump Pits

Located at low points within process drainage. Equipped with:
- **Sloped floors** (1% to 2% gradient toward drain)
- **Sumps with submersible pumps** (Class I Div 1 rated)
- **Level interlocks** (high-high level → automatic shutdown of upstream process)
- **Oil–Water Separators (OWS)** — API 421 compliant

### 2.2.4 Spill Pallets and IBC Bunds

For smaller containers (drums, Intermediate Bulk Containers):
- Polyethylene pallets with integral sump (typically 220 L for 4-drum pallet)
- Bunded IBC stations for 1000 L tanks

## 2.3 Tertiary Containment — Last-Resort Plant Boundary

Tertiary containment manages *widespread* releases when primary *and* secondary both fail.

**Elements:**

1. **Retention Ponds / Lagoons** — large impoundments at plant boundary for firewater runoff and accidental spills. Sized for the **Maximum Credible Accident (MCA)** release.
2. **Levees and Dykes** — earthen embankments along waterway boundaries to prevent off-site migration of contaminated liquids.
3. **Vapor Suppression Foams** — AFFF (Aqueous Film-Forming Foam) or FFFP (Film-Forming FluoroProtein) applied to unignited spills to suppress vapor evolution, reducing flammable/toxic hazard zone.
4. **Emergency Dams / Inflatable Booms** — portable, rapidly deployable barriers for trench, drain, and waterway containment.

## 2.4 Containment for Fire and Explosion

> [!NOTE]
> **Containment vs. Suppression — a critical distinction for KTU exams:**
> - **Containment** = *preventing release* or *limiting its spread*
> - **Suppression** = *actively extinguishing* the fire

However, several systems serve **both** purposes simultaneously:

- **Foam Pourers/Chambers:** deliver foam onto bunded hydrocarbon pools, simultaneously containing the spill and suppressing fire
- **Flame Arresters:** prevent flashback through vent piping into tanks
- **Explosion Venting + Retention:** explosion panels vent to safe location; the vented material is captured in an *explosion retention housing*
- **Deluge Systems + Bunding:** sprinkler spray accumulates in bund; bund must account for firewater.

## 2.5 Specialized Containment Systems

| System | Industry / Application | Standard |
|--------|----------------------|----------|
| **Spent Fuel Pool Containment** | Nuclear power | 10 CFR 50.46, IAEA SSR-2/1 |
| **Flammable Liquid Cabinet** | Laboratories, paint stores | NFPA 30 §9.6 |
| **Battery Room Containment** | UPS, telecom | IEEE 1188, IFC 608.7 |
| **Gas Cabinet / Purge Panel** | Semiconductor, specialty gas | NFPA 318, SEMI S2 |
| **Cryogenic Spill Containment** | LNG terminals | NFPA 59A, EN 1473 |
| **Biological Safety Cabinet (BSC)** | Pharma, microbiology | NSF 49, EN 12469 |
| **Dam Spillway & Diversion Tunnel** | Hydropower | ICOLD guidelines |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Vertical pipe `|` symbols have been deliberately replaced with `\vert` inside table rows to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units | Reference / Standard |
|---|-------------------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | $V_{\text{bund}} \geq 1.10 \cdot V_{\text{tank,max}}$ | Minimum bund capacity, no sprinklers | m³ | NFPA 30 §22.11.2 |
| 2 | $V_{\text{bund}} \geq 1.33 \cdot V_{\text{tank,max}}$ | Bund capacity with active sprinklers | m³ | NFPA 30 |
| 3 | $V_{\text{bund}} \geq V_{\text{spill}} + V_{\text{rain}} + V_{\text{firewater}}$ | Composite bund sizing | m³ | EPA SPCC, API 2510 |
| 4 | $V_{\text{rain}} = A_{\text{bund}} \cdot h_{\text{rain}}$ | Rainfall accumulation volume | m³ | Hydrology design |
| 5 | $V_{\text{firewater}} = q \cdot A_{\text{sprinkled}} \cdot t_{\text{duration}}$ | Firewater accumulation | m³ | NFPA 13 |
| 6 | $V_{\text{cyl}} = \pi \cdot r^{2} \cdot h$ | Cylindrical tank volume | m³ | — |
| 7 | $V_{\text{rect}} = L \cdot B \cdot H$ | Rectangular bund volume | m³ | — |
| 8 | $A_{\text{bund,min}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{required}}}{H_{\text{bund,eff}}}$ | Required bund floor area given wall height | m² | — |
| 9 | $P_{\text{design}} = P_{\text{MAWP}} \cdot SF$ | Pressure containment design | kPa | ASME BPVC |
| 10 | $\dot{m}_{\text{leak}} = C_{d} \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \rho \cdot \Delta P}$ | Mass flow through leak (Bernoulli/Orifice) | kg/s | Crane TP-410 |
| 11 | $t_{\text{evap}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{spill}} \cdot \rho}{k \cdot A_{\text{pool}} \cdot P_{\text{sat}}}$ | Pool evaporation time | s | Mackay–Matsugu |
| 12 | $H_{\text{freeboard}} \geq 0.150 \; \text{m}$ | Minimum freeboard | m | EPA 40 CFR 112 |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refineries and Petrochemical Plants:** Bunds protect against crude oil and gasoline spills.
- **LNG Terminals:** Bund walls (often 3 m high) plus dykes and foam systems protect against cryogenic spills.
- **Pharmaceutical and Semiconductor Facilities:** Gas cabinets with continuous nitrogen purge contain highly toxic dopant gases (AsH₃, PH₃, B₂H₆).
- **Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS):** Containment sums address thermal runaway electrolyte leakage.
- **Environmental Engineering:** Secondary containment prevents soil/groundwater contamination — a major element of ISO 14001 EMS.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Bund Wall Capacity Formula

### Statement
For a single tank of volume $V_{\text{tank}}$ stored within a rectangular bund of inner length $L$, inner width $B$, and effective wall height $H_{\text{eff}}$, show that the bund wall height must satisfy the NFPA / EPA requirement.

### Step 1 — Express Required Bund Capacity
Per NFPA 30 §22.11.2 and EPA 40 CFR 112.8, the minimum containment capacity (without sprinklers) is:

$$V_{\text{req}} = 1.10 \cdot V_{\text{tank}}$$

### Step 2 — Express Available Bund Capacity
For a rectangular bund of plan dimensions $L \times B$ with effective wall height $H_{\text{eff}}$ (after subtracting freeboard):

$$V_{\text{avail}} = L \cdot B \cdot H_{\text{eff}}$$

### Step 3 — Equate and Solve
For the bund to be adequate:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{avail}} &\geq V_{\text{req}} \\
L \cdot B \cdot H_{\text{eff}} &\geq 1.10 \cdot V_{\text{tank}} \\
H_{\text{eff}} &\geq \dfrac{1.10 \cdot V_{\text{tank}}}{L \cdot B}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Account for Freeboard
If the *gross* wall height is $H_{\text{gross}}$, then:

$$H_{\text{gross}} = H_{\text{eff}} + H_{\text{freeboard}} = H_{\text{eff}} + 0.150 \; \text{m}$$

So the final design wall height is:

$$H_{\text{gross}} \geq \dfrac{1.10 \cdot V_{\text{tank}}}{L \cdot B} + 0.150$$

### Step 5 — With Sprinklers (NFPA 30 alternative)
If sprinklers protect the area, the factor rises from 1.10 to 1.33:

$$H_{\text{gross,sprinklered}} \geq \dfrac{1.33 \cdot V_{\text{tank}}}{L \cdot B} + 0.150$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Cylindrical Tank Volume Inside Bund

### Statement
A vertical cylindrical tank of diameter $D$ and height $H_{\text{tank}}$ is to be bunded. Show how to compute the required bund plan area.

### Step 1 — Tank Volume

$$V_{\text{tank}} = \pi \cdot \left(\dfrac{D}{2}\right)^{2} \cdot H_{\text{tank}} = \dfrac{\pi D^{2} H_{\text{tank}}}{4}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Containment Factor (1.10)

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{req}} &= 1.10 \cdot V_{\text{tank}} \\
&= \dfrac{1.10 \cdot \pi \cdot D^{2} \cdot H_{\text{tank}}}{4}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Express Bund Plan Area for Given Wall Height
Given an effective wall height $H_{\text{eff}}$:

$$A_{\text{bund}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{req}}}{H_{\text{eff}}} = \dfrac{1.10 \cdot \pi \cdot D^{2} \cdot H_{\text{tank}}}{4 \cdot H_{\text{eff}}}$$

### Step 4 — Add Maintenance Access and Spacing
Engineering practice adds a **1.0 m minimum radial clearance** around the tank (NFPA 30 §22.11.2.3) for firefighting access:

$$A_{\text{bund,design}} = \pi \cdot \left(\dfrac{D}{2} + 1.0\right)^{2}$$

The bund must be the *larger* of the volume-derived area and the access-derived area:

$$A_{\text{final}} = \max\left\{\dfrac{1.10 \cdot \pi \cdot D^{2} \cdot H_{\text{tank}}}{4 \cdot H_{\text{eff}}} \;,\; \pi \cdot \left(\dfrac{D}{2} + 1.0\right)^{2}\right\}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example (Numerically Evaluated) — KTU 2024 Pattern

### Problem
A vertical cylindrical gasoline storage tank has diameter $D = 6 \; \text{m}$ and shell height $H_{\text{tank}} = 8 \; \text{m}$. The bund wall effective height is $H_{\text{eff}} = 1.5 \; \text{m}$. The area has sprinkler protection. Calculate:
1. Tank volume
2. Required bund capacity
3. Required bund plan area (from volume)
4. Access-required bund plan area
5. Final design bund diameter and verify freeboard.

### Step 1 — Tank Volume

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{tank}} &= \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^{2} \cdot H_{\text{tank}}}{4} \\
&= \dfrac{\pi \cdot (6)^{2} \cdot 8}{4} \\
&= \dfrac{\pi \cdot 36 \cdot 8}{4} \\
&= \dfrac{904.78}{4} \\
&= 226.19 \; \text{m}^{3}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Required Bund Capacity (with sprinklers, factor = 1.33)

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{req}} &= 1.33 \cdot V_{\text{tank}} \\
&= 1.33 \cdot 226.19 \\
&= 300.84 \; \text{m}^{3}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Required Bund Plan Area (from volume)

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{bund,vol}} &= \dfrac{V_{\text{req}}}{H_{\text{eff}}} \\
&= \dfrac{300.84}{1.5} \\
&= 200.56 \; \text{m}^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Access-Required Bund Plan Area

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{bund,access}} &= \pi \cdot \left(\dfrac{D}{2} + 1.0\right)^{2} \\
&= \pi \cdot (3 + 1)^{2} \\
&= \pi \cdot 16 \\
&= 50.27 \; \text{m}^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Final Bund Diameter

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{final}} &= \max(200.56,\; 50.27) = 200.56 \; \text{m}^{2} \\
d_{\text{bund}} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot A_{\text{final}}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot 200.56}{\pi}} = \sqrt{255.40} \approx 15.98 \; \text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

So a circular bund of diameter **≈ 16 m** is required, which also satisfies the 1 m radial clearance access criterion.

### Step 6 — Freeboard Check

$$H_{\text{gross}} = H_{\text{eff}} + 0.150 = 1.5 + 0.15 = 1.65 \; \text{m}$$

This exceeds the minimum 150 mm freeboard requirement. ✅

## 3.4 Derivation of Leak Mass Flow Rate Through an Orifice (Bernoulli Basis)

This formula is often used to estimate how rapidly a tank may lose containment and hence how *large* the secondary containment must be.

### Statement
Derive the mass flow rate of an incompressible fluid leaking through an orifice of area $A$ under hydrostatic pressure head $h$.

### Step 1 — Bernoulli Between Free Surface (1) and Orifice (2)

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{atm}} + \rho g h + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho v_{1}^{2} &= P_{\text{atm}} + 0 + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho v_{2}^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

Assuming $v_{1} \approx 0$ (large tank, slow draining):

$$\rho g h = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho v_{2}^{2}$$

### Step 2 — Solve for Theoretical Velocity

$$v_{2,\text{th}} = \sqrt{2 g h}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Discharge Coefficient $C_{d}$ (accounts for vena contracta)
For sharp-edged orifices: $C_{d} \approx 0.61$; for rounded: $C_{d} \approx 0.97$.

$$v_{2} = C_{d} \cdot \sqrt{2 g h}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Volumetric and Mass Flow Rates

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= A \cdot v_{2} = C_{d} \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 g h} \\
\dot{m} &= \rho \cdot Q = C_{d} \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho^{2} g h} = C_{d} \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho g h \cdot \rho}
\end{aligned}$$

Consolidated form:

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{\text{leak}} = C_{d} \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \cdot \Delta P}}$$

where $\Delta P = \rho g h$ is the gauge pressure at the orifice.

### Step 5 — Engineering Application
Used in API 581 / ISO 17776 for **"Loss of Containment"** consequence analysis — the calculated leak rate feeds downstream pool evaporation, dispersion, fire, or explosion models.

## 3.5 Pool Evaporation Rate (Mackay-Style)

For an unignited liquid pool within a bund:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{evap}} = \dfrac{k \cdot A_{\text{pool}} \cdot P_{\text{sat}}(T)}{R \cdot T}$$

Where:
- $k$ = mass-transfer coefficient (~ 0.001 m/s typical)
- $A_{\text{pool}}$ = pool area (m²)
- $P_{\text{sat}}(T)$ = vapor pressure at pool surface temperature (Pa)
- $R$ = universal gas constant (8.314 J/mol·K)
- $T$ = pool temperature (K)

The pool area is bounded by the bund geometry; this is precisely why **larger bunds slow evaporation** by limiting pool spreading.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference for KTU Problems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this decision matrix for exam-time bund-sizing problems.

| Given Information | Required Quantity | Governing Equation |
|-------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| $V_{\text{tank}}$, bund dimensions, sprinkler status | Verify adequacy | $V_{\text{bund}} \geq f \cdot V_{\text{tank}}$ |
| Tank + rainfall + firewater | Total required volume | $V_{\text{req}} = V_{\text{spill}} + V_{\text{rain}} + V_{\text{fw}}$ |
| Head, orifice area | Leak rate | $\dot{m} = C_{d} A \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$ |
| Pool area, vapor pressure, temperature | Evaporation rate | $\dot{m}_{\text{evap}} = k A P_{\text{sat}} / (RT)$ |
| Containment volume, wall height | Verify height | $H \geq V_{\text{req}} / A_{\text{bund}} + 0.15$ |

---
