# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]**
**Define the term "Containment System" as used in industrial safety. List the three tiers of containment with one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Containment System** is a set of engineered physical barriers that prevent, control, and capture the unintended release of hazardous substances from industrial processes, thereby limiting the consequences of a loss-of-containment event.

The three tiers are:

| Tier | Name | Example |
|------|------|---------|
| Primary | First barrier directly holding the fluid | Storage tank shell, pressure vessel, piping |
| Secondary | Backup if primary fails | Reinforced concrete bund wall, double-walled tank |
| Tertiary | Last-resort plant boundary | Retention pond, foam blanket, levee |

### Question A.2
**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2023, Set B]**
**State the NFPA 30 requirement for minimum bund capacity with and without sprinkler protection.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Without sprinklers:** Bund capacity must be ≥ **110%** of the volume of the largest single tank in the bunded area.
- **With sprinklers:** Bund capacity must be ≥ **133%** of the volume of the largest single tank (additional capacity accounts for accumulated sprinkler discharge and hose streams).

Freeboard (clearance between stored liquid and bund wall top) must be a minimum of **150 mm**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B.A
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various types of containment systems used in process industries, with reference to primary, secondary, and tertiary containment. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A horizontal cylindrical tank of diameter $D = 4 \; \text{m}$ and length $L_{\text{tank}} = 10 \; \text{m}$ is to be installed within a rectangular bund of inner dimensions $8 \; \text{m} \times 12 \; \text{m}$. The tank stores diesel fuel (density $850 \; \text{kg/m}^{3}$). The bund area is protected by sprinklers. Verify whether the bund wall height of $1.2 \; \text{m}$ is sufficient. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution to Question B.A

#### Part (a) — Types of Containment Systems (7 marks)

**Primary Containment (2 marks):**
The first physical barrier directly contacting the process fluid. Components include pressure vessels (ASME BPVC Sec. VIII), storage tanks (API 650, 620, 12F), piping (ASME B31.3), and valves. Material selection, corrosion allowance, weld quality, and inspection regime all contribute to primary integrity.

**Secondary Containment (3 marks):**
Active only when primary containment fails. Includes:
- **Bund walls (dikes)** — reinforced concrete, masonry, or earthen with geomembrane liner
- **Double-walled tanks** — vacuum-monitored or liquid-monitored annular space
- **Catch basins and sumps** — sloped floors with level interlocks
- **Spill pallets** — for drums and IBCs

Design rules: ≥ 110% of largest tank volume (without sprinklers) or 133% (with sprinklers); 150 mm freeboard; rainfall and firewater allowance must be added.

**Tertiary Containment (2 marks):**
Last-resort barrier at the plant boundary. Includes retention ponds sized for the Maximum Credible Accident, levees and earthen dykes for waterway protection, foam blankets (AFFF / FFFP) for vapor suppression, and portable emergency booms/dams for drain and trench blocking.

#### Part (b) — Bund Wall Verification (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute Tank Volume**

$$V_{\text{tank}} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^{2} \cdot L_{\text{tank}}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 4^{2} \cdot 10}{4} = 40 \pi \approx 125.66 \; \text{m}^{3}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Available Bund Volume at H = 1.2 m**

$$V_{\text{avail}} = 8 \times 12 \times 1.2 = 115.2 \; \text{m}^{3}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Required Bund Capacity (with sprinklers, factor = 1.33)**

$$V_{\text{req}} = 1.33 \cdot 125.66 = 167.13 \; \text{m}^{3}$$

**Step 4 — Compare and Verify**

$$V_{\text{avail}} = 115.2 \; \text{m}^{3} \;\; < \;\; V_{\text{req}} = 167.13 \; \text{m}^{3}$$

The existing bund is **NOT sufficient**. The bund is short by:

$$\Delta V = 167.13 - 115.2 = 51.93 \; \text{m}^{3}$$

**Step 5 — Required Bund Wall Height**

For the available plan area of $8 \times 12 = 96 \; \text{m}^{2}$:

$$H_{\text{eff,req}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{req}}}{A_{\text{bund}}} = \dfrac{167.13}{96} = 1.74 \; \text{m}$$

Adding 150 mm freeboard:

$$H_{\text{gross,req}} = 1.74 + 0.15 = 1.89 \; \text{m}$$

**Conclusion:** The bund wall must be raised from **1.2 m to approximately 1.89 m**, or alternatively, the bund plan area must be enlarged to at least **$139.3 \; \text{m}^{2}$** (e.g., $12 \; \text{m} \times 12 \; \text{m}$ or $11 \; \text{m} \times 13 \; \text{m}$) at the current 1.2 m wall height.

---

### Question B.B (Alternative Internal Choice — 14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Discuss the design considerations and materials of construction for bund walls in petrochemical tank farms. What standards govern the design? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 50 m³ diesel storage tank is to be bunded in a square bund. The bund area has sprinkler protection, and a 100-year 24-hour rainfall event of 200 mm must be accommodated. Calculate the minimum required bund plan area if the effective wall height is 1.8 m. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution to Question B.B

#### Part (a) — Bund Wall Design Considerations (7 marks)

**Capacity (2 marks):**
Minimum 110% of largest tank (no sprinklers) or 133% (with sprinklers), per NFPA 30. Add rainfall and firewater contributions.

**Material of Construction (2 marks):**
- Reinforced concrete with sulfate-resistant cement (most durable; ≥ 150 mm thickness)
- Masonry with chemical-resistant mortar
- Earthen embankment with HDPE/LLDPE geomembrane liner (for large farms)
- Steel plate (for offshore modules)
- Impermeable chemical-resistant floor coating (epoxy or polyurethane)

**Design Considerations (2 marks):**
- Freeboard ≥ 150 mm
- 1 m radial clearance for firefighting access (NFPA 30)
- Sump with level sensors and interlocks
- Bund floor sloped 1–2% toward drain
- Vents with flame arresters on bund wall
- Chemical compatibility of materials with stored product
- Slope stability (for earthen bunds)
- Seismic design per IS 1893 / IBC

**Governing Standards (1 mark):**
NFPA 30 (Flammable and Combustible Liquids Code), EPA 40 CFR 112 (SPCC), API 2510 (Design and Construction of LPG Installations), IS 3786, OISD 118.

#### Part (b) — Minimum Bund Plan Area (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $V_{\text{tank}} = 50 \; \text{m}^{3}$
- Sprinkler factor = 1.33
- Rainfall depth $h_{\text{rain}} = 200 \; \text{mm} = 0.2 \; \text{m}$
- Effective wall height $H_{\text{eff}} = 1.8 \; \text{m}$
- Square bund plan

**Step 1 — Bund Volume Required for Spill (with sprinklers)**

$$V_{\text{spill}} = 1.33 \cdot 50 = 66.5 \; \text{m}^{3}$$

**Step 2 — Express Total Required Volume in Terms of Bund Area**

Let $A$ be the bund floor area (m²).

$$V_{\text{rainfall}} = A \cdot 0.2$$

**Step 3 — Total Required Bund Capacity**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{total,req}} &= V_{\text{spill}} + V_{\text{rainfall}} \\
&= 66.5 + 0.2 \cdot A
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Available Capacity**

$$V_{\text{avail}} = A \cdot 1.8$$

**Step 5 — Equate and Solve**

$$\begin{aligned}
1.8 \cdot A &\geq 66.5 + 0.2 \cdot A \\
1.8 A - 0.2 A &\geq 66.5 \\
1.6 A &\geq 66.5 \\
A &\geq \dfrac{66.5}{1.6} \\
A &\geq 41.56 \; \text{m}^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Square Bund Side Length**

$$L_{\text{side}} = \sqrt{41.56} \approx 6.45 \; \text{m}$$

Adopt a standard size of **$6.5 \; \text{m} \times 6.5 \; \text{m}$** (Area = 42.25 m²), which satisfies both capacity and freeboard requirements.

**Verification:**
$V_{\text{avail}} = 42.25 \times 1.8 = 76.05 \; \text{m}^{3}$
$V_{\text{required}} = 66.5 + 0.2 \times 42.25 = 66.5 + 8.45 = 74.95 \; \text{m}^{3}$
Since $76.05 > 74.95$, the bund is adequate. ✅

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Containment Questions — Read Carefully:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the sprinkler factor distinction** (1.10 vs 1.33). Examiners explicitly test this. *If the problem says "sprinkler-protected," use 1.33; otherwise use 1.10.*
> 2. **Omitting freeboard.** Always add **150 mm** to your calculated effective wall height before reporting the final wall height. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks here.
> 3. **Missing rainfall and firewater contributions.** Bund sizing must sum spill + rainfall + firewater unless the problem explicitly excludes them.
> 4. **Confusing containment with suppression.** Containment = passive physical barrier. Suppression = active extinguishing. They are NOT synonyms.
> 5. **Not citing the governing standard.** Always quote the relevant code (NFPA 30, EPA 40 CFR 112, API 2510, IS 3786, OISD 118) when answering design questions.
> 6. **Unit inconsistency.** Stick to SI units throughout. Mixing gallons and m³ in the same derivation is a guaranteed mark loss.
> 7. **Skipping the verification step.** Final answer must end with a clear "Adequate" / "Inadequate" verdict statement — examiners award a mark specifically for this conclusion.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Containment Systems:**
>
> ✅ Containment is a **defense-in-depth** strategy of three tiers: **Primary → Secondary → Tertiary**
>
> ✅ Primary containment = vessel shell, tank, piping (designed per ASME / API / IS standards)
>
> ✅ Secondary containment = bund wall, dike, double-walled tank, catch basin, spill pallet
>
> ✅ Tertiary containment = plant boundary retention pond, levee, foam blanket, portable dam
>
> ✅ **110% rule** (no sprinklers) and **133% rule** (with sprinklers) per NFPA 30
>
> ✅ **150 mm minimum freeboard** at top of bund wall
>
> ✅ Bund capacity formula: $V_{\text{bund}} = V_{\text{spill}} + V_{\text{rainfall}} + V_{\text{firewater}}$
>
> ✅ **1 m radial access clearance** around tank within bund (NFPA 30)
>
> ✅ Leak rate through orifice: $\dot{m} = C_{d} \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \cdot \Delta P}$
>
> ✅ Pool evaporation rate: $\dot{m}_{\text{evap}} = k \cdot A_{\text{pool}} \cdot P_{\text{sat}} / (R T)$
>
> ✅ Bund floor must slope **1–2%** toward drain and sump
>
> ✅ Vent openings on bund wall must be equipped with **flame arresters**
>
> ✅ Double-walled tanks are essential for **highly toxic** and **petroleum retail** storage (UL 1316)
>
> ✅ Governing standards: **NFPA 30**, **EPA 40 CFR 112**, **API 2510**, **OISD 118**, **IS 3786**
>
> ✅ AFFF and FFFP foams provide *simultaneous* spill containment and vapor suppression
>
> ✅ Containment ≠ Suppression — physical barrier vs active extinguishing
>
> ✅ **Buncefield (UK, 2005)** and **Jaipur (India, 2009)** are classic KTU case studies showing consequences of inadequate secondary containment
>
> ✅ For KTU 14-mark problems: always end with a **clear verdict** ("Adequate" / "Not Adequate / Raise wall to X m")

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scrubbers and Flares
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT743 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Scrubbers and Flares

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Industrial Scrubbers — The "Gas Washing Machine"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **scrubber** (also called a *wet scrubber* or *gas scrubbing system*) is an air pollution control device that uses a liquid (typically water or a chemical reagent solution) to **remove particulate matter and/or gaseous pollutants** from an industrial exhaust gas stream by intimate contact between the liquid droplets and the contaminated gas.

**Intuitive Analogy (For a KTU Fresher):**
Imagine you are chopping onions at home and your eyes start watering. To clear the air, you take a spray bottle of water and mist the room. The water droplets *capture* the irritating gas molecules and fall to the floor, leaving the air clean. That is exactly what a scrubber does, but on an industrial scale with high-pressure pumps, carefully designed nozzles, and chemistry instead of just water. In a chemical plant, the "onion" might be a toxic gas like SO₂, HCl, NH₃, or a dust cloud from a reactor.

### 1.2 Industrial Flares — The "Controlled Burn-Off Chimney"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **flare system** is a critical safety and environmental protection device used in refineries, petrochemical plants, and offshore platforms to **safely combust and dispose of waste, excess, or emergency relief hydrocarbon gases** that cannot be recovered or recycled. It converts flammable vapors into CO₂ and H₂O through controlled combustion.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Think of a pressure cooker on a gas stove. If too much steam builds up, the safety valve releases it — but in a chemical plant, releasing raw hydrocarbon gas directly to the atmosphere is illegal and dangerous. Instead, a **flare is like a giant controlled Bunsen burner** at the top of a tall stack that safely burns off the excess gas with a continuous pilot flame, much like how a gas stove uses a small pilot to ignite the main burner.

### 1.3 Why Both Are Critical Safety Devices in KTU Syllabus

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Safety Metrics (Industrial Standards):**
> - **Collection Efficiency of a Scrubber:** typically **95 % to 99.9 %** for well-designed units.
> - **Net Heating Value for Smokeless Flaring:** must exceed **11.2 MJ/Nm³ (≈ 300 BTU/scf)**.
> - **Pilot Reliability:** A flare pilot must remain lit **99.9 %** of the time (per API 537 / API 521 standards).

These systems sit at the very end of a process line — they are the **last line of defense** against atmospheric pollution, fires, and explosions. For this reason, Module 4 of PEEIT743 treats them as mandatory safety instruments requiring dedicated instrumentation, monitoring, and ESD (Emergency Shutdown) interlocks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle of a Wet Scrubber

The scrubbing action relies on three simultaneous physical-chemical phenomena:

1. **Particle Inertial Impaction** — Large dust particles cannot follow the gas streamlines around liquid droplets and strike the droplet surface.
2. **Diffusion (Brownian)** — Sub-micron particles randomly wander and contact droplets.
3. **Gas Absorption / Diffusion** — Soluble gases (SO₂, HCl, NH₃) dissolve into the liquid reagent following Henry's Law.

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating Sequence (Step-wise Logic):**
> 1. **Dirty flue gas** enters the scrubber inlet.
> 2. **Liquor** (scrubbing liquid) is pumped from a recirculation tank through a **set of spray nozzles**, atomizing it into fine droplets.
> 3. **Counter-current contact** (gas up, liquid down) maximizes residence time.
> 4. **Pollutants transfer** from gas to liquid phase.
> 5. **Clean gas** exits the top via a mist eliminator (demister pad).
> 6. **Spent liquor** falls to the sump, where it is either neutralized and discharged or recycled after make-up.

### 2.2 Classification of Scrubbers (KTU-Frequently Tested)

| Type | Pollutant Removed | Efficiency | Pressure Drop |
|------|-------------------|------------|---------------|
| **Spray Tower** | Gases, large particles | Low–Medium (50–70 %) | Very Low (1–2 in. w.c.) |
| **Packed Bed Scrubber** | Gases, fine mists | High (90–98 %) | Moderate (2–6 in. w.c.) |
| **Venturi Scrubber** | Fine particulates | Very High (99 %+) | High (10–60 in. w.c.) |
| **Cyclonic Scrubber** | Coarse dust | Medium (80–90 %) | Low–Moderate |
| **Plate / Sieve Tray** | Gases with reaction | High | Moderate |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and appear in KTU university papers. Memorize units carefully.

| # | Formula | Description / Variable Definition |
|---|---------|-----------------------------------|
| 1 | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}$ | **Collection Efficiency** (fractional); $C$ = outlet/inlet concentration |
| 2 | $P_t = 1 - \eta$ | **Penetration** (fraction of pollutant that escapes) |
| 3 | $y = m \cdot x$ | **Henry's Law** for dilute absorption; $m = \dfrac{H}{P_{total}}$ |
| 4 | $N_{A} = k_G \cdot (y - y_i)$ | **Gas-side mass transfer**; $k_G$ = gas-phase mass transfer coefficient |
| 5 | $N_{A} = k_L \cdot (x_i - x)$ | **Liquid-side mass transfer**; $k_L$ = liquid-phase coefficient |
| 6 | $\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}$ | **Overall gas-phase resistance** (Two-Film Theory) |
| 7 | $\dfrac{y_{N+1} - y^{*}}{y_1 - y^{*}} = \dfrac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$ | **Kremser-Brown-Sherwood equation** for gas absorption |
| 8 | $A = \dfrac{L}{m \cdot G}$ | **Absorption Factor** (L = liquid flow, G = gas flow, m = slope) |
| 9 | $\Delta P_{Venturi} = \rho_g \cdot v_t^{2} \left( 1 - x^{2} \right) / \left( 1 + x \right)^{2}$ | **Venturi scrubber pressure drop** (Calvert model) |
| 10 | $\dot{Q}_{flare} = \dot{m} \cdot CV$ | **Heat release rate** of flare in kW; $CV$ = calorific value (kJ/kg) |
| 11 | $L_f = K \cdot \dot{Q}^{2/3}$ | **Flame length correlation**; K depends on wind, gas composition |
| 12 | $q_{rad} = \dfrac{\varepsilon \cdot \dot{Q}}{4 \pi R^{2}}$ | **Radiative heat flux** at distance R from flare |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Trap:** Many students confuse the symbols $L$ and $G$ — remember: **L = Liquid molar flow (kmol/h)** and **G = Gas molar flow (kmol/h)**. Mixing these up will invert your answer.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refineries** use packed-bed scrubbers to remove H₂S from Claus plant tail gas.
- **Semiconductor fabs** use **Venturi scrubbers** to capture acids (HF, HBr) from etching tool exhaust.
- **Offshore platforms** use **enclosed ground flares** to protect the deck crew from thermal radiation while disposing of well-test gas.
- **Power plants** use **Flue-Gas Desulfurization (FGD) scrubbers** to remove SO₂ from coal-fired boilers — these are the largest wet scrubbers in the world.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Material Balance of Collection Efficiency

Consider a scrubber with a contaminated gas stream of mass flow $\dot{m}_g$ and pollutant inlet/outlet concentrations $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ (in g/m³).

**Step 1 — Pollutant mass entering per second:**
$$\dot{m}_{p,in} = \dot{m}_g \cdot C_{in}$$

**Step 2 — Pollutant mass leaving per second:**
$$\dot{m}_{p,out} = \dot{m}_g \cdot C_{out}$$

**Step 3 — Mass captured by the liquid phase per second:**
$$\dot{m}_{p,cap} = \dot{m}_g \left( C_{in} - C_{out} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Define Collection Efficiency as the ratio of captured pollutant to inlet pollutant:**
$$\eta = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{p,cap}}{\dot{m}_{p,in}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_g \left( C_{in} - C_{out} \right)}{\dot{m}_g \cdot C_{in}}$$

**Step 5 — Cancel the gas mass flow term:**
$$\eta = 1 - \dfrac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}$$

This is the **fundamental definition** of fractional collection efficiency, valid for any gas cleaning device — not just scrubbers.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Pressure Drop in a Venturi Scrubber (Simplified Calvert Form)

The Venturi scrubber is the most efficient particulate scrubber used in industry. Its pressure drop determines the energy cost.

**Step 1 — Define gas velocity at the throat:**
$$v_t = \dfrac{\dot{V}_g}{A_t}$$
where $\dot{V}_g$ = gas volumetric flow at throat conditions, $A_t$ = throat area.

**Step 2 — Define the liquid-to-gas mass ratio:**
$$x = \dfrac{\dot{m}_L}{\dot{m}_g}$$
This is a dimensionless parameter.

**Step 3 — The Calvert model correlates throat pressure drop to gas density and the liquid loading:**
$$\Delta P = \dfrac{\rho_g \cdot v_t^{2}}{2} \cdot \left( 1 - x^{2} \right)$$

**Step 4 — For typical industrial Venturi scrubbers where $x \ll 1$, simplify by neglecting $x^2$:**
$$\Delta P \approx \dfrac{\rho_g \cdot v_t^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Step 1 into Step 4:**
$$\Delta P \approx \dfrac{\rho_g}{2} \cdot \left( \dfrac{\dot{V}_g}{A_t} \right)^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to convenient engineering units (inches of water column):**
$$\Delta P_{(in.w.c.)} = \dfrac{\Delta P_{(Pa)}}{249.1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Since $\Delta P \propto v_t^{2}$, doubling the throat velocity quadruples the energy cost. This is the classic **"efficiency-vs-energy" trade-off** that designers must balance in Module 4 problems.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Two-Film Mass Transfer for a Packed Scrubber

For a single solute diffusing from gas to liquid through two stagnant films:

**Step 1 — Flux through gas film (per unit area):**
$$N_A = k_G \cdot (y - y_i)$$
where $y$ = bulk gas mole fraction, $y_i$ = interface mole fraction.

**Step 2 — Flux through liquid film (equal in steady state):**
$$N_A = k_L \cdot (x_i - x)$$
where $x$ = bulk liquid mole fraction, $x_i$ = interface mole fraction.

**Step 3 — Equilibrium at interface (Henry's Law):**
$$y_i = m \cdot x_i$$

**Step 4 — Express overall flux in terms of bulk concentrations using overall coefficient $K_G$:**
$$N_A = K_G \cdot (y - y^{*})$$
where $y^{*} = m \cdot x$ is the gas mole fraction in equilibrium with bulk liquid.

**Step 5 — Add the individual resistances (resistances in series):**
$$\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}$$

This is the **Two-Film Theory resistance model** — frequently asked as a 7-mark derivation in KTU ESE.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of an Industrial Wet Scrubber System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DIRTY GAS INLET\nReactor or Furnace] --> B[INLET DUCT\nWith Pre-Cooler]
    B --> C[SCRUBBER TOWER\nPacked Bed or Venturi]
    D[CHEMICAL REAGENT TANK\nNaOH or Limestone Slurry] --> E[RECIRCULATION PUMP]
    E --> F[NOZZLE HEADER\nSpray Nozzles]
    F --> C
    C --> G[MIST ELIMINATOR\nDemister Pad]
    G --> H[CLEAN GAS STACK\nTo Atmosphere]
    C --> I[SUMP TANK\nSpent Liquor]
    I --> J[NEUTRALIZATION UNIT\npH Control]
    J --> K[EFFLUENT TREATMENT\nOr Recycle]
    I --> E
    L[INSTRUMENTATION LAYER] -.-> M[PT - Differential Pressure\nTransmitter]
    L -.-> N[FT - Flow Transmitter\non Reagent Line]
    L -.-> O[AT - pH Analyzer\nin Sump]
    L -.-> P[FT - Liquid Flow\nto Spray Nozzles]
    L -.-> Q[TT - Temperature\nfor Inlet Gas]
    M --> C
    N --> E
    O --> I
    P --> F
    Q --> B
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram of a Complete Flare System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[PROCESS VESSELS\nRelief Valves\nVapors] --> S[HEADER MANIFOLD\nCollecting Piping]
    S --> T[KNOCKOUT DRUM\nRemoves Liquid Entrainment]
    T --> U[WATER SEAL DRUM\nPrevents Flashback]
    U --> V[FLARE STACK\nVertical Riser]
    V --> W[FLARE TIP\nBurner Assembly]
    X[FLASHBACK ARRESTOR\nLocated Above Seal] --> U
    Y[PILOT BURNER\nContinuous Ignition Source] --> W
    Z[IGNITION PILOT\nGas Supply System] --> Y
    AA[STEAM / AIR ASSIST\nFor Smokeless Combustion] --> W
    AB[REMOTE IGNITION\nSpark or Hot Surface] --> Y
    AC[INSTRUMENTATION LAYER] -.-> AD[UV / IR FLAME DETECTOR\nPilot Monitoring]
    AC -.-> AE[FLOW TRANSMITTER\non Header S]
    AC -.-> AF[PRESSURE TRANSMITTER\non Knockout Drum]
    AC -.-> AG[TEMPERATURE TRANSMITTER\non Stack V]
    AC -.-> AH[COMBUSTIBLE GAS DETECTOR\nat Pilot]
    AD --> Y
    AE --> S
    AF --> T
    AG --> V
    AH --> Y
    AD --> AI[ESD LOGIC SOLVER\nSafety PLC]
    AE --> AI
    AF --> AI
    AH --> AI
    AI --> AJ[EMERGENCY SHUTDOWN VALVE\non Header S]
```

### 4.3 Comparison Matrix: Scrubber vs. Flare

| Functional Attribute | Scrubber | Flare |
|----------------------|----------|-------|
| **Primary Purpose** | Removes pollutants from exhaust | Disposes of waste gases safely |
| **Phase Action** | Gas-to-Liquid mass transfer | Gas-to-Heat (combustion) |
| **Operating Mode** | Continuous, steady-state | Continuous, Emergency, or Routine |
| **End Products** | Clean gas + Spent liquor | CO₂ + H₂O + Heat + Noise |
| **Key Hazard Mitigated** | Atmospheric pollution, acid mists | Fire, explosion, toxic release |
| **Critical Sensor** | pH analyzer, DP transmitter | UV/IR flame detector |
| **Failure Consequence** | Stack opacity violation, spill | Pilot failure → unburnt gas release |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define the term "collection efficiency" of a scrubber. State its mathematical expression and mention any two factors that affect it.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Collection efficiency is the ratio of the mass of pollutant captured by the scrubbing liquid to the mass of pollutant entering the scrubber with the gas stream.
- **Formula (1 mark):** $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}$ where $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ are inlet and outlet concentrations of pollutant in gas.
- **Two factors (1 mark):** (i) Liquid-to-gas (L/G) flow ratio, (ii) Droplet size from spray nozzles, (iii) Solubility of the pollutant, (iv) Contact/residence time in the tower.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*What is the function of a pilot burner in a flare system? Why is continuous pilot monitoring important?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Function of Pilot (1 mark):** The pilot burner is a small, continuously burning flame at the flare tip that ignites the main gas stream whenever relief gas is released, ensuring immediate combustion.
- **Importance of Monitoring (1 mark):** If the pilot goes out, unburnt hydrocarbon gas will escape to atmosphere, creating a fire/explosion hazard and an environmental disaster (Bhopal 1984 and similar incidents).
- **Monitoring method (1 mark):** A UV (ultraviolet) or IR (infrared) flame detector continuously senses pilot flame presence and sends a signal to the DCS/ESD system, which will trigger a re-ignition attempt or sound an alarm.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — INTERNAL CHOICE

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

*(a) [7 Marks] Explain the working principle of an industrial packed-bed scrubber with a neat sketch. Discuss the absorption process using the Two-Film Theory and derive the overall gas-phase mass transfer coefficient relationship.*

*(b) [7 Marks) Describe the various instrumentation systems used in a scrubber unit for safe and efficient operation. Explain how differential pressure and pH transmitters are integrated with the control loop.*

---

**Model Answer to Question A:**

**(a) Working Principle & Two-Film Theory (7 Marks):**

- **Working Principle (3 Marks):** A packed-bed scrubber consists of a vertical cylindrical column filled with **packing material** (e.g., Pall rings, Raschig rings, Intalox saddles). The dirty gas enters from the bottom and flows upward (up-flow), while the scrubbing liquid is sprayed from the top and flows downward by gravity (counter-current contact). The large surface area of the packing enables intimate gas-liquid contact. Pollutants transfer to the liquid phase, and clean gas exits the top through a demister. (Award 3 marks for a labelled sketch + working description.)

- **Two-Film Theory (2 Marks):** According to this theory, two stagnant films — one of gas and one of liquid — exist at the gas-liquid interface. Mass transfer is by steady-state molecular diffusion through these films.

- **Derivation (2 Marks):** Starting from $N_A = k_G (y - y_i)$ and $N_A = k_L (x_i - x)$, with interface equilibrium $y_i = m x_i$, the overall flux is $N_A = K_G (y - y^{*})$, where:
$$\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}$$
This shows that gas-phase and liquid-phase resistances add up in series.

---

**(b) Instrumentation Systems (7 Marks):**

- **Differential Pressure (DP) Transmitter (3 Marks):** Connected across the packed bed to measure pressure drop. A high DP indicates flooding or choking of the column; a low DP indicates broken packing or a bypass. The DP signal goes to a controller that adjusts the **liquid flow** via a control valve. Range typically 0–100 inches water column.

- **pH Analyzer (2 Marks):** Installed in the sump to monitor acidity of the spent liquor. If SO₂ absorption drops the pH below a setpoint (e.g., 6.0), the controller commands more NaOH reagent pump flow.

- **Other Instruments (2 Marks):** Flow transmitters on the liquid line (FT), temperature transmitters on inlet gas (TT), level transmitter (LT) in the sump, and conductivity meter for reagent concentration.

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

*(a) [7 Marks] Describe the major components of an industrial flare system and explain the function of each. With the help of a block diagram, illustrate the gas flow path from the relief valve to the flare tip.*

*(b) [7 Marks] Discuss the instrumentation and safety interlocks for a flare system. Explain how pilot flame monitoring is achieved and what actions are taken on pilot failure.*

---

**Model Answer to Question B:**

**(a) Major Components of Flare System (7 Marks):**

1. **Header / Manifold (1 Mark):** Collects relief gases from all process units.
2. **Knockout Drum (1 Mark):** Removes entrained liquid droplets using baffles; protects the flare tip from burning liquid carryover.
3. **Water Seal Drum (1 Mark):** Creates a liquid barrier that prevents flashback of flame down the stack.
4. **Flashback Arrestor (1 Mark):** A flame arrestor element (perforated plates or sintered metal) that quenches any flame trying to travel back into the process.
5. **Flare Stack and Tip (1 Mark):** Vertical chimney with a special burner tip that provides stable combustion across a wide flow range.
6. **Pilot Burner (1 Mark):** Continuous small flame at the tip that ignites the main gas.
7. **Steam/Air Assist (1 Mark):** Injects steam or air into the flame zone to promote smokeless combustion when hydrocarbons are heavy.

---

**(b) Instrumentation & Safety Interlocks (7 Marks):**

- **Pilot Flame Detection (2 Marks):** A UV flame detector mounted near the pilot senses ultraviolet radiation emitted by the flame. The detector produces a 4–20 mA signal; if flame is lost, the signal drops to 0 mA.

- **Action on Pilot Failure (2 Marks):** The ESD/SIS logic solver receives the flame-fail signal and immediately (a) sounds an alarm, (b) attempts re-ignition via a spark igniter, (c) if re-ignition fails within a defined time (e.g., 10 s), it isolates the flare header using an ESDV (Emergency Shutdown Valve) to prevent accumulation of unburnt gas.

- **Other Safety Loops (3 Marks):**
  - **Header flow transmitter** + **pressure transmitter** confirm gas is being received; if both zero → flare is healthy.
  - **Combustible gas detector** at pilot area provides redundant confirmation.
  - **Knockout drum level transmitter** triggers high-level alarm and shutdown of the upstream unit if the drum fills.
  - **Thermal radiation monitors** around the flare fence alarm on exceeding safe heat flux (typically **6.31 kW/m²** for personnel access areas).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Do **not** confuse **Penetration ($P_t = 1 - \eta$)** with **Efficiency ($\eta$)**. Wrong sign costs 1 mark.
> - In a Venturi scrubber, the **throat is the narrowest section** — high velocity, high $\Delta P$, highest efficiency. Do not confuse throat with diffuser.
> - For flares, **smokeless operation** needs the **net heating value** to exceed **300 BTU/scf (≈ 11.2 MJ/Nm³)**, not the gross heating value.
> - The **pilot must be monitored continuously** — KTU specifically tests this. Always draw a feedback loop from flame detector to ESD.
> - Never write **"$|x|$"** in a table — use **$\vert x \vert$** or **$\mid x \mid$** to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Recall Points (Memorize Before Exam):**
> - [✓] **Scrubber** = Gas cleaning by **liquid contact**; **Flare** = Gas disposal by **combustion**.
> - [✓] Collection efficiency formula: $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}$
> - [✓] Venturi $\Delta P \propto v_t^{2}$ (throat velocity squared).
> - [✓] Two-Film Theory gives $\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}$.
> - [✓] Henry's Law: $y = m \cdot x$; $m = H / P_{total}$.
> - [✓] Kremser equation: $\dfrac{y_{N+1} - y^{*}}{y_1 - y^{*}} = \dfrac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$, where $A = \dfrac{L}{mG}$.
> - [✓] **Flare components sequence:** Header → Knockout Drum → Water Seal → Flashback Arrestor → Flare Tip → Pilot.
> - [✓] **Pilot monitoring** uses **UV (preferred) or IR flame detector**; on loss → re-ignite → if fails → close ESDV.
> - [✓] **Smokeless flare** requires NHV > **11.2 MJ/Nm³**; achieved using **steam-assisted** or **air-assisted** burners.
> - [✓] **Thermal radiation limit** at personnel boundary: **6.31 kW/m²** (per API 521).
> - [✓] Flame length scales as $\dot{Q}^{2/3}$.
> - [✓] Master the difference: **Packed scrubber** = gas absorption; **Venturi scrubber** = particulate removal; **Cyclonic scrubber** = coarse dust; **Spray tower** = large gas volumes, low efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire and Gas (F&G) Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT743 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY AND INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fire and Gas (F&G) Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fire and Gas (F&G) System** is a dedicated, integrated, safety-instrumented detection and mitigation network deployed in high-hazard process industries (oil & gas, petrochemical, pharmaceutical, LNG terminals, power plants) to continuously monitor the plant environment for the earliest signatures of **combustion events (fire)** and **toxic/flammable vapor release (gas)**, and to initiate a pre-engineered, layered emergency response comprising alarms, equipment shutdowns, process isolation, and activation of suppression or deluge systems.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme vocabulary, the F&G system is treated as a **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** under **IEC 61508** and **IEC 61511**, sitting alongside the **Emergency Shutdown (ESD)** and **Process Shutdown (PSD)** layers in the **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** architecture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** F&G systems are *event-mitigation* systems, NOT process-control systems. Their sole mandate is to detect hazardous releases and reduce the consequence to personnel, asset, and environment to an ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) level.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your home fitted with two separate guardian networks:
1. A **smoke alarm network** (sensors on the ceiling, a hooter on the wall, and an automatic call to the fire brigade).
2. A **LPG leak detector** under the kitchen counter that automatically shuts the cylinder valve and opens the exhaust fan.

Now upscale this idea by 10,000 times into a refinery. There are hundreds of detectors, thousands of alarm sirens, automatic valve closures, water spray cannons, foam injection, hydrocarbon pump trips, and ventilation cut-offs — all orchestrated by a **Safety PLC (Logic Solver)** in milliseconds. **That orchestration is the F&G System.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for First-Time Learners:** A F&G system is essentially a *brain + nerves + muscles* of a plant. The detectors are nerves, the safety PLC is the brain, and the sirens/dampers/deluge valves are the muscles.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Performance Boundaries

| Parameter | Standard Value / Symbol | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Time-to-Detect** | typically **< 30 s** for fire, **< 60 s** for gas | Window for early warning before escalation |
| **Detector Coverage Radius** | per **NFPA 72** spacing rules | Maximum footprint one detector guards |
| **Alarm Setpoint (LEL)** | **10% LEL** (alarm), **20% LEL** (trip), **60% LEL** (evacuation) | Expressed as percentage of **Lower Explosive Limit** |
| **Response Time of Logic Solver** | **< 100 ms** (typical) | Determines the SIF's safety integrity |
| **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | **> 100,000 hours** for certified detectors | Reliability metric for SIL qualification |
| **Safety Integrity Level** | **SIL 1, SIL 2, or SIL 3** per **IEC 61511** | Risk-reduction target (PFD: $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-3}$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **LEL** = *Lower Explosive Limit*, the minimum vapor concentration in air that can ignite. For methane, LEL ≈ **4.4% by volume**; for gasoline vapors, LEL ≈ **1.4% by volume**.

## 1.4 Architectural Position in the Plant Safety Stack

A F&G system occupies a **distinct layer**, separate from the BPCS (Basic Process Control System). It is supervised by its own redundant Safety PLC (e.g., Honeywell Safety Manager, ABB 800xA High Integrity, Siemens AS 410 F, Yokogawa ProSafe-RS).

The hierarchy is:

$$\text{Layer 0 (Field)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Layer 1 (Control)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Layer 2 (Supervisory)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Layer 3 (Operations)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Layer 4 (Management)}$$

The F&G system is a **Layer 1 safety function**, but it bridges upward to **Layer 2 (HMI annunciation)** and **Layer 3 (Operations Management)** for alarm logging, incident investigation, and emergency response coordination.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the F&G System

A F&G system can be analytically decomposed into **four sub-systems** operating in series:

1. **Detection Sub-system** — Field sensors (fire + gas).
2. **Logic Sub-system** — Safety PLC + voting logic + diagnostics.
3. **Alarm & Annunciation Sub-system** — Sirens, beacons, HMI pop-ups, voice paging.
4. **Mitigation / Action Sub-system** — Dampers, isolation valves, deluge valves, ESD-1/ESD-0, firewater pumps, foam systems, HVAC shutoff.

Each sub-system can fail; hence the SIL calculation must consider the **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** of each sub-system summed together.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 'voting' logic matters:** A single faulty detector must not falsely shut down the plant (causing downtime) NOR must it miss a real fire/gas event. Voting logic balances **safe failure** vs **dangerous failure** using redundancy and coincidence.

## 2.2 Voting Logic — The Heart of F&G Reliability

Voting logic expresses the number of detectors that must agree to declare a "confirmed" event. The standard notations are:

| Voting Logic | Meaning | Engineering Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| **1oo1** (1 out of 1) | One detector trips alone | High availability, low safety |
| **1oo2** (1 out of 2) | Either detector can trip | Higher safety than 1oo1, but spurious trip risk remains |
| **2oo2** (2 out of 2) | Both detectors must agree | Very safe, very prone to nuisance trips |
| **2oo3** (2 out of 3) | Any two of three must agree | Balanced — most used in high-consequence zones |

The **Average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** for a redundant voted group is computed using the standard IEC 61511 formulae.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PFD of 1oo1 | $\text{PFD}_{1oo1} = \lambda_D \cdot T_i$ | dimensionless | Single detector |
| 2 | PFD of 1oo2 | $\text{PFD}_{1oo2} = \dfrac{(\lambda_D \cdot T_i)^2}{3}$ | dimensionless | Parallel redundant |
| 3 | PFD of 2oo3 | $\text{PFD}_{2oo3} = \lambda_D^2 \cdot T_i^2$ | dimensionless | Triple modular redundancy |
| 4 | LEL conversion (ppm → %LEL) | $\% \text{LEL} = \dfrac{C_{\text{ppm}}}{C_{\text{LEL, ppm}}} \times 100$ | % | Detector calibration |
| 5 | Flammability Range | $C_{\text{LEL}} \leq C \leq C_{\text{UEL}}$ | %vol | Defining explosive envelope |
| 6 | Detector Coverage Radius (heat) | $r = 7.5 \text{ m}$ (typical) | m | NFPA 72 spacing |
| 7 | Gas Detector Coverage | $A = \pi r^2$ | m² | Area protection |
| 8 | Risk Reduction Factor | $\text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PFD}}$ | — | Quantifying SIL |
| 9 | SIL from PFD | $\text{PFD} \in [10^{-1}, 10^{-2}] \Rightarrow \text{SIL 1}$, etc. | — | IEC 61511 mapping |
| 10 | Setpoint Margin | $M = \dfrac{C_{\text{set}}}{C_{\text{LEL}}}$ | dimensionless | Alarm threshold design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the table above, the **vertical bar character `\|`** is intentionally not used; for absolute values or boundaries, we use the keyword **"LIE"** (Lower Inequality Estimate) or write "from-to" in words to keep the markdown table intact.

## 2.4 Detection Technology — Selection Decision Tree

The choice of detector depends on **what is being detected** and **the plant environment**:

### Fire Detection Technologies
- **Smoke (Photoelectric / Ionization)** — best for smoldering fires, indoor switch rooms.
- **Heat (Fixed temperature / Rate-of-rise)** — best for hot outdoor process areas, ductwork.
- **Flame (IR / UV / IR+UV combined)** — best for hydrocarbon pool fires, very fast (< 10 s) response.
- **Aspirating Smoke Detection (VESDA / FAAST)** — best for high-sensitivity cabinet protection.

### Gas Detection Technologies
- **Catalytic Bead (Pellistor)** — for combustible gases (LEL range), cheap, poisonable.
- **Infrared (NDIR)** — for hydrocarbons, immune to poisoning, fast.
- **Electrochemical Cell** — for toxic gases ($\text{H}_2\text{S}$, $\text{CO}$, $\text{Cl}_2$).
- **Semiconductor (MOS)** — for refrigerant leaks, low cost.
- **Photoacoustic Infrared (PIR)** — for low-ppb VOC detection.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

F&G systems are mandatory under:
- **NFPA 72** (National Fire Alarm and Signaling Code, USA)
- **EN 50402** (Functional safety of fixed gas detection apparatus)
- **IS/IEC 60079** (Explosive atmospheres)
- **OISD-STD-144** (Oil Industry Safety Directorate, India)
- **PNGRB T4S** (Petroleum and Natural Gas Regulatory Board, India)

In India, KTU-affiliated Process Instrumentation students typically encounter F&G systems at **Kochi Refinery, BPCL Mumbai, IOCL Panipat, and Reliance Jamnagar** during industrial training.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of PFD for Voted Detector Groups (IEC 61511 Simplified Model)

We derive the PFD of common voting configurations. The model assumes:
- Identical detectors with constant dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D$ (failures per hour).
- Proof test interval $T_i$ (hours between manual safety checks).
- Perfect diagnostics (no detected-but-uncoveted failures).
- Only **dangerous undetected failures** contribute to PFD (per IEC 61511).

### Derivation 1: PFD of 1oo1 Architecture

A 1oo1 system fails dangerously if the single detector fails. The average unavailability over the interval $[0, T_i]$ is the area under the linear failure-growth curve.

**Step 1.** Probability of being failed at time $t$ (assuming linear growth from 0 at $t=0$ to $\lambda_D T_i$ at $t = T_i$):
$$P(t) \approx \lambda_D \cdot t$$

**Step 2.** Average probability over the interval:
$$\text{PFD}_{1oo1} = \frac{1}{T_i} \int_0^{T_i} \lambda_D \cdot t \, dt$$

**Step 3.** Integrate:
$$\int_0^{T_i} \lambda_D \cdot t \, dt = \lambda_D \left[ \frac{t^2}{2} \right]_0^{T_i} = \frac{\lambda_D T_i^2}{2}$$

**Step 4.** Divide by $T_i$:
$$\text{PFD}_{1oo1} = \frac{1}{T_i} \cdot \frac{\lambda_D T_i^2}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \lambda_D T_i$$

**Step 5.** Conventionally simplified to (using average unavailability approximation):
$$\boxed{\;\text{PFD}_{1oo1} \approx \lambda_D \cdot T_i\;}$$

This represents the **average probability** that, on demand, a single detector will fail to operate.

---

### Derivation 2: PFD of 1oo2 Architecture

In 1oo2, the system fails dangerously only if **both** detectors fail. The system succeeds if at least one works. We need the joint failure probability.

**Step 1.** Each detector independently has unavailability $u(t) = \lambda_D \cdot t$ (small).

**Step 2.** Joint unavailability (independent failures):
$$u_{\text{joint}}(t) = u_1(t) \cdot u_2(t) = (\lambda_D t)^2$$

**Step 3.** Average over the interval:
$$\text{PFD}_{1oo2} = \frac{1}{T_i} \int_0^{T_i} (\lambda_D t)^2 \, dt$$

**Step 4.** Integrate:
$$\int_0^{T_i} \lambda_D^2 t^2 \, dt = \lambda_D^2 \left[ \frac{t^3}{3} \right]_0^{T_i} = \frac{\lambda_D^2 T_i^3}{3}$$

**Step 5.** Divide by $T_i$:
$$\boxed{\;\text{PFD}_{1oo2} \approx \frac{(\lambda_D T_i)^2}{3}\;}$$

Compare with 1oo1: if $\lambda_D T_i = 0.01$, then
- $\text{PFD}_{1oo1} = 0.01$
- $\text{PFD}_{1oo2} = 0.0000333$

The redundancy gives a **300×** safety improvement — but **spurious trip rate also doubles**, an essential engineering trade-off.

---

### Derivation 3: PFD of 2oo3 Architecture

In 2oo3, the system fails dangerously only when **two or more** of three detectors fail. We compute the sum of all dangerous failure combinations.

**Step 1.** Unavailability of one detector: $u \approx \lambda_D T_i$ (averaged).

**Step 2.** Probability that a *specific* pair (e.g., 1 & 2) both fail while the third works: $u \cdot u \cdot (1 - u)$.

**Step 3.** Number of ways to choose 2 detectors out of 3: $\binom{3}{2} = 3$.

**Step 4.** Probability that *all three* fail: $u^3$ (negligible, can be dropped).

**Step 5.** Total PFD:
$$\text{PFD}_{2oo3} = 3 \cdot u^2 \cdot (1 - u) + u^3$$

**Step 6.** For small $u$, $(1 - u) \approx 1$ and $u^3 \approx 0$:
$$\boxed{\;\text{PFD}_{2oo3} \approx 3 (\lambda_D T_i)^2\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> 2oo3 PFD is *roughly 9×* better than 1oo2 in dangerous-failure space, but it adds cost and complexity. It is the **preferred architecture for high-consequence zones** like compressor shelters and LNG loading arms.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Exam-Favorite)

| Voting | PFD Expression | Spurious Trip Rate (STR) | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1oo1 | $\lambda_D T_i$ | $\lambda_S T_i$ (low) | Non-critical alarm only |
| 1oo2 | $\dfrac{(\lambda_D T_i)^2}{3}$ | $2 \lambda_S T_i$ | Fire detection (balanced) |
| 2oo2 | $\dfrac{(\lambda_D T_i)^2}{3}$ | $\dfrac{2 \lambda_S^2 T_i^2}{3}$ (very high) | Rarely used |
| 2oo3 | $3 (\lambda_D T_i)^2$ | $6 \lambda_S^2 T_i^2$ | High-consequence zone |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A flame detector group uses 2oo3 voting. Each detector has a dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour and the proof test interval is $T_i = 8760$ hours (1 year). Compute the PFD, the Risk Reduction Factor (RRF), and the corresponding SIL band.

**Step 1.** Compute $\lambda_D T_i$:
$$\lambda_D T_i = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 = 4.38 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 2.** Compute PFD of 2oo3:
$$\text{PFD}_{2oo3} = 3 (\lambda_D T_i)^2 = 3 \times (4.38 \times 10^{-2})^2$$
$$\text{PFD}_{2oo3} = 3 \times 1.918 \times 10^{-3} = 5.75 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3.** Compute RRF:
$$\text{RRF} = \frac{1}{\text{PFD}} = \frac{1}{5.75 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 174$$

**Step 4.** Map to SIL band (per IEC 61511):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SIL 1:}\quad & 10^{-1} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-2} \\
\text{SIL 2:}\quad & 10^{-2} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-3} \\
\text{SIL 3:}\quad & 10^{-3} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $\text{PFD} = 5.75 \times 10^{-3}$, it falls in the range $[10^{-3}, 10^{-2}]$, so the system qualifies as **SIL 2**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Exam:** Always show the PFD range to identify SIL. Most F&G detection loops in the industry target **SIL 1 or SIL 2**; SIL 3 is reserved for ESD-1 (catastrophic event protection).
